title: Poling-free Spontaneous Parametric Down Conversion without for Silicon Carbide and Lithium Niobate photonics
abstract: State-of-the-art photon sources based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) currently rely on artificial structuring of the material nonlinearity to satisfy phase-matching conditions. This technique, known as periodic poling, is available only in a limited number of material platforms and introduces additional fabrication steps and errors, which are detrimental to up-scaling efforts. Here, we present a device architecture that enables SPDC of a wide range of frequencies without the need for periodic poling. We present explicit designs and calculations for 4H Silicon Carbide on-insulator, in which SPDC photon generation is so far unavailable, and thin-film Lithium Niobate on-insulator, a state-of-the-art quantum photonics platform. Our design, based on mode conversion and subsequent modal phase-matched SPDC, facilitates a CMOS compatible $χ^{(2)}$ platform, and simplifies photon sources by removing the requirement of periodic poling and the associated additional fabrication complexity.
one_liner: A waveguide design enables SPDC in SiC and thin-film LiNbO3 without periodic poling by using mode conversion and modal phase matching.
why_it_matters: Removing periodic poling simplifies fabrication and opens scalable chi(2) photon-pair sources in CMOS-compatible and established photonic platforms.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a poling-free SPDC architecture based on mode conversion followed by modal phase matching, avoiding artificial nonlinear-domain engineering. It gives explicit device designs and calculations for 4H-SiC-on-insulator, where SPDC sources have been lacking, and for thin-film LiNbO3 on insulator. The scheme supports a broad range of frequency combinations while reducing fabrication overhead and poling-induced imperfections. This makes integrated chi(2) sources more compatible with scalable foundry-style photonics.
main_result: The authors show that modal engineering can enable efficient SPDC without periodic poling in silicon carbide and lithium niobate photonic platforms.
application_angle: This could lower the manufacturing barrier for integrated quantum light sources and expand deployable photon-pair generation to CMOS-compatible SiC photonics.

title: Toward quantum interconnects featuring nanometer-to-picometer bandwidth compression and THz-range quantum frequency conversion
abstract: The long-range transmission of quantum information relies on multiple interfaces between photons, acting as flying qubits, and localized memories, serving as repeaters, to mitigate transmission losses. Efficient, long-range transmission necessitates the use of short, picosecond-scale photons, which are markedly different from the narrowband, nanosecond-scale photons optimal for absorption by memory elements, typically operating at wavelengths far from telecom. In this article, we point toward designs capable of bridging these regimes, leveraging the interplay between sum-frequency generation-based quantum frequency conversion and resonant confinement in an integrated ring resonator.
one_liner: An integrated ring-resonator concept targets simultaneous THz-scale quantum frequency conversion and extreme photon bandwidth compression for quantum interconnects.
why_it_matters: Bridging fast broadband telecom photons and narrowband memory-compatible photons is a central bottleneck for practical quantum repeater and network hardware.
detailed_summary: The work outlines designs that combine sum-frequency-generation-based quantum frequency conversion with resonant confinement in an integrated ring resonator. The goal is to map picosecond, broadband flying-photon states to the much narrower spectral and wavelength regimes required by stationary quantum memories. The proposed devices aim at nanometer-to-picometer bandwidth compression together with THz-range frequency translation in a single photonic interface. This directly addresses the mismatch between telecom transmission channels and memory absorption lines.
main_result: The paper identifies ring-resonator quantum transducers capable in principle of combining extreme spectral compression with large-range quantum frequency conversion.
application_angle: Such interfaces are relevant for connecting telecom photonic links to quantum memories in repeater nodes and heterogeneous quantum networks.

title: Mutually-commuting von Neumann algebra models of quantum networks and violation of Bell-type inequalities
abstract: Employing mutually-commuting von Neumann algebras to represent the algebra of observables on quantum systems provides a framework for studying quantum information theory in systems with infinite degrees of freedom and quantum field theory, yielding many profound results that differ from non-relativistic quantum systems. In this paper, we establish a mutually-commuting von Neumann algebra model of quantum networks with arbitrary structures. We derive Bell-type inequalities on this model, and determine various bounds for Bell-type inequalities based on the structure of underline von Neumann algebras, and identify the algebraic structural conditions required for their violation. The conditions on the algebraic structure of observables for maximal violation of Bell-type inequalities, which we discovered in the context of von Neumann algebra models, can in turn guide the search for measurements in the non-relativistic setting.
one_liner: A von Neumann algebra framework for arbitrary quantum networks derives Bell-type bounds and structural conditions for their violation.
why_it_matters: It extends network nonlocality analysis to infinite-dimensional and field-theoretic settings, informing how observables should be structured for strong Bell violations.
detailed_summary: The authors model quantum networks with arbitrary connectivity using mutually commuting von Neumann algebras as the observable framework. Within this setting, they derive Bell-type inequalities and determine bounds that depend on the underlying algebraic structure. They further identify algebraic conditions required for violations, including conditions associated with maximal violation. Although theoretical, the framework is relevant to networked quantum systems beyond finite-dimensional nonrelativistic models.
main_result: The paper establishes Bell-type inequality bounds for arbitrary quantum-network structures in a commuting-von-Neumann-algebra model and pinpoints structural criteria for their violation.
application_angle: These results can guide the design of measurement structures for distributed entanglement and nonlocality tests in advanced quantum communication networks.

title: Clock Noise Cancellation in Heterodyne Links between Optical Cavities for Space-Borne Gravitational-Wave Telescopes
abstract: Space-borne gravitational-wave telescopes are key to extend the observation band below $10\,\mathrm{Hz}$. The use of inter-satellite optical cavities linked by heterodyne interferometry is a promising approach to reach the sensitivity level of $10^{-22}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ in the decihertz band. While heterodyne interferometry is advantageous for relaxing arm-length control requirements, it introduces susceptibility to clock jitter, which can be a significant noise source. In the back-linked Fabry--Perot (BLFP) interferometer aiming at the decihertz band, the required clock stability exceeds that of current space-qualified oscillators by more than an order of magnitude. We propose a clock noise cancellation scheme that uses two heterodyne signals with positive and negative beat-note frequencies, naturally obtained using both incoming and outgoing laser beams of arm cavities without additional clock modulation schemes. By forming a weighted combination of these signals with time-dependent coefficients, clock jitter contributions are eliminated while preserving gravitational-wave information. We present the theoretical framework, analyze performance under realistic arm-length drifts, and validate the approach through time-domain simulations using parameters from the B-DECIGO concept. Results show that the synthesized signal recovers the original sensitivity and even improves the signal-to-noise ratio by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ for shot noise.
one_liner: A dual-heterodyne combination cancels clock jitter in spaceborne optical-cavity links while preserving gravitational-wave sensitivity.
why_it_matters: Clock noise is a major practical obstacle in long-baseline space interferometry, so cancellation without extra modulation materially improves implementability.
detailed_summary: The paper studies heterodyne links between inter-satellite optical cavities for decihertz gravitational-wave telescopes and targets the clock-jitter limitation. It proposes using the naturally available positive- and negative-beat heterodyne signals from incoming and outgoing cavity beams, then forming a time-dependent weighted combination that removes clock noise. Simulations under realistic arm-length drift parameters from the B-DECIGO concept show recovery of the original sensitivity target. The synthesized observable also improves shot-noise-limited SNR by a factor of sqrt(2).
main_result: The authors demonstrate a clock-noise-canceling signal synthesis for BLFP-style cavity links that restores target sensitivity and yields a sqrt(2) shot-noise SNR gain.
application_angle: The method improves the practicality of ultra-stable optical links for space missions and related long-baseline quantum-limited photonic metrology systems.

title: Measurement-defined control of single-particle interference
abstract: Interference is conventionally attributed to path-accumulated phase differences, with measurement treated as a passive readout. Here we demonstrate that single-particle interference is governed by the relative phase between the prepared quantum state and the detector-defined measurement basis -- a joint quantity that is operationally inaccessible in any conventional interferometer. Using coherently seeded entangled nonlinear biphoton sources, we show that independently scanning the pump phase difference, the seed phase difference, or the signal interferometric phase each produces identical sinusoidal fringes ($V\approx0.99$) -- a three-scan equivalence impossible in any two-mode interferometer. The fringe visibility is continuously controlled through the idler-state overlap, directly encoding quantum distinguishability without idler detection. The same measurement-defined interference law persists from the single-photon to the high-flux regime. The bright-dark collective-state structure demonstrated here unifies coherent population trapping and electromagnetically induced transparency in atomic $Λ$-systems, discrete photonic interference, and single-slit diffraction within a common framework differing only in dark-subspace dimensionality, establishing measurement-defined photonic modes as a fundamental resource for quantum photonics.
one_liner: Coherently seeded biphoton experiments show that single-particle interference can be defined by the detector basis as much as by path phase.
why_it_matters: It introduces a controllable interference mechanism that links distinguishability, measurement basis engineering, and bright-dark mode structure across quantum photonics regimes.
detailed_summary: Using coherently seeded entangled nonlinear biphoton sources, the authors show that scanning the pump phase, seed phase, or signal interferometric phase yields equivalent high-visibility fringes with V about 0.99. The fringe contrast is tuned continuously through idler-state overlap, allowing quantum distinguishability to be encoded without detecting the idler. The same measurement-defined interference law persists from the single-photon regime to high flux. The work frames interference in terms of bright-dark collective measurement modes, connecting photonic interference to lambda-system dark-state physics.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates a three-scan phase equivalence and near-unity-visibility interference governed by the relative phase between prepared state and detector-defined measurement basis.
application_angle: This offers a new control knob for integrated quantum photonic interferometers and measurement-engineered hardware that manipulates interference without extra detection channels.

title: Tight Trade-off Between Internal, Assisted, and External Entanglement
abstract: We derive a tight and saturable monogamy relation for three-qubit pure states that bounds the sum of concurrence and concurrence of assistance by the entanglement with an external qubit. The bound decreases strictly with increasing external entanglement, establishing a precise trade-off between internal and environment-induced entanglement. Equivalent formulations in terms of negativity and its convex-roof extensions follow. Our result provides a unified and quantitative constraint on entanglement distribution in open multipartite quantum systems.
one_liner: A tight monogamy law quantifies how internal and assisted entanglement are reduced by stronger entanglement with an external qubit.
why_it_matters: Precise entanglement-sharing limits are important for noisy network nodes, open-system communication protocols, and certifying multipartite resources.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a tight and saturable trade-off relation for pure three-qubit states, bounding the sum of concurrence and concurrence of assistance by entanglement with an external qubit. The bound decreases strictly as external entanglement increases, making the competition between internal and environment-coupled entanglement explicit. Equivalent formulations are also given in terms of negativity and convex-roof extensions. The result unifies several views of entanglement sharing in open multipartite systems.
main_result: The authors prove a tight, saturable monogamy relation linking internal entanglement, assisted entanglement, and external entanglement for three-qubit pure states.
application_angle: The bound can inform resource accounting and noise-aware protocol design in multipartite quantum communication and distributed entanglement settings.

title: Numerical simulation methods for quantum sensing at parametric criticality
abstract: Microwave photon detection is a key technology for low-temperature superconducting electronics and quantum information processing. A promising possibility is to use switching processes in parametric superconducting devices at criticality, which can be triggered by small perturbations. Here we demonstrate the unique sensing properties of the superconducting Kerr parametric resonator when operated in the proximity of the phase transition boundary. We utilize a semiclassical approximation to provide numerical and analytical results for the Heisenberg-Langevin and Fokker-Planck equations that describe the switching mechanism. We show that the probability of switching events is enhanced by probe input states with energies down to single quanta levels.
one_liner: Models a Kerr parametric resonator near criticality as a microwave-photon sensor sensitive down to near-single-quantum inputs.
why_it_matters: It sharpens how superconducting parametric devices can be engineered for ultralow-energy microwave detection in cryogenic quantum systems.
detailed_summary: The work studies a superconducting Kerr parametric resonator operated close to its phase-transition boundary, where small perturbations can trigger switching events. Using a semiclassical treatment of the Heisenberg-Langevin and Fokker-Planck equations, it characterizes the switching mechanism and the statistics of detection events. The simulations show that probe states with energies at the single-quantum scale substantially enhance switching probability, identifying critical parametric operation as a useful sensing regime.
main_result: The authors show numerically and analytically that near-critical switching in a Kerr parametric resonator is strongly boosted by probe inputs with energies approaching single microwave quanta.
application_angle: This directly supports cryogenic microwave photon detectors for superconducting quantum processors and other low-temperature quantum-electronic platforms.

title: Crossed-Product von Neumann Algebras for Incompressible Navier--Stokes Flows and Spectral Complexity Indicators
abstract: We introduce a traceable operator-algebraic framework for incompressible transport on M= T3 (and, more generally, compact Riemannian manifolds endowed with a smooth invariant probability measure). Given an autonomous divergence-free velocity field u, the time-1 map $Φ$ induces the Koopman unitary U on L2(M) and the crossed-product finite von Neumann algebra Mu\,:= L$\infty$(M) ___$α$ Z= W$\star$(L$\infty$(M),U), equipped with its canonical faithful normal trace $τ$u. Within Mu we define tracial complexity functionals from commutators [U,Mf] (with Mf the multiplication operators) and associated positive elements, and we connect these quantities to Fuglede--Kadison determinants and entropy-like tracial functionals. In parallel, we introduce bounded regularized advection operators T(s) u\,:= KsTuKs as differential-level probes of transport oncommutativity, and we recall the Lie-bracket commutator identity at the formal generator level. This provides a natural algebraic setting in which tracial invariants are well posed and, in principle, computable on discretizations (e.g. cavity flow and vortex benchmarks).
one_liner: Builds an operator-algebraic framework for incompressible flow complexity using crossed-product von Neumann algebras and tracial indicators.
why_it_matters: It offers mathematically well-posed, computable spectral-complexity diagnostics, though its relevance is more indirect than most experimental quantum-sensing papers.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates incompressible transport on compact manifolds through the time-1 flow map, its Koopman unitary, and the associated crossed-product finite von Neumann algebra. Within this algebra, it defines tracial complexity measures from commutators and links them to Fuglede-Kadison determinants and entropy-like functionals. It also introduces regularized advection operators as bounded probes of transport noncommutativity, giving a route to discretizable diagnostics for benchmark flows such as cavity and vortex problems.
platform_or_system: Koopman-flow operator algebra for incompressible transport
main_result: The main result is a traceable algebraic construction of spectral-complexity indicators for incompressible flows that are well defined and in principle computable on discretized systems.
application_angle: Its practical value is in providing rigorous, computation-friendly diagnostics for complex flow dynamics rather than a direct new quantum device or sensing hardware result.

title: Decoherence in Waveguide Quantum Electrodynamics using Matrix Product States
abstract: We present a matrix product state (MPS) method for including decoherence processes in calculations involving waveguide quantum electrodynamics (waveguide QED) using density matrices. The approach is based on collision quantum optics, where the many-body state of the waveguide is represented as discrete time bins, which are efficiently represented using an MPS chain. Our method is a generalization of previous MPS methods, and we demonstrate how one can efficiently expand to density matrices, allowing for the inclusion of various loss processes in the form of Lindblad terms in the Liouvillian superoperator responsible for the relevant dissipation dynamics. As an application of the theory, we study various waveguide QED systems and the influence of emitter pure dephasing (which is one of the most important processes in real systems) on the light-matter interactions, including a two-level system (TLS) in a semi-infinite waveguide with time-delayed feedback, two spatially separated TLSs with finite delays, and finally the scattering of few-photon Fock pulses on a TLS. In addition to emitter pure dephasing, we also show how to include off-chip radiative decay, and show how it differs qualitatively from pure dephasing.
one_liner: Extends MPS waveguide-QED simulations to density matrices, enabling realistic decoherence and loss modeling in delayed photonic networks.
why_it_matters: It gives quantum-photonics designers a scalable way to predict how pure dephasing and radiative loss reshape few-photon interactions in real devices.
detailed_summary: The authors generalize collision-model MPS methods for waveguide QED from pure states to density matrices, representing discrete time bins in an efficient tensor-network chain while adding Lindblad dissipation. They analyze several realistic settings, including a two-level system in a semi-infinite waveguide with delayed feedback, two separated emitters with finite propagation delays, and few-photon Fock-pulse scattering. The study shows how emitter pure dephasing and off-chip radiative decay produce qualitatively different signatures in light-matter dynamics.
platform_or_system: waveguide QED with discrete time-bin MPS
main_result: The paper demonstrates an efficient density-matrix MPS framework that captures dephasing and radiative loss in waveguide-QED systems with delay and few-photon scattering.
application_angle: This is useful for designing integrated quantum-photonic circuits, quantum interconnects, and single-photon devices under realistic noise conditions.

title: Preparation of quasi-two-dimensional Bose mixture of ultracold $^{23}$Na and $^{87}$Rb atoms
abstract: Quantum gases confined in reduced dimensions have enabled the observation of many exotic quantum phenomena. While existing experiments primarily focus on homonuclear systems, we report here on the efficient preparation of a quasi-two-dimensional (2D) heteronuclear quantum degenerate mixture of ultracold $^{23}$Na and $^{87}$Rb. We describe the design of the vacuum system and detail the experimental procedures for preparing the 2D quantum mixture. The designed apparatus has several unique features, including compact and modular 2D-MOT sources, a science chamber that accommodates various lattice geometries, a precision in-vacuum electrode assembly, and high-resolution imaging for both atomic species. After loading the dual-species condensate into a single layer of a vertical optical lattice, we prepare a 2D gas mixture and observe quantum immiscibility in the in-situ equilibrium density profiles. The observed density profiles agree well with mean-field theories. The apparatus provides a versatile platform for investigating several interesting problems, including quantum impurities, quantum droplets, or polar molecules in low dimensions.
one_liner: Demonstrates preparation of a quasi-2D degenerate 23Na-87Rb mixture and observes quantum immiscibility in situ.
why_it_matters: It delivers a flexible cold-atom platform for low-dimensional mixture physics relevant to droplets, impurities, and molecule-oriented quantum experiments.
detailed_summary: This experimental paper details the apparatus and protocol for producing a quasi-two-dimensional heteronuclear quantum-degenerate mixture of 23Na and 87Rb. After loading a dual-species condensate into a single layer of a vertical optical lattice, the team prepares a 2D gas and measures equilibrium density profiles showing quantum immiscibility. The observed profiles agree with mean-field modeling, validating the platform and its control infrastructure, including modular MOT sources, electrode assembly, and high-resolution dual-species imaging.
main_result: The authors realize a quasi-2D degenerate Na-Rb mixture in a single optical-lattice layer and experimentally observe immiscible density profiles consistent with theory.
application_angle: The setup provides an experimentally versatile base for studying low-dimensional quantum mixtures, droplets, impurity physics, and routes toward polar-molecule platforms.

title: Generation of energy-time entangled triphotons in a six-level cold atomic system
abstract: Multiphoton entangled states are pivotal resources for implementing optical quantum information protocols. Recently, energy-time-entangled triphotons have been observed in hot atomic ensembles. However, in these protocols, the complex fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility entailed by four- or five-level systems limits our understanding of triphoton generation. Here, to directly capture the generation mechanism of triphotons and their associated optical properties, we investigate the generation of energy-time-entangled triphotons in a six-level cold atomic ensemble. The fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility indicates the existence of two sets of spontaneous six-wave mixing in the system. Notably, triphoton generation in this system is subject to stringent timing constraints. Collectively, these characteristics give rise to threefold coincidence counts, which -- dominated by the fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility -- exhibit asymmetrically damped Rabi oscillations in the two-dimensional time domain. Furthermore, we analytically derive that the temporal correlation properties of conditional two-photon states are preserved -- a unique feature of $W$-class tripartite entanglement. These results not only lay the groundwork for the experimental preparation of triphotons using six-level systems but also provide key support for understanding the generation mechanism of triphotons involving more complex fifth-order nonlinear susceptibilities.
one_liner: Analyzes six-wave-mixing generation of energy-time-entangled triphotons in a six-level cold atomic ensemble with distinctive timing structure.
why_it_matters: It clarifies a complex nonlinear triphoton source mechanism that could underpin richer multipartite photonic entanglement resources.
detailed_summary: The paper studies triphoton generation in a six-level cold atomic system where fifth-order nonlinear susceptibility produces two spontaneous six-wave-mixing pathways. It predicts strict timing constraints and threefold coincidence patterns with asymmetrically damped Rabi oscillations across a two-dimensional time domain. The analysis also shows that conditional two-photon temporal correlations are preserved, consistent with W-class tripartite entanglement.
main_result: The main result is a concrete theoretical description of energy-time-entangled triphoton generation and coincidence structure in a six-level cold-atom six-wave-mixing system.
application_angle: Such sources are relevant for multipartite quantum networking, photonic entanglement distribution, and advanced optical quantum-information protocols.

title: Dissipative dynamics and superradiant countinuous time crystal in a Rydberg-dressed Dicke system
abstract: The interplay between many-body interactions and controlled dissipation provides a rich framework for exploring nonequilibrium quantum phases. In this work, we explore an open Dicke model including Rydberg-dressed interactions in a driven-dissipative cavity and unveil its unique nonequilibrium dynamics therein. We find that Rydberg-dressed interactions generate an additional critical coupling, which alters the stability of fixed points and hence determines fruitful dynamical phase transitions. Beyond the mean-field limit, we demonstrate that our system supports a superradiant continuous time crystal (CTC) phase, proving CTC can exist in an interacting spin-1/2 system. By bridging driven-dissipative quantum cavity and interacting atomic systems, our Rydberg-dressed Dicke system offers measurable signatures from the cavity emission photons, making it experimentally feasible as a versatile platform for exploring dynamical phase transitions and macroscopic temporal order in open quantum matter.
one_liner: Predicts a superradiant continuous time crystal in an open Rydberg-dressed Dicke system with experimentally visible cavity-emission signatures.
why_it_matters: It identifies a realistic cavity-atom hardware route to nonequilibrium temporal order and dynamical phase transitions in open quantum matter.
detailed_summary: The authors investigate an open Dicke model with Rydberg-dressed interactions in a driven-dissipative cavity, showing that the added interactions create an extra critical coupling and reshape fixed-point stability. Going beyond mean-field theory, they find a superradiant continuous time-crystal phase in an interacting spin-1/2 system. Because the relevant observables are encoded in cavity emission, the predicted nonequilibrium phases should be experimentally accessible in cavity-QED hardware.
main_result: The paper predicts that Rydberg-dressed interactions enable dynamical phase transitions and a superradiant continuous time-crystal phase in an open Dicke cavity system.
application_angle: This points to a measurable atomic-cavity platform for engineering temporal order, probing open-system criticality, and testing nonequilibrium quantum hardware concepts.

title: A Slow-Time Receiver Interface for Turbulent Free-Space Quantum Polarization Links
abstract: Atmospheric turbulence makes free-space quantum polarization links intrinsically time varying, whereas receiver-side reduced interfaces are often treated as static. This paper develops a slow-time receiver interface by extending an aperture-conditioned static model to the temporal domain. The receiver-plane phase field, beam-centroid displacement, and scintillation are modeled as hidden slow-time stochastic processes, from which the reduced interface is generated at each instant. A leading-order closure maps coarse-grained phase roughness to an effective polarization-mixing variance while preserving the inherited local polarization-channel family. Aperture conditioning then yields time-dependent effective depolarization, coherence, and detection descriptors. In a representative weak-turbulence case, the polarization branch remains close to the near-ideal regime, with effective depolarization on the order of \(10^{-3}\) and effective coherence close to unity, whereas the detection branch exhibits visibly stronger fluctuations and a longer correlation time. These results show that a single static receiver-side parameterization is insufficient to characterize the temporal behavior of turbulent free-space quantum links. The resulting interface is intended for receiver-side characterization of time-varying quantum links, with MDI-QKD as one representative downstream application.
one_liner: A slow-time stochastic receiver model captures how turbulence dynamically perturbs free-space quantum polarization links.
why_it_matters: It gives QKD receivers a time-resolved interface for tracking depolarization and detection fluctuations that static channel models miss.
detailed_summary: The paper extends an aperture-conditioned static receiver model into the temporal domain by treating receiver-plane phase, beam wander, and scintillation as hidden slow-time stochastic processes. A leading-order closure converts coarse-grained phase roughness into an effective polarization-mixing variance while preserving the local polarization-channel family. In weak turbulence, the polarization branch stays near ideal with depolarization around 10^-3 and coherence near unity, while detection observables fluctuate more strongly and with longer correlation times. The result is a reduced, time-dependent receiver-side interface intended for downstream protocols such as MDI-QKD.
main_result: It derives a time-dependent receiver interface showing that polarization degradation can remain small even when detection statistics exhibit stronger, slower fluctuations.
application_angle: The model supports receiver-side characterization and adaptive operation of free-space quantum communication links, especially for practical QKD deployments.

title: Enhanced Mid-Infrared Single-Photon Detection with Antenna-Coupled Superconducting Nanowires
abstract: Scaling the photon-detection area of superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) has traditionally been achieved by nanowire meandering. However, material inhomogeneities and fabrication-induced defects, such as line-edge roughness, increase with nanowire length, leading to reduced internal photon-detection efficiency and elevated dark-count rates. This trade-off becomes increasingly pronounced as nanowires are scaled to sub-100 nm widths and sub-5 nm thicknesses required for mid- to far-infrared sensitivity. Here, we demonstrate an antenna-coupled SNSPD architecture that enhances the effective photon-detection area without increasing nanowire length. A crossed bowtie antenna integrated with an 80 nm-wide, 3 nm-thick WSi nanowire yields 15.7$\times$ increase in effective detection area at 7.4 $μ$m compared to a bare nanowire of identical geometric footprint, while maintaining the same internal detection efficiency and dark-count rate. Antenna coupling improves noise-equivalent power and provides a more scalable route to increasing photon-detection area than conventional meander geometries, offering performance benefits for applications in astronomy, biological imaging, and molecular spectroscopy.
one_liner: A crossed-bowtie antenna boosts mid-IR SNSPD collection area by 15.7x without lengthening the nanowire.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core scaling bottleneck for long-wavelength single-photon detection while preserving efficiency and dark-count performance.
detailed_summary: The authors integrate a crossed bowtie antenna with an 80 nm-wide, 3 nm-thick WSi superconducting nanowire to enlarge the effective optical absorption area at 7.4 microns. Compared with a bare nanowire of the same footprint, the antenna-coupled device achieves a 15.7x larger effective detection area while maintaining the same internal detection efficiency and dark-count rate. Because the architecture avoids longer meanders, it mitigates defect and inhomogeneity penalties that worsen in ultra-thin, ultra-narrow long-wavelength SNSPDs. The antenna coupling also improves noise-equivalent power, indicating a cleaner scaling path for mid- to far-IR detectors.
main_result: The device demonstrates a 15.7x increase in effective detection area at 7.4 microns with unchanged internal efficiency and dark-count rate.
application_angle: This architecture is directly relevant to scalable mid-IR photon counting for astronomy, bio-imaging, and molecular spectroscopy.

title: Coherence-gated quantum devices via real-time weak measurement
abstract: Single-photon routers in cavity and circuit QED direct photons based on which energy eigenstate a qubit occupies -- a projective decision that destroys coherence. We propose \emph{coherence-gated routing}, where the routing decision depends on the magnitude of quantum coherence, estimated in real time from simultaneous weak measurements of $σ_x$ and $σ_z$. A photon is accepted depending on whether $S(T) = \sqrt{\langleσ_x\rangle_c^2 + \langleσ_y\rangle_c^2}$ exceeds a tunable threshold~$S_{\mathrm{th}}$. Because coherence is certified at emission, the protocol enables two applications beyond conventional heralded sources: (i)~a quantum random number generator with min-entropy bounded by Bloch-sphere geometry, $H_\infty \geq -\log_2\bigl(\frac{1+\sqrt{1-S_{\mathrm{th}}^2}}{2}\bigr)$, and (ii)~a phase-tracked photon source where independent certification at two nodes bounds the matter-matter entanglement fidelity after Bell-state measurement. The real-time estimator is a security primitive, not merely a numerical tool. We benchmark seven configurations across 3000 trajectories and show that deliberately underestimating detector efficiency ($η_{\mathrm{a}} < η_{\mathrm{true}}$) simultaneously stabilizes the numerics and suppresses overcertification. We trace this analytically through a purity monotonicity result, identify a geometric loophole amplifying purity undercertification into coherence overcertification by~$45\times$, and develop two pointwise bounds: an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck comparison yielding $4.5\%$ operational overcertification (validated at $3.6\%$ from $10^6$ trajectories), and an exponential supermartingale establishing an exponential tail. The gap -- a single polynomial optimization -- defines a path to fully composable security.
one_liner: Weak-measurement coherence estimates are used in real time to gate photon routing and certify emitted photonic resources.
why_it_matters: It turns real-time coherence estimation into a device-level security primitive for certified quantum photonic sources and protocols.
detailed_summary: The work proposes coherence-gated routing in cavity or circuit QED, where photon acceptance depends on a thresholded estimate of qubit coherence reconstructed from simultaneous weak measurements of sigma_x and sigma_z. This replaces eigenstate-based projective routing with a real-time certification layer that enables a min-entropy-bounded QRNG and phase-tracked photon emission for remote entanglement generation. Across 3000 trajectories, the authors show that conservative detector-efficiency assumptions suppress overcertification, and they analyze a geometric loophole that can amplify purity underestimation into 45x coherence overestimation. They derive pointwise control using an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck comparison and an exponential supermartingale, with operational overcertification validated at 3.6% over 10^6 trajectories.
main_result: It demonstrates a coherence-threshold routing protocol with explicit analytical and numerical bounds on certification error for photonic output decisions.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant for secure quantum light sources, device-certified QRNGs, and network nodes that need online quality assurance for emitted photons.

title: Programmable recirculating bricks mesh architecture for photonic neural networks
abstract: General-purpose programmable photonic processors are considered a crucial technology because they combine the ultra high-speed, massive bandwidth, and energy efficiency of light-based computing with the flexibility of software-defined hardware. Unlike application-specific photonic integrated circuits (ASPIC) designed for one task, these processors use reconfigurable waveguide meshes to implement various functions, such as switching, filtering, or AI computation, on a single chip, allowing for rapid prototyping and versatile, on-demand hardware redefinition. Here we report a recirculating bricks mesh architecture that can be easily implemented in photonic neural networks. It will be shown that a single programmable optical system is capable of performing various functions depending on the requirements. In particular, we will show that the same network, after being reprogrammed, can perform many different functions, ranging from a crossbar network to optical interference circuits with variable structures, which can then be subjected to Singular Value Decomposition. Furthermore, the "bricks" mesh serves as an excellent foundation for implementing a monitoring system capable of monitoring the power in each location of the circuit and, subsequently, sel-fcalibrating and stabilizing the circuit using a feedback loop.
one_liner: A recirculating bricks mesh enables reprogrammable photonic neural hardware with built-in monitoring and self-calibration paths.
why_it_matters: It points to more flexible and maintainable photonic processors that can switch roles between AI compute, routing, and interferometric functions.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a programmable recirculating bricks mesh architecture for photonic neural networks and more general photonic signal processing. The same reconfigurable optical hardware can be retasked as a crossbar network, variable-structure interference circuit, or linear transform engine compatible with singular value decomposition workflows. A notable feature is the mesh-friendly monitoring concept, which can observe optical power throughout the circuit and drive feedback-based self-calibration and stabilization. That combination targets one of the main practical barriers to general-purpose photonic processors: keeping large interferometric meshes aligned and usable after reprogramming.
main_result: It presents a single programmable optical mesh that can be reconfigured across multiple circuit functions while supporting distributed monitoring and feedback stabilization.
application_angle: The architecture is relevant for deployable photonic accelerators and field-programmable optical processors that need both versatility and operational stability.

title: Fundamentals and Applications of Hybrid Electroand Opto-mechanical system coupled to Superconducting Qubit: A Short Review
abstract: Superconducting qubits, realized by incorporating Josephson junctions into superconducting circuits, behave as artificial atoms with anharmonic energy spectra and can be precisely controlled and measured using microwave cavities within the framework of circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED). Since its emergence in the early 2000s, cQED has established superconducting qubits as leading candidates for scalable quantum devices and has enabled the exploration of hybrid quantum systems that integrate disparate physical platformsThis review surveys superconducting hybrid quantum electromechanical systems in which mechanical resonators are coupled to superconducting qubits, with a focus on two widely used qubit platforms: the transmon and the fluxonium. We provide an overview of the underlying coupling mechanisms arising from interactions through the phase and charge degrees of freedom of the qubit, and discuss how these mechanisms give rise to both longitudinal and transverse qubit-mechanical interactions. We further review extensions of electromechanical platforms to electro-optomechanical architectures, in which optical cavities are integrated to enable coherent interfacing between superconducting circuits and optical photons. This review aims to present a unified framework and perspective on qubit-mechanical and qubit-mechanical-optical hybrid systems in superconducting quantum technologies and applications related to sensors.
one_liner: This review maps how superconducting qubits couple to mechanical and optical modes in hybrid electromechanical platforms.
why_it_matters: It consolidates the design space for transduction and sensing architectures that could link superconducting processors to mechanics and optics.
detailed_summary: The article surveys hybrid systems where transmon or fluxonium qubits interact with mechanical resonators through charge- and phase-mediated couplings, producing both longitudinal and transverse interaction channels. It also covers electro-optomechanical extensions that add optical cavities to connect microwave-domain superconducting circuits with optical photons. Rather than presenting a single experiment, it organizes the coupling mechanisms and platform tradeoffs into a unified framework for qubit-mechanical and qubit-mechanical-optical hardware. The emphasis is on how these hybrids support sensing functions and coherent interfacing across otherwise incompatible frequency domains.
main_result: It provides a unified hardware-level framework for coupling superconducting qubits to mechanical and optical degrees of freedom across key hybrid architectures.
application_angle: The synthesis is useful for designing quantum sensors and microwave-to-optical interfaces needed in modular quantum hardware.

title: Quantum theory for phonon lasing and non-classical state generation in mixed-species and single trapped ions
abstract: In this article we present a comprehensive theoretical investigation of phonon lasing with mixed-species trapped ions, as demonstrated in [T. Behrle, Phys. Rev. Lett. 131 (2023)], employing both a semi-classical mean-field description and a full quantum theory. We derive an analytic expression for the second-order coherence function, confirming the experimental observation of the system's lasing behaviour above threshold. Building on the successful implementation of the two-ion lasing scheme, we propose a novel approach for achieving phonon lasing with a single trapped ion, offering significant experimental advantages and making the implementation of multiple phonon lasers within a single setup feasible. Furthermore, we explore lasing in a squeezed basis and in different regimes of the Lamb-Dicke approximation, highlighting the potential to produce non-classical states with promising applications in precision sensing. Our analysis of a sensing protocol based on squeezed states, using experimentally feasible parameters, shows a sensitivity enhancement of up to two orders of magnitude.
one_liner: A full quantum theory of trapped-ion phonon lasing identifies routes to single-ion lasers and squeezed-state sensing gains.
why_it_matters: It connects experimentally accessible ion-trap phonon lasers to nonclassical motional states with strong sensing advantages.
detailed_summary: The paper develops both semiclassical and full quantum descriptions of phonon lasing in mixed-species trapped ions, deriving an analytic second-order coherence function that confirms above-threshold lasing behavior seen experimentally. Building on the two-ion scheme, it proposes a single-ion phonon laser that could simplify implementation and allow multiple phonon lasers within one setup. The analysis also explores lasing in a squeezed basis and beyond standard Lamb-Dicke regimes to generate nonclassical motional states. Using experimentally realistic parameters, the authors estimate sensing improvements of up to two orders of magnitude from squeezed-state protocols.
main_result: It establishes a quantum theory for ion-based phonon lasing and proposes single-ion and squeezed-basis operating modes with predicted sensing gains up to 100x.
application_angle: The work is relevant for compact trapped-ion quantum sensors and motional-state engineering in ion-based quantum hardware.

title: Notes on Images and Communication
abstract: This is an active module taught to Bachelor's and Master's students at the University of Birmingham since 2021, covering selected topics in applied optics with an emphasis on imaging, lasers, and classical and quantum communication. The module covers both the theoretical foundations and experimental aspects of these topics, and explores a range of instrumentation examples, including optical and radio telescopes, adaptive optics, laser cutting systems, optical tweezers, laser interferometers, optical atomic clocks, optical coatings, coaxial cables and optical fibres, frequency combs, and quantum key distribution technologies.
one_liner: A teaching-focused optics module surveys imaging, lasers, and classical-to-quantum communication instrumentation, including QKD.
why_it_matters: It is useful as an application-oriented overview linking core optics hardware to real quantum communication systems and measurement platforms.
detailed_summary: This article is a course module rather than a primary research report, but it is strongly application-facing. It connects theoretical and experimental optics across imaging, interferometry, optical clocks, fibers, frequency combs, and quantum key distribution. The value is in how it frames quantum communication technologies within the same instrumentation stack used in broader applied optics. It can serve as a compact technical entry point for researchers moving into photonic quantum communication hardware.
main_result: The work compiles a technically grounded curriculum spanning applied optics devices and quantum communication technologies, with explicit coverage of QKD.
application_angle: It helps bridge training and deployment by contextualizing quantum communication components alongside mainstream optical instrumentation.

title: Engineered broadband Purcell protection using a shared $Π$-filter for multiplexed superconducting qubits
abstract: We propose a broadband Purcell-protection scheme based on a single shared filter integrated directly into the feedline, enabling simultaneous protection of multiple qubits in a compact architecture with minimal hardware overhead. The filter consists of two open-ended stubs connected by an in-line transmission line, forming a $Π$ geometry, and operates via engineered passive microwave interference that suppresses the real part of the environmental admittance over a wide frequency window. Circuit simulations and finite-element modeling show strong suppression of transmission within the target band (the qubit's frequencies) while preserving the readout and reset modes of the multiplexed architecture. For realistic device parameters, the proposed design yields Purcell-limited relaxation times exceeding $1$ ms over a frequency span of approximately $1.5$ GHz, which can be further extended with straightforward modifications of the design. Our results establish the $Π$-filter as a compact and scalable solution for broadband impedance engineering in superconducting quantum circuits, compatible with standard dispersive readout protocols.
one_liner: A shared microwave Pi-filter provides broadband Purcell protection for multiple superconducting qubits with minimal hardware overhead.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical scaling bottleneck in multiplexed superconducting processors by extending qubit lifetimes without sacrificing readout compatibility.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a passive broadband Purcell-protection element formed by two open-ended stubs and an in-line transmission line embedded in the feedline. Simulations and finite-element modeling show that the filter suppresses the real part of the environmental admittance across the qubit band while preserving readout and reset operation in a multiplexed architecture. For realistic parameters, the design yields Purcell-limited T1 values above 1 ms across roughly a 1.5 GHz span, with room for wider bandwidth through modest redesign. The scheme is compact, shared across qubits, and compatible with standard dispersive measurement stacks.
main_result: A single shared Pi-filter can passively protect multiple superconducting qubits, delivering simulated Purcell-limited relaxation times beyond 1 ms over about 1.5 GHz.
application_angle: The design reduces per-qubit hardware overhead and supports denser superconducting quantum processors with scalable multiplexed control and readout.

title: The Rise of Quantum Computing -- Take a BITE for Built Environment and Urban Microclimate Research
abstract: Quantum computing is a new approach to computation that utilizes superposition, entanglement, interference, and tunneling to solve problems too complex for classical computers. This paper discusses the basic concepts and development of quantum computing, exploring its potential applications in the built environment and urban microclimate research. In buildings, quantum computing may help optimize energy management, control HVAC systems, and plan electric vehicle charging networks more efficiently. For urban microclimates, it could accelerate renewable energy planning and support multi-objective design, making it easier to balance urban building performance with climate conditions. Since current quantum hardware is still in the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) stage, we propose the "BITE" principle to guide researchers in choosing suitable problems for quantum acceleration: B (Big search), I (Input-light), T (Tiny computation), and E (Evaluation polish). Although quantum computing still faces challenges such as noise and hardware limits, it offers great potential for developing more climate-resilient, sustainable, and energy-efficient cities of the future.
one_liner: A perspective paper proposes the BITE principle for identifying built-environment problems that may benefit from NISQ-era quantum computing.
why_it_matters: It translates quantum-computing constraints into a practical screening framework for energy, HVAC, EV charging, and urban microclimate optimization.
detailed_summary: This is a methods and applications perspective aimed at researchers in buildings and urban climate rather than a hardware demonstration. The authors summarize quantum-computing primitives and then define the BITE heuristic: Big search, Input-light, Tiny computation, and Evaluation polish. They map this framework onto candidate tasks such as energy management, HVAC control, EV charging coordination, and renewable planning under multi-objective constraints. Its technical contribution is problem selection guidance for near-term quantum workflows in applied environmental research.
platform_or_system: NISQ quantum computing for built-environment models
main_result: The main result is a domain-specific framework, BITE, for selecting built-environment and urban microclimate problems that are plausible targets for near-term quantum acceleration.
application_angle: It gives practitioners a concrete filter for deciding where quantum methods may be useful before investing in full algorithm or hardware integration.

title: Security Risks of VOA-Induced Luminescence in Chip-Based quantum key distribution
abstract: Integrated photonics is widely regarded as a key enabler for scalable quantum key distribution (QKD), offering compactness, stability, and compatibility with semiconductor fabrication. Despite rapid advances in chip-based QKD, the implementation security of integrated photonic components remains insufficiently understood. Here we present the first systematic study of an implementation-level security vulnerability associated with p-n junction-based variable optical attenuators (VOAs), a ubiquitous component in integrated QKD transmitters. We theoretically and experimentally demonstrate that electrically biased p-n junction VOAs emit spontaneous luminescence. Using a single-photon-sensitive spectral measurement technique, we identify the emission wavelength to be centered around 1107 nm, well separated from the C-band quantum signals. This spectral separation gives rise to a previously unrecognized wavelength-resolved side channel, enabling potential wavelength-splitting attacks without directly disturbing the encoded quantum states. By incorporating the measured luminescence into a quantitative security analysis, we show that even extremely weak emission can lead to non-negligible information leakage. Our findings reveal a fundamental and previously overlooked security risk in photonic integrated QKD systems and highlight the necessity of security-aware device design for future integrated quantum communication technologies.
one_liner: Chip-based QKD transmitters using p-n junction VOAs can leak information through spontaneous luminescence at a separate wavelength.
why_it_matters: It exposes a concrete integrated-photonics side channel that can compromise QKD security even when the encoded quantum states are not directly disturbed.
detailed_summary: The authors identify and characterize spontaneous luminescence from electrically biased p-n junction variable optical attenuators used in integrated QKD transmitters. Using single-photon-sensitive spectral measurements, they find emission centered near 1107 nm, spectrally separated from the C-band quantum channel. That separation creates a wavelength-resolved side channel that could support wavelength-splitting attacks without perturbing the encoded states directly. A quantitative security analysis shows that even very weak luminescence can produce non-negligible information leakage.
platform_or_system: Integrated photonic QKD transmitters with p-n VOAs
main_result: The paper demonstrates and quantifies a VOA-induced luminescence side channel in chip-based QKD, with measurable leakage risk despite weak emission.
application_angle: It directly informs secure PIC design rules for deployable QKD systems, including component choice, filtering, and security certification workflows.

title: High precision micro-optical elements on fiber facets via focused-ion beam machining
abstract: Fiber-integrated micro-optical elements promise a scalable approach to photon collection and beam shaping for quantum information processing. Here, we demonstrate single-step fabrication of micro-spherical, micro-spiral, and micro-axicon structures directly on the core of single-mode optical fibers using focused ion beam (FIB) machining with nanometer-scale precision. Atomic force microscopy reveals that micro-concave and micro-convex spherical surfaces achieve shape accuracies of approximately $λ/80$ and $λ/50$ at $λ= 780$ nm, respectively. Optical characterization using a He-Ne laser at 633 nm confirms the expected far-field donut beam patterns for the micro-spiral and micro-axicon structures. Mach-Zehnder interferometry further verifies the corresponding azimuthal and radial phase profiles of the light emitted from the spiral and axicon fibers. Surface metrology shows that the optimized FIB process preserves optical-grade surface quality, introducing no measurable additional roughness at spatial scales relevant to visible and near-infrared operation. These monolithically integrated fiber micro-optical elements enable a broad range of applications in quantum technology, including fiber micro-cavities for cavity quantum electrodynamics, beam shaping for neutral atom trapping, and the generation of structured light for free-space quantum network links.
one_liner: Focused-ion-beam machining creates high-precision micro-optical elements directly on single-mode fiber facets for quantum photonics.
why_it_matters: It offers a fabrication route for monolithic fiber-integrated beam shaping and cavity interfaces needed in scalable quantum hardware and links.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates single-step FIB fabrication of spherical, spiral, and axicon microstructures directly on fiber cores with nanometer-scale precision. AFM shows surface-shape accuracies around lambda/80 for micro-concave and lambda/50 for micro-convex spherical elements at 780 nm, while interferometry verifies the expected azimuthal and radial phase profiles for spiral and axicon outputs. Optical tests confirm donut-mode far-field patterns, and metrology indicates no measurable roughness penalty at visible and near-IR spatial scales. The result is a monolithically integrated fiber platform for collection, focusing, and structured-light generation.
platform_or_system: FIB-machined micro-optics on single-mode fiber facets
main_result: The authors fabricate and validate fiber-facet micro-optics with optical-grade surfaces and phase-engineered output modes suitable for quantum photonic use.
application_angle: These elements can simplify photon interfaces for neutral-atom trapping, fiber micro-cavities, and free-space quantum network transmitters by moving optics onto the fiber itself.

title: Coherent terahertz field tomographic imaging in warm Rydberg vapors
abstract: Rydberg atom-based sensors have emerged as highly sensitive tools for terahertz (THz) metrology, yet most current imaging techniques discard crucial phase information. In this Letter, we present a coherent THz-to-optical conversion scheme in warm Rb vapor that enables complex-amplitude field imaging. By manipulating the phase-matching conditions via an adjustable interference pattern of optical probe beams, we demonstrate the ability to perform tomographic reconstruction of the THz field distribution. We experimentally validate the spatial resolution and phase-sensitivity of the system by resolving sub-centimeter features and identifying incident angles of arrival. Our results establish a robust framework for phase-resolved THz imaging and holography using atomic vapors at room temperature.
one_liner: Warm Rydberg vapor enables coherent THz-to-optical conversion for phase-resolved tomographic imaging of terahertz fields.
why_it_matters: Recovering complex THz field information at room temperature expands atomic-vapor sensors from power detection to imaging, holography, and direction finding.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a coherent THz imaging method in warm rubidium vapor that preserves both amplitude and phase through THz-to-optical conversion. By tuning phase-matching with an adjustable optical probe interference pattern, the system supports tomographic reconstruction of the incident THz field. Experiments resolve sub-centimeter spatial features and determine the angle of arrival, demonstrating both spatial and phase sensitivity. This moves Rydberg-vapor THz sensing beyond intensity-only imaging toward full complex-field metrology.
platform_or_system: Warm Rb Rydberg vapor THz sensor
main_result: The authors demonstrate room-temperature complex-amplitude THz field imaging and tomographic reconstruction using coherent conversion in warm Rydberg vapor.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for practical THz metrology, noninvasive imaging, wavefront sensing, and holographic systems that need phase information without cryogenic hardware.

title: Random-State Generation and Preparation Complexity in Rydberg Atom Arrays
abstract: Rydberg atom arrays are powerful platforms for studying quantum many-body systems. We consider the Rydberg-Ising Hamiltonian on periodic chains and numerically study ensembles of states generated by random global pulse sequences subject to hardware constraints and fixed evolution times. We compare the statistical properties of such states with those of Haar-random states within the relevant lattice symmetry sector. In the strong-interaction regime (short interatomic distance), the dynamics is governed by an effective blockade that restricts Hilbert-space exploration and limits entanglement growth. In this regime, level-spacing statistics of reduced density matrices are close to random-matrix predictions, while the distribution of measurement probabilities deviates from Porter-Thomas behavior. For weaker interactions (larger interatomic distance), the system approaches Haar-like statistics at long times, as reflected in entanglement entropy, entanglement spectrum statistics, and the distribution of measurement probabilities. At intermediate interactions, this behavior is observed on experimentally relevant timescales. Motivated by this observation, we investigate whether generic symmetric quantum states can be efficiently prepared using quantum optimal control in this regime. Employing target states drawn from an ensemble with a broad entropy distribution, we observe high fidelities (infidelities between $10^{-5}$ and $3\times 10^{-2}$ for 9 spins). The fidelity, however, decreases with the entanglement entropy of the target state, demonstrating that highly entangled states are intrinsically harder to prepare under realistic constraints.
one_liner: This work maps when constrained pulse-driven Rydberg arrays generate Haar-like many-body states and how target-state entanglement limits preparation fidelity.
why_it_matters: It gives experimentally relevant guidance on when neutral-atom hardware can act as a practical random-state generator and when control complexity becomes the bottleneck.
detailed_summary: The paper numerically studies random global pulse sequences in periodic Rydberg-Ising chains under fixed-time and hardware-constrained control, comparing the resulting state ensembles with Haar-random states in the symmetry sector. In the strong-blockade regime, Hilbert-space exploration and entanglement growth are restricted: reduced-density-matrix level spacings look random-matrix-like, but output probabilities deviate from Porter-Thomas statistics. For weaker and intermediate interactions, long-time dynamics becomes increasingly Haar-like on experimentally relevant timescales. Using optimal control in that regime, the authors prepare generic symmetric 9-spin target states with infidelities from 1e-5 to 3e-2, with worse performance for higher-entropy targets.
main_result: Intermediate-to-weak interaction regimes enable near-Haar random-state statistics and high-fidelity preparation of generic symmetric states, while highly entangled targets remain harder to realize.
application_angle: The results help define operating regimes for benchmarking, randomized protocols, and state-engineering workflows on neutral-atom processors.

title: Scaling of Quantum Resources for Simulating a Long-Range System
abstract: We simulate a long-range extended Ising model in one dimension using a hybrid quantum algorithm, namely Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE). In this quantum simulation, we investigate how quantum resources scale with system size and interaction strength. Three structure-aware ansatze incorporating nearest-neighbor (NN), next-nearest-neighbor (NNN), and next-next-nearest-neighbor (NNNN) entangling blocks are constructed by mimicking the string operators in the Hamiltonian. We show that energy fidelity alone is not a good indicator for finding the ground state of our model. To overcome this problem, we introduce an additional criterion based on pairwise logarithmic negativity as a more reliable way to find the actual ground state by the VQE. We find that the interaction range parameter alpha primarily governs the minimum number of ansatz layers required, rather than proximity to the quantum critical point. In particular, we show that in the non-local regime (alpha <= 1), the NNN and NNNN ansatze reduce the layer scaling rate by factors of 2.5x and 3.8x relative to NN in all phases, including the critical point. The total number of two-qubit gates required for reliable simulation grows quadratically with system size for all three ansatze. This is consistent with the theoretical prediction, as the number of non-local terms in the Hamiltonian also grows quadratically with the system size. In the local regime, however, the number of required two-qubit gates grows linearly with system size. In contrast, in the quasi-local regime, the required number of two-qubit gates for the quantum simulation is more subtle and depends on the phase of the Hamiltonian.
one_liner: A VQE resource study shows how ansatz locality and interaction range set the gate scaling for simulating long-range Ising chains.
why_it_matters: It provides concrete scaling rules for designing hardware-efficient simulations of nonlocal spin models and shows energy alone can misidentify the true ground state.
detailed_summary: The authors simulate a 1D long-range extended Ising model with structure-aware VQE ansatze built from NN, NNN, and NNNN entangling blocks that mirror Hamiltonian string terms. They show that energy fidelity is insufficient as a ground-state diagnostic and add pairwise logarithmic negativity as a more reliable criterion. In the nonlocal regime with alpha <= 1, NNN and NNNN ansatze cut the required layer scaling by about 2.5x and 3.8x versus NN, including near criticality. The total reliable two-qubit-gate count scales quadratically with system size in the nonlocal case, but linearly in the local regime.
main_result: Interaction range, more than criticality, determines ansatz depth, and longer-range entangling blocks substantially reduce required VQE layers for nonlocal models.
application_angle: These scaling laws inform circuit design and hardware resource budgeting for near-term simulation of long-range quantum matter.

title: Continuous Wave Second Harmonic Generation from an Etchless Lithium Niobate Resonant Metasurface
abstract: Nonlinear metasurfaces provide a route to compact frequency conversion by replacing phase matching and long interaction lengths with resonantly enhanced light matter interaction in subwavelength structures. Extending this capability to continuous wave (CW) operation is particularly important for applications requiring narrow linewidth, stable frequency, and stationary optical fields, but remains extremely challenging. Here, we demonstrate CW second-harmonic generation on a transmission mode, etchless thin film lithium niobate platform enabled by a patterned silicon rich nitride metasurface. This hybrid design combines guided mode resonance coupling, low optical loss, and CMOS compatible processing while keeping most of the optical mode confined in the unpatterned lithium niobate, yielding a measured quality factor of ~2300. Clearly resolved SHG is achieved under sub-kW/cm^2 CW pumping, with a normalized conversion efficiency of 0.156 % cm^2/GW in the low power regime. Interestingly, our work reveals that CW resonant SHG in metasurfaces can exhibit pronounced transient dynamics, including power dependent resonance evolution, overshoot, and nonideal scaling. These findings establish etchless LN SRN metasurfaces as a promising platform for compact CW nonlinear photonics, and show that resonance dynamics are central to the operation and evaluation of CW driven nonlinear metasurfaces.
one_liner: An etchless lithium-niobate metasurface achieves continuous-wave second-harmonic generation with sub-kW/cm^2 pumping and resolved resonant dynamics.
why_it_matters: It advances a CMOS-compatible path to compact CW nonlinear photonics, where stable narrow-linewidth operation matters more than pulsed peak power.
detailed_summary: The device combines an unpatterned thin-film lithium niobate layer with a patterned silicon-rich nitride metasurface to realize transmission-mode guided-mode resonances while keeping most optical energy in the low-loss LN. The measured resonance quality factor is about 2300, and clearly resolved CW SHG is observed under sub-kW/cm^2 pump intensities with normalized conversion efficiency of 0.156% cm^2/GW in the low-power regime. The study also identifies pronounced transient behavior under CW drive, including power-dependent resonance shifts, overshoot, and nonideal scaling. These dynamics show that CW nonlinear metasurface performance must be evaluated beyond steady-state efficiency alone.
main_result: The authors demonstrate CW second-harmonic generation in a transmission-mode etchless lithium-niobate metasurface with measurable efficiency at low continuous pump intensity.
application_angle: This platform is relevant for integrated frequency conversion, narrow-linewidth photonics, and chip-scale nonlinear optics compatible with scalable fabrication.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Maximizing Quantum Fisher Information in Time-Dependent Many-Body Systems
abstract: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) sets the ultimate precision limit for parameter estimation and is therefore a central quantity in quantum metrology. In time-dependent many-body systems, however, maximizing QFI is a highly non-trivial task due to the combined effects of non-commutativity, control complexity, and the exponential growth of the Hilbert space. In this work, we present a physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework to address this problem through the learning of counter-diabatic quantum dynamics. Our approach combines a variational PINN formulation with a Magnus-expansion treatment of time-ordered evolution, enabling the adiabatic gauge potential and the scheduling function to be inferred directly from the underlying physics while enforcing the Euler-Lagrange structure of the protocol. The method is applied to several families of driven spin Hamiltonians, including nearest-neighbor, dipolar, and trapped-ion-inspired interactions, for systems of up to six qubits. The numerical results show that the proposed framework systematically improves over reference solutions based only on the Euler-Lagrange condition, yielding high normalized QFI together with favorable fidelity and extremal-balance metrics while preserving small phsical residuals. The analysis further shows that learning the scheduling function provides a clear performance advantage in most cases, and reveals non-trivial finite-size effects, with $q=3$ emerging as a particularly challenging regime. Although scalability remains limited by the exponential growth of the operator space and by automatic-differentiation costs, the results demonstrate that PINNs constitute a viable and physically grounded route for learning metrologically optimal control strategies in interacting quantum systems.
one_liner: A physics-informed neural-network framework learns counter-diabatic controls that raise quantum Fisher information in driven many-body spin systems.
why_it_matters: It offers a physically grounded route to metrology-oriented control synthesis in interacting systems where exact optimization is hard.
detailed_summary: The method combines a variational physics-informed neural network with a Magnus-expansion treatment of time-ordered evolution to infer both the adiabatic gauge potential and the scheduling function directly from the governing dynamics. It is tested on nearest-neighbor, dipolar, and trapped-ion-inspired driven spin Hamiltonians up to six qubits. Across these families, the learned protocols outperform reference solutions based only on the Euler-Lagrange condition, improving normalized QFI while maintaining favorable fidelity, extremal-balance metrics, and small physical residuals. The study also finds that learning the schedule itself usually helps and that the q=3 regime is especially challenging.
main_result: PINN-learned counter-diabatic protocols systematically improve metrological performance, achieving higher normalized QFI than baseline control constructions across several many-body models.
application_angle: The approach can support practical design of sensing protocols in programmable quantum simulators and other controllable many-body metrology platforms.

title: Bosonization, vertex operators and maximal violation of the Bell-CHSH inequality in wedge regions
abstract: It is pointed out that the vertex operators of a chiral boson in 1+1 dimensions provide an explicit realization of dichotomic, bounded, Hermitian operators that saturate the Tsirelson bound of the Bell-CHSH inequality in the vacuum state.
one_liner: Chiral-boson vertex operators are shown to realize bounded dichotomic observables that maximally violate Bell-CHSH in wedge regions.
why_it_matters: It gives an explicit field-theoretic construction of Tsirelson-saturating observables, sharpening links between relativistic quantum fields and operational nonlocality.
detailed_summary: The paper identifies vertex operators of a 1+1D chiral boson as explicit bounded Hermitian dichotomic observables associated with wedge-region measurements. Using these operators, the vacuum state attains the maximal Bell-CHSH value allowed by quantum theory, saturating the Tsirelson bound. The result is constructive rather than purely existential, tying bosonization tools directly to Bell nonlocality in relativistic settings. It provides a compact analytic example of maximal quantum correlations in quantum field theory.
main_result: Vertex operators of the chiral boson furnish an explicit realization of observables whose vacuum correlations saturate the Bell-CHSH Tsirelson bound.
application_angle: While foundational, the construction informs architectures that emulate field-theoretic entanglement and nonlocal correlations in photonic or analog quantum platforms.

title: AtomTwin.jl: a physics-native digital twin framework for neutral-atom quantum processors
abstract: AtomTwin$.$jl is an open-source Julia package for developing and simulating quantum protocols, hardware configurations and building digital twins for neutral-atom quantum processors and related atomic quantum devices. AtomTwin operates between mathematical models and physical devices; modeling atoms, optical tweezers, laser fields, atomic motion, interactions, and noise processes natively from physical geometry and parameters, without requiring users to define Hamiltonians manually. The package provides hardware-level instruction sequences, high-performance solvers for coupled quantum and classical dynamics, and a ready-to-use model for ytterbium-171 atoms in an extensible framework designed to accommodate a greater variety of atomic species and hardware components in the future. This paper describes the software architecture, performance benchmarks against existing toolboxes, and a demonstrated end-to-end application: preparation of a logical Bell state in the $[[4,2,2]]$ error-detecting code with four $^{171}$Yb atoms in moveable tweezers.
one_liner: AtomTwin.jl is a physics-native digital-twin stack for neutral-atom processors, demonstrated on logical Bell-state preparation with movable tweezers.
why_it_matters: It closes a practical gap between device physics and protocol design by letting users simulate geometry, motion, control, and noise without hand-building Hamiltonians.
detailed_summary: AtomTwin.jl is an open-source Julia framework that natively models atoms, tweezer layouts, laser fields, motional dynamics, interactions, and noise from physical device parameters rather than manually specified Hamiltonians. It includes hardware-level instruction sequences and high-performance solvers for coupled quantum-classical evolution, plus a ready Yb-171 device model. The paper benchmarks the package against existing toolboxes and demonstrates an end-to-end application: preparing a logical Bell state in the [[4,2,2]] error-detecting code using four movable-tweezer Yb-171 atoms. This makes the package both a simulation environment and a hardware-development digital twin.
main_result: The framework enables physics-native digital-twin simulation of neutral-atom hardware and is validated on logical Bell-state preparation in a four-atom [[4,2,2]] code experiment.
application_angle: It can accelerate calibration, protocol development, and architecture co-design for emerging neutral-atom quantum processors and related atomic devices.

title: Dissipative Preparation of Correlated Quantum States in Dipolar Rydberg Arrays
abstract: Preparing correlated quantum states is essential for emerging technologies, but remains challenging in many-body systems. Here we propose a dissipative protocol that engineers nonreciprocal, energy-selective transitions to steer dipolar quantum systems toward desired many-body states. This is realized by introducing two types of controllable dissipative auxiliary atoms that act as nonreciprocal excitation and de-excitation channels, respectively, enabling a directional walk in Hilbert space. This approach enables stabilization of states across the many-body spectrum, not limited to the ground state and requiring no \textit{a priori} knowledge of the Hamiltonian. Our approach is designed for neutral atoms in dipolar Rydberg arrays, but applies broadly to setups with similar capabilities, providing a flexible and scalable framework for state preparation in programmable platforms.
one_liner: A dissipative scheme uses auxiliary atoms to steer dipolar Rydberg arrays into targeted correlated many-body states.
why_it_matters: It offers a scalable state-preparation route for programmable neutral-atom hardware without requiring prior knowledge of the many-body Hamiltonian.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes dissipative preparation based on two auxiliary-atom channels that implement nonreciprocal excitation and de-excitation processes. These engineered transitions create a directional walk through Hilbert space, allowing the system to accumulate in selected correlated eigenstates across the many-body spectrum rather than only the ground state. The protocol is formulated for dipolar Rydberg arrays, where controllable dissipation and strong interactions are available. Because it is energy selective and does not require a priori Hamiltonian knowledge, it is well suited to programmable many-body platforms.
