main_result: The work demonstrates a general dissipative protocol that stabilizes target correlated many-body states in Rydberg arrays via engineered nonreciprocal auxiliary dissipation.
application_angle: The method can reduce overhead for preparing resource states in analog simulators and neutral-atom quantum processors, improving scalability of quantum state engineering.

title: Floquet engineering of spin-spin interactions in a hybrid atomic system
abstract: We demonstrate dynamical control of the effective spin-spin interaction, dominated by Fermi-contact interaction, in a hybrid spin system via parametric modulation. We show that, in an alkali-noble-gas comagnetometer, periodic modulation of the direction of the electron spin polarization with respect to the nuclear polarization leads to a Floquet-induced renormalization of the spin-exchange coupling, governed by a zeroth-order Bessel function. This effect enables continuous tuning and suppression of the effective interaction strength without altering the intrinsic properties of the system. We develop a theoretical model that supports the experimental measurements. The results establish a general mechanism for controlling interaction strengths in hybrid atomic systems and provide new opportunities for precision measurements and quantum memories.
one_liner: Floquet modulation tunes and can suppress effective spin-spin coupling in an alkali-noble-gas hybrid comagnetometer.
why_it_matters: Controllable interaction renormalization in hybrid atomic systems can directly improve precision sensing and enable better-tailored quantum memory operation.
detailed_summary: The experiment modulates the direction of electron-spin polarization relative to nuclear polarization in an alkali-noble-gas comagnetometer. This periodic drive renormalizes the effective Fermi-contact-dominated spin-spin interaction according to a zeroth-order Bessel-function dependence, giving continuous control over coupling strength. A theoretical Floquet model matches the measurements and explains the suppression regime. The approach changes the effective interaction dynamically without altering intrinsic material or atomic parameters.
main_result: The authors experimentally show continuous Floquet control and suppression of effective spin-exchange coupling in a hybrid atomic spin system.
application_angle: Such tunable coupling is useful for optimizing comagnetometer sensitivity, mitigating back-action, and configuring interaction strengths for atomic-memory and precision-measurement protocols.

title: Efficient Routing of Quantum LDPC Codes on Programmable 2D Toric Architectures
abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check codes are promising candidates towards scalable fault-tolerant quantum computation. Among these, bivariate bicycle (BB) codes offer superior encoding rates and large code distance compared to surface codes. However, their requirement on long-range stabilizer measurements poses significant challenges for implementation on realistic hardware with limited connectivity, such as superconducting circuit platforms. In this work, we introduce a novel hardware-software co-design that leverages a programmable communication network architecture to address these limitations. Our approach utilizes a 2D toric network of oscillators as a flexible communication fabric linking qubits at each site. Such architecture significantly reduces the number of long-range couplers required from $O(n)$ to $O(\sqrt{n})$. Dual-rail qubits, along with native gates including Swap-Wait-Swap gates and beamsplitter SWAPs, ensure that long-range two-qubit gates can be executed with high fidelity and low latency. To further enhance performance, our qubit layout and routing algorithm utilize symmetries of the codes and enable maximum parallelism for long-range two-qubit gates, maintaining a low syndrome extraction cycle duration and scalability over the code length. We perform circuit-level simulation with realistic noise modeling based on experimental hardware parameters, observing an logical error rate per logical qubit per cycle of 3.06\% for $[[18, 4, 4]]$ BB code, 2.6$\times$ less than the existing experimental result. These findings provide a practical roadmap and identify key technological advancements needed to achieve low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computing at scale.
one_liner: A toric oscillator network and routing stack make quantum LDPC syndrome extraction more practical on 2D programmable hardware.
why_it_matters: It addresses one of the main implementation barriers for high-rate LDPC codes by reducing long-range connectivity overhead on realistic architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a hardware-software co-design for implementing bivariate bicycle LDPC codes on a programmable 2D toric architecture of oscillators. The communication fabric reduces the number of required long-range couplers from O(n) to O(sqrt(n)), while dual-rail qubits and native Swap-Wait-Swap and beamsplitter SWAP operations support fast remote two-qubit gates. A symmetry-aware qubit layout and routing algorithm maximize parallel stabilizer operations and keep syndrome extraction cycles short. Circuit-level simulations with realistic noise give a logical error rate per logical qubit per cycle of 3.06% for the [[18,4,4]] BB code, about 2.6 times lower than an existing experimental result.
platform_or_system: programmable 2D toric oscillator network with dual-rail qubits
main_result: The work shows that a toric communication fabric plus optimized routing can efficiently realize BB-code stabilizer measurements with improved simulated logical performance.
application_angle: This provides a concrete path to deploying higher-rate quantum LDPC codes on scalable superconducting or oscillator-based hardware with reduced interconnect cost.

title: Learning error suppression strategies for dynamic quantum circuits
abstract: Dynamic quantum circuits integrate unitary evolution with mid-circuit measurement and feedforward, enabling conditional operations essential for efficient quantum algorithms and foundational for fault-tolerant quantum computation. However, such operations introduce measurement-induced errors and control constraints that are not addressed by conventional error-suppression techniques. Here, we introduce an empirical learning framework that optimizes dynamical decoupling (DD) sequences for dynamic circuits at the level of circuit subintervals and qubit subregisters. Applying empirically learned DD sequences, we achieve a three-fold reduction in average dynamic circuit error rates as measured via randomized benchmarking. We apply the learned strategies to the dynamic circuit implementation of the quantum Fourier transform with measurement (QFT+M), demonstrating nontrivial process fidelity on connected chains of up to 20 qubits. Applying the resulting enhancement, we perform a high signal-to-noise QFT immediately following the preparation of a 10-qubit entangled state. Our results demonstrate that empirically optimized DD systematically outperforms theoretically derived sequences for dynamic circuits, establishing it as an efficient approach for error suppression in dynamic quantum circuits, with direct relevance to applications requiring measurement and feedback such as quantum error correction.
one_liner: Empirically learned dynamical-decoupling schedules cut dynamic-circuit errors and enable larger feedforward-based quantum routines.
why_it_matters: Dynamic circuits are central to error correction and adaptive algorithms, so practical error suppression at the subinterval level is directly valuable for near-term hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors develop an empirical learning framework that optimizes dynamical-decoupling sequences for specific subintervals and qubit subregisters in dynamic circuits. On hardware with mid-circuit measurement and feedforward, the learned sequences reduce average dynamic-circuit error rates by about a factor of three under randomized benchmarking. They then apply the method to measurement-based quantum Fourier transform circuits, obtaining nontrivial process fidelity on connected chains up to 20 qubits. The improved control also supports a high-SNR QFT immediately after preparing a 10-qubit entangled state.
main_result: The study demonstrates that empirically optimized DD outperforms theory-derived sequences and yields a three-fold reduction in dynamic-circuit error rates.
application_angle: The method is a hardware-efficient route to stabilize measurement-and-feedback primitives needed for quantum error correction, adaptive sensing, and conditional quantum algorithms.

title: Operational Discriminability and Bell-Contextual Correlations
abstract: We investigate discriminability from an operational and contextuality-oriented perspective using a two-copy comparison game based on SWAP-type measurements. The resulting score $D_{\mathrm{op}}$ provides an experimentally accessible notion of distinguishability that does not rely on a minimum-error discrimination task. We first examine whether this discriminability game can directly witness preparation contextuality. Within a preparation-noncontextual ontological model, we derive a direct upper bound on the game score under a SWAP-like comparison rule and a sharp single-copy test, and show that this bound is saturated in the natural qubit realization. Thus, the direct game alone does not provide a contextuality witness in that regime. We then consider a Bell-coupled scenario in which two-copy comparison measurements are applied to Bob's conditional preparations. This yields a state-dependent upper bound on the CHSH value in terms of operational separation parameters, and hence in terms of the distinguishability of the conditional states. Our results establish a quantitative link between operational discriminability and the strength of nonclassical correlations, showing that discriminability can act as an operational resource controlling Bell-type contextual behavior.
one_liner: An operational two-copy discriminability game is linked quantitatively to Bell-type contextual correlations.
why_it_matters: It reframes distinguishability as an experimentally accessible resource connected to nonclassical correlations, which is relevant for photonic Bell tests and comparison measurements.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a two-copy comparison game using SWAP-type measurements to define an operational discriminability score, D_op. It proves that in preparation-noncontextual models, the direct game obeys an upper bound that is saturated in the natural qubit realization, so this standalone task is not a contextuality witness in that regime. The analysis then shifts to a Bell-coupled setting where Bob receives conditional preparations and comparison measurements induce a state-dependent CHSH bound in terms of operational separation parameters. This establishes a concrete relation between distinguishability and the achievable strength of Bell-contextual correlations.
main_result: The main result is a quantitative bound connecting operational state discriminability to CHSH Bell-correlation strength in a Bell-coupled scenario.
application_angle: The framework can inform photonic experiments that use interference-based comparison or Bell tests by tying accessible discrimination metrics to nonclassical resource strength.

title: Low noise resonant amplification by optical injection-locking and residual phase noise cancellation
abstract: We demonstrate a low noise, high-gain, resonant optical amplifier that combines injection locking with feed-forward cancellation of residual phase noise. The wavelength-agnostic architecture uses a commercial semiconductor diode laser as a power amplifier while preserving the spectral purity of a weak reference. Although injection locking enforces phase coherence, finite residual phase noise within the locking regime limits high-fidelity transfer of low phase noise from the reference laser to the injection-locked laser, particularly at large gain. Here, the residual phase error is measured via optical heterodyne detection and canceled using feed-forward phase correction. Compared to injection locking alone, the amplifier achieves up to 38 dB phase-noise reduction at Fourier frequencies above 1 kHz for injection ratios down to -57 dB. This approach enables ASE-free amplification of low-power, low-noise optical references, including individual lines from optical frequency combs.
one_liner: Injection-locked diode amplification with feed-forward phase correction delivers high-gain optical amplification with strongly reduced phase noise.
why_it_matters: It enables practical low-noise amplification of weak optical references and comb lines without adding ASE, a key need in precision photonics systems.
detailed_summary: The authors combine optical injection locking of a commercial semiconductor diode laser with heterodyne-based measurement and feed-forward cancellation of residual phase noise. While injection locking preserves phase coherence, residual phase fluctuations limit spectral-purity transfer at high gain; the added feed-forward path suppresses that residual error. The system achieves up to 38 dB additional phase-noise reduction above 1 kHz at injection ratios as low as -57 dB compared with injection locking alone. The architecture is wavelength agnostic and functions as an ASE-free resonant amplifier for very weak references.
main_result: The work demonstrates low-noise resonant optical amplification with up to 38 dB residual phase-noise reduction beyond standard injection locking.
application_angle: This is directly useful for optical clocks, coherent links, comb-based metrology, and other photonics systems that need to amplify weak ultra-low-noise optical references.

title: High-flux sub-Poissonian twin-beam generation from warm atomic vapor
abstract: We demonstrate sub-Poissonian twin-beam generation via near-degenerate spontaneous four-wave mixing in warm $^{85}\mathrm{Rb}$ at 795 nm. The twin beams exhibit approximately $5.5~\mathrm{dB}$ of intensity-difference squeezing in free space and about $3~\mathrm{dB}$ after coupling into polarization-maintaining fibers. Time-resolved photon counting yields Mandel parameters of $Q \approx -0.7$ for each beam, revealing strong photon-number squeezing in each beam individually. The temporal correlation between the twin photons exhibits a distinctive flat-topped profile, reflecting multiple $χ^{(3)}$ processes in the atomic medium and showing excellent agreement with theory. This fiber-compatible, near atomic-resonance, high-flux sub-Poissonian twin-photon source is well suited for integration into scalable quantum-enhanced sensing and information processing applications.
one_liner: Warm 85Rb vapor generates high-flux twin beams with sub-Poissonian statistics and fiber-coupled squeezing near 795 nm.
why_it_matters: It combines atomic-resonant operation, high brightness, and fiber compatibility, making nonclassical light more practical for sensing and networked photonics.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates near-degenerate spontaneous four-wave mixing in warm 85Rb vapor to produce twin beams at 795 nm with nonclassical intensity correlations. The authors measure about 5.5 dB intensity-difference squeezing in free space and about 3 dB after coupling into polarization-maintaining fiber, showing useful preservation under photonic interfacing. Time-resolved photon counting gives Mandel Q around -0.7 for each beam individually, indicating strong photon-number squeezing beyond only pairwise correlation measurements. They also resolve a flat-topped temporal correlation profile attributed to multiple chi(3) processes in the atomic medium and matched by theory.
main_result: A warm-vapor source produces high-flux, near-resonant twin beams with strong sub-Poissonian statistics and measurable squeezing after fiber coupling.
application_angle: This is relevant for deployable quantum sensing and communication systems that need bright nonclassical light compatible with atomic interfaces and standard fiber handling.

title: Understanding Quantum Instruments
abstract: The quantum instrument (QI) formalism is required to model mid-circuit measurements (MCMs) and the dependence of the post-measurement state on the measurement outcome. Correctly modeling QIs is essential for applications using MCMs, such as adaptive circuits and quantum error correction. Although QIs yield a joint quantum-classical state after measurement, errors in QIs can still be represented by a $d^2 \times d^2$ superoperator (e.g., process or transfer matrix) for each outcome, just as superoperators describe Markovian errors on unitary gates. However, because the joint quantum-classical system has a distinct error model for each outcome, this complicates the usual interpretation of process- or transfer-matrix error models. This Note offers practical guidance on understanding and interpreting QI error models.
one_liner: This note clarifies how to model and interpret quantum instruments for mid-circuit measurement and outcome-conditioned noise.
why_it_matters: Reliable quantum-instrument models are necessary for error correction and adaptive circuits, where measurement backaction and outcome dependence directly affect hardware performance.
detailed_summary: The work explains the quantum-instrument formalism needed when measurements produce both a classical outcome and an outcome-dependent post-measurement quantum state. It emphasizes that each measurement outcome can still be represented by its own d^2 by d^2 superoperator, even though the overall object is a joint quantum-classical process. The technical contribution is interpretive rather than experimental: it shows how standard process- or transfer-matrix intuition must be modified for outcome-conditioned error channels. This is particularly relevant for benchmarking and calibrating hardware primitives used in mid-circuit measurement workflows.
platform_or_system: mid-circuit measurement primitives on quantum processors
main_result: The paper provides a practical framework for understanding per-outcome superoperator error models for quantum instruments.
application_angle: It helps hardware teams characterize and reason about measurement-induced errors in adaptive and fault-tolerant quantum circuits.

title: Volumetric Processing of Structured Light Integrated in Glass
abstract: Light with complex structures in polarization, phase and amplitude, has attracted a lot of attention in a broad range of applications and fundamental studies in classical and quantum optics. Along with the increased interest in structured light comes a need for efficient modulation platforms operating simultaneously for many modes. Multi plane light conversions (MPLC), i.e., multiple consecutive phase modulations in combination with free space propagation, have enabled such unitary transformations, which are usually built by bulky optical components, limited to scalar modulation, or rely on advanced nanofabrication techniques. Here, we demonstrate an efficient, monolithic MPLC architecture through direct laser writing in standard fused silica glass, resulting in a device with a compact form factor of only a few cubic millimeters. Our scheme is based on volumetric engineering of the glass's birefringence through laser-written nanogratings, which enables spatial control over full vectorial light structures. To showcase the approach's potential for integrated multimode-multipath optical networks, we demonstrate multi-mode unitary transformations, mode conversions, and complex beam-splitting for scalar light. We further extend the MPLC operation to vectorial light and implement various polarization-controlled spatial mode operations as well as the transformation of the topology of an optical Skyrmion. Finally, we highlight our scheme's promise for optical communications and implement a miniaturized multiplexer for spatial modes and polarization operating at telecom wavelength.
one_liner: A laser-written glass device implements compact volumetric MPLC for scalar and vector structured-light transformations at telecom wavelengths.
why_it_matters: It shrinks complex mode and polarization processing into a monolithic glass element, which is attractive for scalable multimode photonic links and integrated quantum optics.
detailed_summary: The authors realize a monolithic multi-plane light conversion architecture by direct laser writing nanogratings into fused silica, engineering spatially varying birefringence in a volume only a few cubic millimeters in size. Unlike many MPLC implementations, the platform supports full vectorial control, enabling transformations over polarization as well as spatial mode structure. They demonstrate multimode unitary transforms, mode conversion, beam splitting for scalar light, and polarization-controlled spatial-mode operations including optical-Skyrmion topology transformation. A telecom-wavelength spatial-mode and polarization multiplexer shows direct relevance to compact optical networking.
main_result: A compact glass-integrated MPLC performs efficient scalar and vector structured-light transformations, including telecom-band multiplexing.
application_angle: The approach could reduce size, alignment complexity, and fabrication overhead for multimode quantum and classical communication hardware.

title: Stabilization of bulk quantum orders in finite Rydberg atom arrays
abstract: Arrays of ultracold neutral atoms, also known as Rydberg atom arrays, are rapidly developing into a powerful and versatile platform for quantum simulation. However, theoretical predictions about the bulk quantum phases of matter present in these systems have often diverged from experimental realizations on finite-sized arrays due to the strong effects of the boundaries. Here we propose a general, experimentally straightforward strategy to mitigate the effects of the boundaries and thus enable finite-sized arrays to stabilize bulk-like quantum order. Our scheme makes use of the properties of the ubiquitous disordered phase in Rydberg systems, driving the boundaries into an unbiased set of configurations that depend on the bulk physics. We numerically demonstrate the efficacy of this protocol in one- and two-dimensional systems on both ordered and critical phases.
one_liner: A boundary-conditioning protocol lets finite Rydberg arrays stabilize bulk-like ordered and critical phases more faithfully.
why_it_matters: Finite-size boundary effects are a major bottleneck in neutral-atom simulators, so a simple mitigation method can improve phase studies without larger hardware.
detailed_summary: This paper proposes an experimentally simple strategy to suppress boundary-induced distortions in finite Rydberg atom arrays by steering edge atoms into an unbiased ensemble of configurations linked to the bulk physics. The mechanism exploits the disordered phase commonly present in these systems, using it to soften the boundary's tendency to pin or distort ordering patterns. Numerical results show improved stabilization of both ordered and critical phases in one- and two-dimensional geometries. The method addresses a central mismatch between theoretical bulk phase predictions and what current finite arrays can realize.
main_result: Numerics show that controlled boundary conditioning can recover bulk-like quantum order in finite 1D and 2D Rydberg arrays.
application_angle: This can improve the fidelity of many-body phase experiments on near-term neutral-atom hardware without requiring substantially larger devices.

title: Option Pricing on Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Computers: A Quantum Neural Network Approach
abstract: In a global derivatives market with notional values in the hundreds of trillions of dollars, the accuracy and efficiency of pricing models are of fundamental importance, with direct implications for risk management, capital allocation, and regulatory compliance. In this work, we employ the Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) framework not as an end in itself, but as a controlled benchmark environment in which to rigorously assess the capabilities of quantum machine learning methods. We propose a fully quantum approach to option pricing based on Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs), and, to the best of our knowledge, present one of the first implementations of such a methodology on currently available quantum hardware. Specifically, we investigate whether QNNs, by exploiting the geometric structure of Hilbert space, can effectively approximate option pricing functions. Our implementation utilizes a compact 2-qubit QNN architecture evaluated across multiple state-of-the-art quantum processors, including IBM Fez, IQM Garnet, IonQ Forte, and Rigetti Ankaa-3. This cross-platform study reveals distinct hardware-dependent performance characteristics while demonstrating that accurate pricing approximations can be achieved consistently across different devices despite the constraints of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware. The results provide empirical evidence that QNN-based approaches constitute a viable framework for derivative pricing. While the analysis is conducted within the BSM setting, the broader significance lies in the potential extension of these methods to more realistic and computationally demanding models, including local volatility, stochastic volatility, and interest rate frameworks commonly used in practice.
one_liner: A 2-qubit quantum neural network prices options across several NISQ platforms with hardware-dependent but consistent accuracy.
why_it_matters: Cross-platform demonstrations on real processors help test whether quantum ML can deliver useful approximations under realistic hardware noise constraints.
detailed_summary: The authors implement a compact 2-qubit quantum neural network for option pricing in the Black-Scholes-Merton setting and run it on multiple current quantum processors, including IBM Fez, IQM Garnet, IonQ Forte, and Rigetti Ankaa-3. The study uses finance as a controlled benchmark to assess how well QNN function approximators survive device noise and platform-specific characteristics. Results show that pricing functions can be approximated with useful accuracy across disparate hardware modalities despite NISQ limitations. The comparison also exposes hardware-dependent performance differences that matter for application mapping and workload selection.
platform_or_system: 2-qubit QNN on superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral platforms
main_result: Real-device experiments show that a small QNN can approximate option-pricing functions across several NISQ processors.
application_angle: It is a practical benchmark for near-term quantum application deployment and for matching quantum ML workloads to available hardware.

title: Lindbladian Homotopy Analysis Method to Solve Nonlinear Partial Differential Equations
abstract: Quantum scientific computing is to solve engineering and science problems such as simulation and optimization on quantum computerss. Solving ordinary and partial differential equations (PDEs) is essential in simulations. However, existing quantum approaches to solve nonlinear PDEs suffer from the issues of curse of dimensionality and convergence during the linearization process. In this paper, a Lindbladian homotopy analysis method (LHAM) is proposed as a quantum differential equation solver to simulate nonunitary and nonlinear dynamics. The original nonlinear problem is first converted to a recursive sequence of linear PDEs with the homotopy analysis and reformulated as a higher-dimensional lower block triangular linear homogeneous system. The solution is then embedded in the density matrix and obtained through the Lindbladian dynamics simulation. Compared to other methods such as the Carleman linearization and Koopman-von Neumann approach where the dimension of Hilbert space increases polynomially with the inverse of truncation error, the Hilbert space in LHAM increases only logarithmically. LHAM is demonstrated nonlinear PDEs including Burgers' equation and magnetohydrodynamics equations.
one_liner: A Lindbladian homotopy method maps nonlinear PDE solving to nonunitary quantum dynamics with favorable Hilbert-space scaling.
why_it_matters: If realizable, it could make quantum PDE solvers for engineering models more resource-efficient than common linearization-based approaches.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a Lindbladian homotopy analysis method that converts a nonlinear PDE into a recursive sequence of linear PDEs and then embeds the resulting lower block-triangular homogeneous system into density-matrix evolution. By framing the computation as Lindbladian simulation, the method targets nonunitary and nonlinear dynamics directly rather than relying on more expansion-heavy encodings. The claimed technical advantage is that the required Hilbert-space dimension grows only logarithmically with inverse truncation error, contrasting with polynomial growth in Carleman and Koopman-von Neumann style methods. Demonstrations are given for benchmark nonlinear problems including Burgers' equation and magnetohydrodynamics equations.
main_result: The method reformulates nonlinear PDE solving as Lindbladian dynamics with improved asymptotic Hilbert-space scaling relative to common alternatives.
application_angle: Efficient nonlinear PDE solvers would be relevant to simulation-heavy sensing and photonics design workflows, especially where dissipative or nonlinear models dominate.

title: A robust laser cavity platform for NV-diamond singlet infrared absorption magnetometry
abstract: The negatively charged nitrogen-vacancy center (NV$^-$) in diamond is a versatile platform for quantum magnetometry under ambient conditions. Recently, laser threshold magnetometry (LTM) has been proposed as a means to significantly enhance the sensitivity of NV-based magnetometers by incorporating a diamond hosting NV$^-$ centers within a laser cavity and operating near threshold. While demonstrations have validated the concept, practical implementations remain technically demanding, requiring high pump powers and precise alignment of free-space cavities. It remains unclear whether the benefits of operating near threshold will outpace increased laser noise. In this work, we integrate an NV-diamond with a high NV$^-$ content into a compact external cavity diode laser and demonstrate singlet infrared absorption optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR). The system exhibits exceptional threshold current stability, enabling ODMR using the threshold current as the read-out parameter. We report a five-fold enhancement in the ODMR contrast by operating near threshold. The best magnetic field sensitivity of $7.6~\mathrm{nT/\sqrt{Hz}}$ (DC-500 Hz) is achieved well above threshold, while near threshold sensitivity is limited by increased probe laser noise. These results establish a compact and mechanically robust platform for singlet absorption-based NV$^-$ magnetometry and highlight key trade-offs between contrast enhancement and laser noise near threshold.
one_liner: An external-cavity diode laser with NV diamond enables compact singlet-IR absorption magnetometry with threshold-enhanced ODMR contrast.
why_it_matters: It shows a mechanically robust route to NV magnetometers while quantifying the real trade-off between near-threshold contrast gain and excess laser noise.
detailed_summary: The paper integrates a high-NV-density diamond into a compact external-cavity diode laser and uses singlet infrared absorption for ODMR readout. The laser threshold current is stable enough to act as the measurement observable, and operating near threshold boosts ODMR contrast by about 5x. However, the best reported field sensitivity, 7.6 nT/sqrt(Hz) over DC-500 Hz, occurs above threshold because probe-laser noise degrades the near-threshold operating point.
main_result: The authors demonstrate singlet-IR absorption ODMR in a compact NV-diamond laser cavity platform with 5x contrast enhancement near threshold and 7.6 nT/sqrt(Hz) best sensitivity above threshold.
application_angle: This architecture is closer to deployable ambient magnetometers than free-space threshold schemes and clarifies how to balance sensitivity gains against technical laser-noise penalties.

title: Entanglement dynamics of delocalized interacting particles
authors: F. A. B. F. de Moura
abstract: Quantum entanglement in systems of identical particles is often obscured by the interplay between exchange-induced correlations and the operational framework used to define entanglement. To study the role of exchange statistics, we propose a scheme using two \textit{distinguishable} particles where an exchange symmetry is artificially engineered via a relative phase $θ$ in the initial state. This approach allows continuous tuning from bosonic ($θ= 0$) to fermionic ($θ= π$) statistics. By monitoring the interplay between purity and coherence, we uncover distinct dynamical regimes dictated by the interaction strength $U$ and the phase $θ$. For particles initially loaded in a bound state, strong $U$ suppresses coherence development by avoiding the scattering band, reducing the purity toward its minimum. For particles initially on neighboring sites, coherence grows linearly in time. While non-symmetric inputs feature a sharp purity reduction at intermediate $U$, due to the competition between bound and unbound states, symmetric initial conditions produce transient coherence bursts that significantly enhance the purity. More generally, tuning the phase $θ$ reveals a high-purity region over a range of $θ$ at intermediate interactions, with the purity collapsing to $1/2$ as $θ$ approaches the fermionic limit. Our results show that the imposed statistics, or lack thereof, reshapes the entanglement dynamics and its response to the interaction $U$.
one_liner: A tunable exchange-phase model maps entanglement dynamics between bosonic and fermionic limits for interacting delocalized particles.
why_it_matters: It isolates how engineered particle statistics and interaction strength shape coherence and purity, informing controllable quantum simulators and metrology-relevant correlation dynamics.
detailed_summary: The authors study two distinguishable interacting particles prepared with an engineered relative phase theta that continuously interpolates between bosonic and fermionic exchange symmetry. They track purity and coherence for different initial states and interaction strengths, identifying regimes where bound-state physics suppresses coherence or where transient coherence bursts raise purity. At intermediate interactions they find a high-purity region over a finite theta range, while purity collapses toward 1/2 near the fermionic limit.
platform_or_system: two-particle lattice model with tunable exchange phase
main_result: The work shows that engineered exchange symmetry strongly reshapes entanglement dynamics, with intermediate interactions producing distinct purity maxima or collapses depending on symmetry and initial state.
application_angle: The framework could guide experiments that emulate statistics-dependent correlation dynamics in programmable quantum simulators used for sensing-inspired state engineering.

title: Quantum Decoherence of the Surface Code: A Generalized Caldeira-Leggett Approach
abstract: Standard quantum error correction (QEC) models typically assume discrete, Markovian noise, obscuring the continuous quantum nature of physical environments. In this manuscript, we investigate the fundamental limits of an actively corrected surface code coupled to a continuous, un-reset quantum environment at zero and finite temperature. Using the generalized Caldeira-Leggett framework, we map the long-time evolution of the logical qubit to a boundary conformal field theory, establishing an exact equivalence to the anisotropic Kondo model. We evaluate computational times for a finite code distance $L$ for all spatial and temporal correlations. Our analysis reveals that a true thermodynamic threshold exists strictly for short-range environments ($z>1/(s+1)$). In critical or long-range regimes, the macroscopic footprint of the code weaponizes the continuous bath, hindering the topological protection.
one_liner: A generalized Caldeira-Leggett treatment derives exact long-time decoherence limits for an actively corrected surface code in continuous baths.
why_it_matters: It replaces idealized discrete-noise assumptions with a microscopic bath model, exposing when realistic correlated environments destroy topological protection.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes an actively corrected surface code coupled to an unreset continuous quantum environment at zero and finite temperature. By mapping the logical-qubit dynamics to a boundary conformal field theory and then to the anisotropic Kondo model, the authors obtain exact statements about long-time behavior and finite-distance computational times under spatially and temporally correlated noise. They find a true thermodynamic threshold only for short-range environments satisfying z > 1/(s+1), whereas critical or long-range baths cause the code's macroscopic footprint to amplify decoherence.
platform_or_system: surface-code logical qubit in a continuous quantum bath
main_result: The main result is an exact threshold criterion showing that only sufficiently short-range environments permit a genuine thermodynamic error-correction threshold for the surface code.
application_angle: These results directly inform hardware architects about which bath spectra and correlation ranges are compatible with scalable surface-code protection.

title: Integrated Supermode Photonics Enabled by Supersymmetric Transformation
abstract: We report a systematic methodology to obtain supermodes with equidistant effective index distribution and to excite arbitrary target supermodes with high precision. By employing a multi-well optical potential realized by a judiciously designed waveguide array, the supported supermodes achieve maximal spacing and an equidistant distribution in effective index. More importantly, we develop a 2nd-order discrete supersymmetric (DSUSY) transformation method that enables the excitation and detection of two supermodes at the same time and can be extended to any number of supermodes via simple cascading. Together, these findings overcome the long-standing bottlenecks in integrated supermode photonics and provide an intrinsically scalable route towards harnessing supermodes as a new degree of freedom for encoding, transmitting, and processing information. We experimentally demonstrate the feasibility and universality of this method by realizing two- and four-supermode multiplexing systems. Benefitting from the large effective index spacing between supermodes and the isospectral nature of the DSUSY transformation, the fabricated devices show low insertion losses (< 2.48 dB at 1550 nm) and intermodal crosstalk (< -18 dB at 1550 nm) for all mode channels over a 100-nm wavelength range (1500-1600 nm). The high-speed data transmission experiment performed on the four-channel system achieves an aggregate data rate of 1.024 Tb/s while maintaining considerably low bit error rates, underscoring the potential of supermode photonics for high-capacity on-chip optical communications. This work lays the foundation for integrated supermode photonics, which uses supermodes as a new degree of freedom for light manipulation and opens new avenues for supermode-based applications including but not limited to on-chip optical communications, intelligent optical computing and quantum information technologies.
one_liner: Supersymmetric waveguide design enables scalable low-crosstalk supermode multiplexing with terabit-per-second on-chip transmission.
why_it_matters: It establishes supermodes as a practical integrated degree of freedom for dense optical routing, communications, and photonic information processing.
detailed_summary: The authors design multi-well waveguide arrays whose supported supermodes have maximally spaced, nearly equidistant effective indices, then use a second-order discrete supersymmetric transformation to selectively excite and detect target supermodes. They experimentally implement two- and four-supermode multiplexers with insertion loss below 2.48 dB and intermodal crosstalk below -18 dB at 1550 nm across a 1500-1600 nm band. A four-channel device carries an aggregate 1.024 Tb/s data stream with low bit-error rates, validating the platform for broadband high-capacity operation.
main_result: The work demonstrates scalable two- and four-supermode multiplexing with low loss, low crosstalk, broad bandwidth, and 1.024 Tb/s aggregate on-chip data transmission.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to dense photonic interconnects and could extend to supermode-based quantum and neuromorphic photonic processors.

title: On-demand generation of all four Bell states using a single PPKTP entangled photon source
abstract: We present a compact, automated, high-brightness entangled photon source capable of generating all four Bell states with high fidelity. The system utilizes a type-0 quasi-phase-matched PPKTP crystal embedded within a polarization Sagnac interferometer. We introduce a switching scheme based on the controlled, motorized translation of the nonlinear crystal. This device is capable of generating any one of the Bell states on-demand. Experimentally, we demonstrate that translating the crystal from the interferometer's balanced position repeatedly toggles the state between $|φ^+ \rangle$ and $|φ^- \rangle$ (as well as $|ψ^+ \rangle$ and $|ψ^- \rangle$) at regular intervals of $122 \pm 14 ~μm$. Subsequently, a half-wave plate (HWP) in the idler arm transitions between the quantum states $|φ^{\pm}\rangle$ and $|ψ^{\pm}\rangle$. While the non-collinear geometry imposes an upper limit on the translation range as verified via EMCCD imaging, the source however, displays very little change of intensity in the operational window. State purity and entanglement are certified through quantum state tomography (QST), visibility measurements, Bell state measurements (BSM), and CHSH inequality violations, confirming that the source is robust and provides a repeatable, high-fidelity output.
one_liner: A single PPKTP Sagnac source generates any of the four Bell states on demand by crystal translation plus a simple wave-plate switch.
why_it_matters: It offers a compact, automated entangled-photon source with flexible state programmability, reducing source overhead for quantum network and optics experiments.
detailed_summary: This source uses a type-0 quasi-phase-matched PPKTP crystal inside a polarization Sagnac interferometer and switches Bell states by motorized translation of the crystal combined with a half-wave plate in the idler arm. The translation periodically toggles between phi-plus and phi-minus, or psi-plus and psi-minus, with a measured period of 122 +/- 14 um. Quantum state tomography, visibility, Bell-state measurements, and CHSH tests verify repeatable high-fidelity entangled-state generation across the operational range.
main_result: The authors demonstrate on-demand generation of all four Bell states from a single compact source using mechanical crystal translation and polarization control while maintaining high state fidelity.
application_angle: A reconfigurable Bell-pair source is immediately useful for quantum communication, teleportation, and entanglement-swapping setups that need fast switching among resource states.

title: Asymptotic Metrological Scaling and Concentration in Chaotic Floquet Dynamics
abstract: We study quantum sensing with Floquet chaotic dynamics generated by Haar random unitary gates. The metrological resources consist of three ingredients: A given initial state, a set number of Haar random unitary gates and the sensing gates. There are two natural ways of organizing the resources: the first one is the "control" protocol, where the random unitary gates act as random controls and intertwine with the deterministic sensing gates and the second one is the "state-preparation" protocol, where random unitary gates play the role of preparing the metrological useful states. In each protocol, we consider both global Haar random unitary gates and a set of local two-site Haar random unitary gates that forms a Floquet random quantum circuit (RQC) respectively. We find linear, shot-noise scaling of the metrological precision, quantified by the quantum Fisher information (QFI), in the asymptotic limit when the Hilbert space dimension becomes large, and quantum advantages beyond linear scaling in the non-asymptotic regimes. We also bound the fluctuation of the QFI using concentration inequalities. Our analytical findings are corroborated by numerical simulations. Finally, along the way of analyzing the precision limit, we prove an empirical conjecture of RQC: In the asymptotic limit of large local Hilbert space dimension, the Floquet operator of a Floquet RQC essentially behaves like a global unitary operator.
one_liner: Chaotic Floquet circuits yield asymptotically shot-noise metrological scaling, with non-asymptotic quantum advantages and concentration bounds on QFI.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when chaotic dynamics help sensing and when they wash out quantum gains, giving theory benchmarks for random-circuit metrology.
detailed_summary: The paper studies sensing protocols where Haar-random global unitaries or local two-site Floquet random quantum circuits act either as controls interleaved with sensing gates or as state-preparation resources. In the large-Hilbert-space limit, the quantum Fisher information scales linearly, giving shot-noise precision, while finite-size regimes can still exhibit superlinear quantum advantages. The authors also derive concentration inequalities for QFI fluctuations and prove that, at large local dimension, the Floquet operator of a random quantum circuit effectively behaves like a global Haar unitary.
platform_or_system: Floquet random quantum circuits for metrology
main_result: The main result is that chaotic Floquet metrology becomes asymptotically shot-noise limited, though finite-size random-circuit regimes can still provide quantum-enhanced precision.
application_angle: These bounds help determine whether complexity-heavy control in programmable quantum platforms is worthwhile for practical sensing tasks.

title: Wave--particle transition and quantum Zeno effect in which-way experiments with a superconducting quantum processor
abstract: Wave--particle duality demonstrates the peculiar nature of quantum mechanics. In which-way experiments, depending on the measurement scheme, a particle exhibits either wave-like or particle-like properties, as summarized by Bohr's principle of complementarity. In this work, we implement Mach-Zehnder (MZ) interferometry on a two-dimensional (2D) superconducting quantum processor. With precise control of the which-way measurement strength, we demonstrate the transition of a photon from wave-like to particle-like behavior. Furthermore, by performing quantum state tomography on two qubits located in the two paths, we demonstrate that which-way measurements break the entanglement and coherence between the two paths and cause information leakage from the quantum system to the environment. To capture this behavior quantitatively, we derive complementarity relations between the entropy and the fringe visibility. By applying a continuous which-way measurement during the evolution, we also observe the quantum Zeno effect that partially obstructs the interferometer path, giving rise to nonmonotonic behavior of purity and von Neumann entropy. Our experiments provide a detailed characterization of the full interferometer dynamics, reveal the relation between wave--particle duality and quantum information, and demonstrate the potential of superconducting quantum processors for testing quantum foundations under high precision and controllability.
one_liner: A superconducting 2D processor emulates Mach-Zehnder which-way measurements, resolving wave-particle crossover and a measurement-induced quantum Zeno effect.
why_it_matters: It turns a programmable superconducting chip into a precise testbed for measurement backaction, decoherence, and entanglement loss in hardware-relevant quantum dynamics.
detailed_summary: The work implements a Mach-Zehnder interferometer on a two-dimensional superconducting quantum processor and tunes the strength of which-way measurements to drive a continuous transition from interference to path information. Using two-qubit state tomography on the interferometer arms, the authors directly connect reduced fringe visibility to broken coherence, entanglement loss, and information leakage to the environment. They further derive complementarity relations involving entropy and visibility, and observe nonmonotonic purity and von Neumann entropy under continuous monitoring consistent with a partial quantum Zeno obstruction.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates controllable wave-to-particle transition and a continuous-measurement-induced quantum Zeno effect on a superconducting qubit platform.
application_angle: The techniques inform how measurement backaction and decoherence can be characterized and controlled in superconducting processors used for sensing, readout, and fault-tolerant hardware studies.

title: Ultimate sensitivity of multiparameter estimation in quantum sensing with undetected photons
abstract: Quantum sensing with undetected photons is a technique where photons of one wavelength probe a sample, but information is extracted by measuring photons of another wavelength that never interacts with the sample. This has seen significant experimental advances in applications such as spectroscopy, microscopy, and bio-sensing. However, a detailed theoretical analysis using the tools of quantum metrology is currently lacking. Thus it is unclear how far away current schemes are from fundamental limits, and what the optimal measurement strategies are. We apply a multiparameter quantum estimation framework to quantify the error when estimating the unknown transmission and phase shift of a sample. The optimal measurement scheme is shown to require only a single controllable phase shift, easily implementable in existing setups. We also study how to use multipass interactions to maximise information gain. In general the optimum number of passes scales inversely with the log of the transmission of the sample. This work clarifies the metrological power of quantum sensing with undetected photons, and provides guidance for the design of experiments requiring high sensitivity.
one_liner: This paper sets the quantum-metrology limits for estimating phase and transmission in sensing with undetected photons and identifies a simple near-optimal measurement strategy.
why_it_matters: It provides a concrete design rulebook for higher-sensitivity spectroscopy, microscopy, and biosensing schemes that probe with one wavelength and detect with another.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate sensing with undetected photons in a multiparameter quantum-estimation framework to jointly bound errors on sample transmission and phase shift. They show the optimal readout requires only a single tunable phase shift, making the prescription directly compatible with existing induced-coherence style experiments. They also analyze multipass probing and find the optimal number of passes generally scales inversely with the logarithm of the sample transmission, clarifying how to trade loss against information gain.
main_result: The study derives the ultimate sensitivity bounds and an experimentally simple optimal measurement protocol for joint phase and transmission estimation with undetected photons.
application_angle: These results can directly improve practical infrared or otherwise hard-to-detect probing in spectroscopy, microscopy, and bio-imaging while keeping detection in a convenient band.

title: Noise Reduction for Universal Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Computation
abstract: Hybrid continuous-variable--discrete-variable (CV--DV) architectures process quantum information in bosonic modes and qubits, but noise limits their performance. To reduce the noise, existing DV error correction must be complemented by CV noise reduction. Existing CV noise-reduction schemes -- such as GKP-stabilizer codes -- can reduce CV noise, but only for Gaussian gates. Therefore, no current noise-reduction scheme can correct arbitrary CV--DV gates, including non-Gaussian ones. Here, we develop noise reduction for a universal CV--DV gate set, making it applicable to arbitrary CV--DV gates. We do so by introducing an ancilla qubit into a GKP-stabilizer code, allowing us to reduce the standard deviation of Gaussian displacement noise from $σ$ to $\tilde O(σ^2)$. To demonstrate the scheme, we show that it significantly reduces noise and improves fidelity in the preparation of non-Gaussian cat and Fock states.
one_liner: A universal CV-DV noise-reduction scheme extends GKP-style protection to arbitrary hybrid oscillator-qubit gates, including non-Gaussian operations.
why_it_matters: It addresses a central bottleneck in bosonic-qubit architectures by suppressing oscillator displacement noise even during universal hybrid processing.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces an ancilla-qubit-assisted extension of GKP-stabilizer coding for hybrid oscillator-qubit computation, targeting universal CV-DV gate sets rather than only Gaussian operations. The protocol reduces Gaussian displacement-noise standard deviation from σ to approximately order σ squared, enabling protection through arbitrary hybrid gates. Numerical demonstrations show improved fidelities in preparing non-Gaussian cat and Fock states, which are especially sensitive to residual bosonic noise.
main_result: The main result is a universal hybrid error-mitigation scheme that quadratically suppresses oscillator displacement noise and improves non-Gaussian state preparation fidelity.
application_angle: The method is relevant to superconducting-cavity and other bosonic quantum processors seeking scalable logical operations beyond Gaussian-only protected control.

title: Propagation-based classification of linear magnetoelectric response in dielectrics
abstract: We study electromagnetic wave propagation in homogeneous dielectrics endowed with a linear magnetoelectric (ME) response in the geometric-optics regime. Assuming isotropic permittivity and permeability while keeping a generic $3\times 3$ ME matrix $α_{ij}$, we derive the eikonal (Fresnel) eigenvalue problem for the polarization vector and obtain a compact quartic dispersion relation for the normalized phase speed $r=v/v_d$, where $v_d=(μ\varepsilon)^{-1/2}$ is the phase speed of the underlying dielectric. We then classify the propagation effects of $α_{ij}$ by decomposing it into trace, symmetric-traceless, and antisymmetric sectors. We show that (i) the pure-trace sector is propagation-silent at leading geometric-optics order; (ii) the antisymmetric sector yields a factorized quartic and produces two branches with closed-form phase speeds, including regimes where $|v|>v_d$; and (iii) the symmetric-traceless sector encodes the richest directional dependence through algebraic invariants that control the Fresnel wave surface and polarization mixing. Finally, we discuss how the predicted phase-speed shifts can be accessed by phase-sensitive transmission and resonant techniques, and we outline numerical workflows to validate the analytic dispersion and map polarization signatures in bulk and finite geometries.
one_liner: A propagation-based theory classifies how different magnetoelectric tensor sectors alter optical dispersion, phase speed, and polarization in dielectrics.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical framework for identifying measurable magnetoelectric signatures in optical transmission and resonant photonic experiments.
detailed_summary: Assuming isotropic permittivity and permeability with a general 3x3 magnetoelectric tensor, the authors derive the eikonal eigenproblem and a compact quartic dispersion relation for normalized phase speed. They decompose the response into trace, antisymmetric, and symmetric-traceless sectors and show that the trace term is silent at leading order, the antisymmetric term yields closed-form split branches, and the symmetric-traceless term drives the richest angular dependence and polarization mixing. The paper also outlines phase-sensitive transmission, resonance-based validation, and numerical workflows for bulk and finite geometries.
main_result: The work delivers an analytic classification linking each magnetoelectric tensor sector to distinct optical propagation signatures and measurable phase-speed shifts.
application_angle: It can guide photonic material characterization and device design for engineered magnetoelectric media, including resonant components and polarization-sensitive optics.

title: Quantum Homomorphic Encryption: Towards Practical and Private Computation on Untrusted Quantum Hardware
abstract: As quantum computing matures into a practical paradigm, the need for secure and private quantum computation on untrusted hardware becomes increasingly urgent. While classical fully homomorphic encryption has enabled computation over encrypted data in untrusted environments, a fully homomorphic and practically implementable quantum counterpart remains elusive. In this work, we propose a universal quantum homomorphic encryption (QHE) framework developed from the Quantum One-Time Pad (QOTP) scheme. Our approach (QOTPH) maintains information-theoretic security and supports a broad class of quantum operations on encrypted quantum states through a systematic set of homomorphic gate decompositions and key update rules. By leveraging the symmetric structure of QOTP and exploiting the transformation properties of quantum gates under Pauli encryption, we enable non-interactive homomorphic evaluation of arbitrary circuits expressible in the Clifford+T gate set, as well as controlled and parameterized operations relevant to variational quantum algorithms and delegated computation. We provide a formal specification of the proposed encryption model, detail its implementation procedure, and report the results obtained from both simulated environments and real quantum processors. Experimental validation demonstrates the correctness of the homomorphic operations and the preservation of key secrecy under circuit-level noise and real-device constraints. This work takes a step toward bridging the gap between theoretical quantum homomorphic encryption and practical realization on near-term quantum hardware, offering a scalable and symmetric cryptographic primitive for privacy-preserving quantum computation.
one_liner: A QOTP-based quantum homomorphic encryption framework enables non-interactive encrypted evaluation of Clifford+T and relevant controlled or parameterized circuits on real hardware.
why_it_matters: It pushes privacy-preserving delegated quantum computing closer to practice by combining information-theoretic security with hardware-tested execution rules.
detailed_summary: The authors build a universal quantum homomorphic encryption scheme from the quantum one-time pad, specifying gate decompositions and key-update rules that preserve encryption through computation. The framework supports non-interactive evaluation of arbitrary Clifford+T circuits as well as controlled and parameterized gates useful for variational and delegated workloads. Simulations and runs on real quantum processors validate correct homomorphic operation and retained key secrecy under realistic circuit noise and device constraints.
platform_or_system: QOTP-based encrypted circuit execution on quantum processors
main_result: The paper demonstrates a practically oriented homomorphic protocol for encrypted quantum computation that works across a broad universal gate set and on real devices.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to secure cloud quantum computing, where users need to run proprietary data and algorithms on untrusted external hardware.

title: Calibrated electric-field imaging with Rydberg-state fluorescence and Autler-Townes splitting
abstract: We demonstrate a spatially resolved method for imaging millimeter-wave (mmWave) electric fields using Rydberg-state fluorescence in a warm atomic vapor. By utilizing a multi-photon ladder excitation scheme, we leverage a specific decay channel that remains dark in the absence of the mmWave field, resulting in high-contrast imaging with effectively zero background. Absolute calibration of the local electric field is achieved by reconstructing the Autler-Townes splitting of the Rydberg resonance across the imaging volume. To ensure robust field extraction across a wide dynamic range--including regimes where spectral features are not fully resolved--we employ a steady-state analysis based on the Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad (GKSL) master equation. We apply this technique to visualize standing-wave interference patterns within a vapor cell and demonstrate the ability to engineer local field distributions using structured dielectric reflectors. This approach provides a versatile and self-calibrating platform for the diagnostic imaging of high-frequency electromagnetic fields and the characterization of mmWave-optical interfaces.
one_liner: Warm-vapor Rydberg fluorescence imaging with Autler-Townes calibration maps millimeter-wave electric fields with high contrast and absolute field extraction.
why_it_matters: It provides a self-calibrated route to visualizing mmWave fields and interfaces, useful for quantum hardware diagnostics and high-frequency device metrology.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses multiphoton ladder excitation in a warm vapor cell to access a fluorescence channel that is dark without the millimeter-wave drive, yielding near-zero-background electric-field images. Local field amplitudes are absolutely calibrated by reconstructing position-dependent Autler-Townes splitting of the Rydberg resonance. A GKSL master-equation analysis extends field extraction into regimes where spectral features are partially unresolved, and the method is used to image standing waves and engineered distributions created by dielectric reflectors.
main_result: The authors demonstrate spatially resolved, absolutely calibrated millimeter-wave electric-field imaging using Rydberg fluorescence and Autler-Townes splitting.
application_angle: This is useful for noninvasive characterization of mmWave components, cavity and interface diagnostics, and alignment of emerging atom-based quantum and RF hardware.

title: Generating pairwise entanglement in periodically driven quantum spin chains with stochastic resetting
abstract: We show that stochastic resetting may lead to finite entanglement between individual, spatially separated spins (pairwise entanglement) in the steady state of the spin chains driven periodically with frequency $ω_D$. We find the presence of a critical resetting rate $r_c$ below which the steady state pairwise entanglement, measured via concurrence $C$, vanishes. We also identify an optimal resetting rate $r_m$ at which $C$ becomes maximum. These critical and optimal rates exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on $ω_D$. Our analysis demonstrates the existence of special drive frequencies at which $r_c$ vanishes and $r_m$ attains minima. We compute $C$ in the presence of stochastic resetting using exact diagonalization for both the integrable XY model and non-integrable Rydberg spin chains, which demonstrate these features. Our numerical results match perturbative analytical expressions for the special drive frequencies in the large drive amplitude regime.
one_liner: Periodic driving plus stochastic resetting creates steady-state long-range pairwise entanglement in XY and Rydberg spin chains.
why_it_matters: It identifies a controllable nonequilibrium knob for stabilizing useful two-spin entanglement in driven quantum hardware despite otherwise vanishing steady-state concurrence.
detailed_summary: The paper studies periodically driven quantum spin chains subject to stochastic resetting and shows that the steady state can support finite pairwise entanglement between spatially separated spins. It finds a critical reset rate r_c below which concurrence vanishes and an optimal rate r_m that maximizes concurrence, with both rates varying non-monotonically with drive frequency. Exact diagonalization on integrable XY chains and non-integrable Rydberg chains reveals special drive frequencies where r_c goes to zero and r_m is minimized. Perturbative analytics in the large-drive-amplitude regime agree with numerics at those special frequencies.
platform_or_system: Driven XY and Rydberg spin chains
main_result: Stochastic resetting can induce and optimize steady-state pairwise entanglement in periodically driven spin chains, including non-integrable Rydberg models.
application_angle: Reset-engineered entanglement could become a control primitive for near-term simulators and processors that use periodic driving and mid-circuit reinitialization.

title: Directional Scattering-Induced Optical Forces on a Mie Particle near a Metal Interface
abstract: Optical manipulation of Mie-resonant dielectric nanoparticles is strongly influenced by their enhanced scattering and multipolar response, which fundamentally modifiesthe balance of optical forces. In this work, we study the optical forces acting on a resonant dielectric nanoparticle placed near a metal interface, where scattering occurs into both free-space and surface plasmon-polariton (SPP) channels. We show that the interference of electric and magnetic dipole moments leads to highly directional scattering in these channels, and the direction and magnitude of the scattering-induced force are directly linked to the angular directivity of the corresponding radiation channels. We show that in a cross-beam configuration, where the radiation-pressure contribution is suppressed, the optical force can be changed for almost 2π in a wide range of particle sizes that provides a route toward optical sorting of resonant nanoparticles.
one_liner: Directional scattering into free-space and SPP channels lets optical forces on a Mie nanoparticle near metal swing through nearly 2π.
why_it_matters: It offers a route to plasmon-assisted optical sorting and manipulation of resonant nanoparticles by engineering multipolar scattering rather than relying on simple radiation pressure.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze optical forces on resonant dielectric nanoparticles placed near a metal interface, where scattering can couple both to free-space modes and surface plasmon-polaritons. Interference between electric and magnetic dipole moments controls the angular directivity of these channels, and the net scattering-induced force follows that directivity. In a cross-beam configuration that suppresses ordinary radiation pressure, the force direction can be tuned across almost a full 2π over a broad particle-size range. The work connects multipolar scattering physics directly to force engineering at plasmonic interfaces.
platform_or_system: Mie dielectric nanoparticle near a metal interface
main_result: Directional multipolar scattering near a metal surface enables large tunable changes in optical-force direction, including nearly full-angle steering in a cross-beam setup.
application_angle: The mechanism is relevant for chip-scale particle routing, sorting, and plasmonic trapping architectures in nanophotonic manipulation systems.

title: Towards Application of Nanodiamonds for in-situ Monitoring of Radicals in Liquid Phase Chemical Reactions
abstract: In many chemical reactions, short-lived radical intermediates play a crucial role, while detecting such short-lived species in-situ remains challenging. The optically readable electronic spin of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond is a nanoscale sensor for such radical species: its longitudinal spin relaxation time (T$_{1}$) reacts to magnetic fluctuations from the unpaired electrons of radical species in its local environment. In this setting, we demonstrate the successful in-situ detection of the nitroxide radical 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidinyloxyl (TEMPO) using NV center-based T$_1$ relaxometry after depositing nanodiamonds onto the inner wall of a glass cuvette. A significant concentration-dependent shortening of the relaxation time was observed, from $197\:μs \pm 21\:μs$ without radical to $66\:μs \pm 30\:μs$ at a concentration of 1 M TEMPO. The detection is sensitive in the nanomolar (nM) range and the determined signal-to-noise ratio is between 1.6 and 3.
one_liner: NV nanodiamonds on a cuvette wall detect liquid-phase TEMPO radicals in situ through concentration-dependent T1 relaxometry.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical path to embedding quantum sensors into reaction vessels for real-time monitoring of short-lived radical chemistry in liquids.
detailed_summary: This work uses NV centers in deposited nanodiamonds as local magnetic-noise sensors to detect radicals in liquid-phase chemistry via longitudinal spin-relaxation measurements. For TEMPO, the measured T1 drops from 197 ± 21 us without radicals to 66 ± 30 us at 1 M concentration, demonstrating a clear concentration-dependent response. The reported sensitivity extends to the nanomolar range with signal-to-noise ratios between 1.6 and 3. The experiment is implemented with nanodiamonds attached to the inner wall of a glass cuvette, which is important for realistic reaction monitoring geometries.
platform_or_system: NV nanodiamonds in a liquid-phase cuvette
main_result: In-situ NV T1 relaxometry detects TEMPO radicals in liquid solution with concentration-dependent relaxation shortening and nanomolar sensitivity.
application_angle: This could enable quantum-enabled inline diagnostics for catalysis, electrochemistry, and reaction optimization where transient radicals are otherwise hard to monitor.

title: Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing with Trapped Ions: The Walking Cat Architecture
abstract: We propose a fault-tolerant quantum computer architecture for trapped-ion devices, which we call the walking cat architecture. Our blueprint includes a compiler, a detailed description of all the quantum error-correction protocols, a micro-architecture, a sufficiently fast decoder, and thorough simulations. The backbone of the architecture is a cat factory, producing cat states distributed throughout the machine, which are consumed to perform logical operations. The walking cat architecture is based entirely on a modern quantum error-correction approach called low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes. We identify promising instances of the walking cat architecture, such as (1) a simple architecture based on a single LDPC code, (2) a fast architecture based on fast logical gates relying on a [[70, 6, 9]] code, equipped with Clifford-frame tracking for any 6-qubit Clifford gate, and (3) a dense architecture based on a [[102, 22, 9]]] code encoding 22 logical qubits per memory block. Our dense architecture provides a design with 110 logical qubits executing about one million T gates per day using only 2,514 physical qubits. We estimate that the quantum Hamiltonian simulation of a Heisenberg model on 100 sites can be executed within one month with 10,000 physical qubits, including all shots required to achieve chemical accuracy, suggesting that such a device could enter the regime of classically intractable physics simulations. Our design relies on hardware components that have been experimentally demonstrated on small devices. We emphasize simplicity over hypothetical performance to facilitate the practical realization of this machine. Based on this approach, we believe that a fault-tolerant quantum computer with hundreds of logical qubits capable of running millions of logical gates can be built in the near term, providing a platform to explore a broad range of applications.
one_liner: A trapped-ion fault-tolerant architecture based on LDPC codes and distributed cat states targets hundreds of logical qubits with near-term hardware.
why_it_matters: It turns fault tolerance into a concrete trapped-ion systems blueprint with compiler, decoder, microarchitecture, and resource estimates tied to application workloads.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes the walking cat architecture, a full-stack trapped-ion design for fault-tolerant quantum computing that uses a cat-state factory and LDPC-based error correction throughout the machine. It specifies logical protocols, a microarchitecture, compilation flow, and a sufficiently fast decoder, then evaluates several architectural points including simple, fast, and dense variants. The dense design encodes 110 logical qubits using 2,514 physical qubits and is estimated to execute about one million T gates per day. The authors further estimate that a 10,000-physical-qubit machine could perform 100-site Heisenberg-model Hamiltonian simulation within about one month including the shots needed for chemical accuracy.
main_result: The architecture provides a realistic trapped-ion path to hundreds of logical qubits and million-scale logical-gate workloads using experimentally demonstrated components.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to hardware roadmaps because it translates trapped-ion capabilities into executable fault-tolerant workloads with explicit resource and performance targets.

title: Quantum mechanics over real numbers fully reproduces standard quantum theory
abstract: Standard quantum mechanics employs complex Hilbert spaces, but whether complex numbers are fundamental or merely convenient has long been debated. For decades, real-valued equivalents were considered mathematically possible but cumbersome. However, a landmark 2021 result claimed that any quantum theory based on real numbers is experimentally falsifiable via network Bell experiments. Yet, it remains an open question whether this falsification applies to all real-valued theories. Here we show that this conclusion rests on an incomplete real formulation, and we present a rigorous real-valued framework that perfectly reproduces all predictions of standard quantum mechanics, i.e. standard quantum mechanics. We demonstrate that the standard real tensor product ($\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}$) used in previous no-go theorems is algebraically incompatible with the rich structure of standard quantum mechanics. We present a real framework based on \ka space and prove that it is exactly isomorphic to standard quantum mechanics via an explicit bijection $γ$. The isomorphism extends to composite systems through a symplectic composition rule $\otimes^{\ks}$ that replaces the Kronecker product. Consequently, our formulation achieves the maximal $\mathrm{CHSH}_{3}$ violation of $6\sqrt{2}$ using purely real variables, directly contradicting previous falsification claims. These results demonstrate that complex numbers are not fundamentally required by nature; rather, they encode a deeper real geometric structure that governs quantum interference and entanglement, settling this long debate.
one_liner: A real-number formulation with a modified composition rule is claimed to reproduce all standard quantum predictions exactly, including Bell-network behavior.
why_it_matters: If correct, it reframes the mathematical foundations behind entanglement and interference and challenges prior claims that complex amplitudes are experimentally indispensable.
detailed_summary: The authors construct a real-valued reformulation of quantum mechanics and argue that previous no-go results relied on an incomplete choice of tensor product structure. Their framework replaces the standard real Kronecker product with a symplectic composition rule and proves an explicit isomorphism to standard complex quantum mechanics. Within this formalism they claim to reproduce all quantum predictions exactly, including maximal CHSH_3 violation of 6sqrt(2) using purely real variables. The result is foundational rather than device-specific, but it bears on how multipartite photonic Bell experiments are interpreted.
main_result: The paper claims an explicitly real formulation of quantum mechanics that is fully prediction-equivalent to standard complex quantum theory for composite systems.
application_angle: For quantum communication and photonic nonlocality experiments, it could change the theoretical interpretation of what those platforms actually certify about the underlying formalism.

title: Electrically-injected room-temperature waveguide polariton laser
abstract: Exciton-polariton lasers are coherent light sources which do not require the population inversion (transparency) condition to be fulfilled. They have been conceptualized at the end of the XXth century but until now they operate almost exclusively under optical injection, which severely limits the widespread integration of the polariton-based devices implemented so far. Here we tackle this issue by reporting an electrically-pumped exciton-polariton laser based on GaN and operating at room temperature in a mode-locked regime. The laser architecture is close to the geometry of commercial ridge-waveguide GaN lasers, but based on a bulk GaN active region instead of quantum wells. Unique features of polariton lasers are demonstrated, in particular the breakdown of the transparency condition, which enables our polariton lasers to operate even when only a small fraction (20\%) of the cavity length is injected. Moreover, the large polaritonic gain allows for the operation of a short cavity length (60$μm$) compared to commercial lasers. From the very same sample, we also achieve polariton lasing under optical injection, confirming that the doped layers necessary for electrical injection do not prevent strong-coupling nor polariton lasing. Our results open a new perspective for polariton-based devices.
one_liner: A GaN ridge-waveguide device delivers electrically injected room-temperature polariton lasing in a mode-locked regime.
why_it_matters: It removes a major integration barrier for polariton devices by demonstrating electrically pumped operation in a geometry close to commercial GaN lasers.
detailed_summary: This paper reports an electrically pumped exciton-polariton laser based on bulk GaN operating at room temperature in a ridge-waveguide architecture. The device shows hallmark polariton behavior, including operation without satisfying the usual transparency condition and lasing when only about 20% of the cavity length is injected. The large polaritonic gain supports a short 60 um cavity, and the same sample also exhibits optically pumped polariton lasing, confirming strong coupling survives the doped layers needed for electrical injection. The design closely tracks commercial GaN laser geometries, improving its practical relevance.
platform_or_system: Electrically injected GaN waveguide polariton laser
main_result: The authors demonstrate room-temperature, electrically injected polariton lasing in a GaN waveguide device, including mode-locked operation and sub-transparency behavior.
application_angle: This is a concrete step toward integrated low-threshold coherent light sources and polaritonic photonic circuits compatible with scalable semiconductor fabrication.

title: Cross Waveguide Design for Color-Centers in Diamond for Photonic Quantum Computing
abstract: Color centers in diamond are a promising platform for quantum computing applications because of their optical and spin properties.
