However, diamond presents some technological challenges that limit its use in complex or large photonic circuits. To mitigate these limitations, it is technically effective to separate the smallest possible diamond photonic structures or chiplet containing the color center(s) from the rest of the circuit, which is fabricated on another material platform, and then heterogeneously integrate them. Considering efficient excitation and photon collection from waveguide-coupled color centers, we design a cross waveguide as the primary component of our chiplet to access the color centers, channeling excitation and emitted photons into different waveguides, and connecting the structure to the other components of the photonic circuit. The chiplet containing the cross waveguide and supporting structures requires careful optimization of each subcomponent. The receptor's design is also critical for optimal signal transmission. In this paper, we develop a simple but efficient methodology to optimize the main components constituting both the chiplet and the receptor for their synergistic operation. The designed structure has an excitation-to-emission conversion of more than 5.4%, crosstalk of less than -40 dB, a working bandwidth of 160 nm, fabrication feasibility and tolerance within the limits of modern nanofabrication, and a mechanical solid structure with a footprint of less than 2000 $μ$m
one_liner: A diamond chiplet cross-waveguide design enables efficient color-center excitation and collection for heterogeneous photonic quantum circuits.
why_it_matters: It tackles a core integration bottleneck for diamond color-center photonics by combining usable efficiency, low crosstalk, broad bandwidth, and fabrication tolerance.
detailed_summary: The paper designs a cross-waveguide chiplet that spatially separates excitation and emission paths for diamond color centers while interfacing to a larger non-diamond photonic circuit. The authors jointly optimize the chiplet subcomponents and the receptor structure for system-level performance rather than isolated element metrics. The resulting design reaches more than 5.4% excitation-to-emission conversion, less than -40 dB crosstalk, and a 160 nm operating bandwidth. It also targets realistic nanofabrication tolerances and a mechanically robust footprint below 2000 um^2.
main_result: They present an optimized cross-waveguide/receptor architecture for diamond color centers with >5.4% conversion, <-40 dB crosstalk, and 160 nm bandwidth.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to scalable photonic quantum computing because it supports modular diamond emitters integrated into larger heterogeneous photonic chips.

title: Efficient optimisation of multi-parameter quantum control protocols for strongly-coupled systems
abstract: Achieving high-fidelity control in the presence of strong non-Markovian noise is critical for the optimization of emergent solid-state quantum devices. We present a highly efficient optimization framework that combines automatic differentiation with the non-Markovian uniTEMPO algorithm, enabling direct gradient-based optimization of complex objective functions. We apply this method to semiconductor quantum dots, optimizing multi-pulse excitation schemes: specifically Swing-UP of a Quantum EmmiteR (SUPER) and Floquet-engineered Two-Photon Excitation (FTPE) for single- and bi-exciton generation. Our approach yields high preparation fidelities within experimentally accessible parameter regimes. By integrating adiabatic rapid passage (ARP), we systematically enhance both SUPER and FTPE, demonstrating that these optimized protocols consistently outperform standard resonant pi-pulses and two-photon excitation. Notably, this performance gap widens at elevated temperatures, establishing the superior thermal robustness of our optimized multi-pulse strategies for real-world quantum hardware.
one_liner: An autodiff plus uniTEMPO framework efficiently optimizes robust multi-pulse quantum-dot control under strong non-Markovian noise.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical route to higher-fidelity and more temperature-robust control protocols for semiconductor emitters used in real quantum photonic hardware.
detailed_summary: The work combines automatic differentiation with the non-Markovian uniTEMPO solver to enable direct gradient optimization of complicated control objectives in strongly coupled open systems. It is applied to semiconductor quantum dots for Swing-UP of a Quantum Emitter and Floquet-engineered two-photon excitation, targeting single- and bi-exciton preparation. The authors further incorporate adiabatic rapid passage to improve both protocols. Across experimentally accessible regimes, the optimized multi-pulse strategies outperform standard resonant pi-pulses and conventional two-photon excitation, especially at elevated temperatures.
main_result: The method produces experimentally realistic multi-pulse control protocols that achieve higher state-preparation fidelity and better thermal robustness than standard excitation schemes.
application_angle: Better control of exciton and biexciton generation can improve deterministic single-photon and entangled-photon sources for quantum photonics.

title: Impact of Photoelectric Readout Noise on Magnetic Field Sensitivity of NV Centers in Diamond
abstract: Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond are of great interest for nano- and macro-scale magnetic field sensing. Most sensing protocols rely on conventional optical readout, which is limited by photon shot noise. The recently developed photoelectrical (PE) readout of the NV center electron spin state promises to overcome these limitations. However, the noise of the PE readout and its influence on readout efficiency have not been thoroughly studied. In this work, we perform magnetic field sensing and estimate the sensitivity using optical and PE readout with a single and an ensemble of NV centers in diamond. We investigate the electronic noise associated with the photoelectric detection and estimate the readout efficiency, using Gaussian statistics. Our quantitative analysis shows that the Johnson-Nyquist noise-limited photoelectric magnetic field sensitivity could outperform optical measurements by an order of magnitude. This work is an essential step towards the development of on-chip magnetometers using photoelectrical detection in diamond.
one_liner: This study quantifies photoelectric readout noise in NV-diamond magnetometry and shows a path to order-of-magnitude sensitivity gains.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete noise model and performance comparison showing photoelectric readout could materially outperform optical NV sensing in on-chip devices.
detailed_summary: The authors compare optical and photoelectric readout for magnetic sensing using both single NV centers and NV ensembles in diamond. They characterize the electronic noise in photoelectric detection, model readout efficiency with Gaussian statistics, and connect the readout noise budget to achievable magnetic sensitivity. Their analysis indicates that if limited by Johnson-Nyquist noise, photoelectric readout can surpass optical sensitivity by roughly an order of magnitude. The work establishes the key noise considerations needed to engineer practical photoelectrically read out NV sensors.
main_result: They show that Johnson-Nyquist-noise-limited photoelectric readout could enable NV magnetic-field sensitivity about an order of magnitude better than optical readout.
application_angle: The result supports compact on-chip diamond magnetometers with improved sensitivity and potentially simpler integration than photon-counting-based readout.

title: Toward designing workload-aware Surface Code Architectures
abstract: Practical quantum advantage is expected to depend on fault-tolerant quantum computing, although the architectural overhead needed to support fault tolerance is still extremely high. Prior FTQC designs generally emphasize either fast logical-qubit accessibility at the cost of significant qubit overhead, or high logical-qubit density at the cost of added workload latency. We propose an architecture that balances these competing objectives by placing surface-code patches around an ancilla-centric region, which yields nearly uniform ancilla access for all data qubits. Building on this design, we introduce a new workload-driven placement method that uses the $T$-gate profile of an application to determine an effective floorplan. We further provide a reconfigurable optimization for reducing the latency of $Y$-gate measurements on a per-workload basis. To improve flexibility, we also study concurrent execution of multiple programs on the same architecture. Numerical evaluation indicates that our approach keeps cycles per instruction near the optimal regime while reducing the number of required data tiles by up to $\sim21\%$, and achieves up to $\sim90\%$ efficiency when running 10 programs concurrently.
one_liner: A workload-aware surface-code architecture balances qubit density and access latency using ancilla-centric floorplanning and application-specific placement.
why_it_matters: It shows that fault-tolerant layouts can be co-optimized with workload structure to cut tile overhead and preserve near-optimal execution efficiency.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a surface-code layout that places data patches around an ancilla-centric region to equalize ancilla access and reduce architectural imbalance. On top of that, it introduces a placement method driven by the T-gate profile of a workload, plus a per-workload reconfiguration for faster Y-gate measurements. The authors also analyze concurrent execution of multiple programs on the same machine. Simulations indicate cycles per instruction near the optimal regime while reducing required data tiles by up to about 21%, with up to about 90% efficiency for 10 concurrent programs.
main_result: The architecture and placement strategy reduce data-tile count by up to ~21% while maintaining near-optimal CPI and reaching ~90% efficiency for 10-way concurrency.
application_angle: This improves the practicality of early fault-tolerant quantum processors by lowering hardware overhead and increasing throughput for real application mixes.

title: Spin Kerr-cat qubits
abstract: The use of noise-robust qubit encodings provides a way of extending the lifetime of quantum information at the hardware level. In this work, we introduce the spin Kerr-cat encoding, which leverages a clock transition in the spectrum of quadrupolar nuclei (having spin length $I\geq 1$) to achieve a first-order suppression of noise leading to qubit dephasing. The basis states of the spin Kerr-cat qubit are given by the two lowest levels of a $\mathbb{Z}_2$-symmetric nuclear-spin Hamiltonian and are well approximated by spin cat states. We compute the dephasing time of the spin Kerr-cat qubit under a model of $1/f$ noise, as well as relaxation of the qubit due to breaking of the $\mathbb{Z}_2$ symmetry by charge-noise-induced fluctuations of the quadrupolar tensor. Using measured parameters for antimony (${}^{123}\mathrm{Sb}$) donors in silicon, we estimate that a coherence time of $T_2^*=100$ s could be achieved with this encoding. We propose a two-qubit gate mediated by hopping electrons and estimate that with an enhancement of measured quadrupolar splittings by a factor of $\approx 4$, a gate fidelity of $99\%$ could be achieved for spin Kerr-cat qubits encoded in ${}^{123}\mathrm{Sb}$ nuclear spins, neglecting errors that impact the electron while it is being shuttled and read out.
one_liner: Spin Kerr-cat qubits encode information in quadrupolar nuclear spins to strongly suppress dephasing at the hardware level.
why_it_matters: It points to an unusually long-lived solid-state qubit encoding with plausible silicon-donor parameters and a path to high-fidelity two-qubit gates.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a spin Kerr-cat encoding based on the two lowest states of a Z2-symmetric Hamiltonian for quadrupolar nuclei with spin I >= 1. The encoding exploits a clock transition to suppress first-order dephasing, and the logical basis is well approximated by spin cat states. Using a 1/f noise model and measured parameters for 123Sb donors in silicon, they estimate T2* around 100 s. They also propose an electron-mediated two-qubit gate and project 99% fidelity if quadrupolar splitting is increased by about a factor of four, excluding electron shuttling and readout errors.
main_result: The work predicts spin Kerr-cat qubits with T2* ~100 s and outlines a two-qubit gate that could reach 99% fidelity under improved quadrupolar splitting.
application_angle: If realized, this encoding could reduce error-correction pressure by extending coherence directly in silicon-compatible quantum hardware.

title: Architecting Early Fault Tolerant Neutral Atoms Systems with Quantum Advantage
abstract: Recent advancements in neutral atom platforms have enabled exploration of early fault-tolerant (FT) architectures for applications with quantum advantage, such as quantum dynamics simulations. An efficient fault-tolerant architecture has both spatially efficient quantum error correction codes (low qubit overhead), and efficient methodologies (transversal based gates, extractor based gates, etc.) for logical computation, to minimize overall execution time. Achieving the right balance between space and time can be critical for enabling early FT demonstrations of quantum advantage. In this work, we identify bottlenecks in existing spatially efficient schemes, which tend to be very serial, and do not take advantage of unutilized space. We introduce a teleportation-based scheme that leverages the reconfigurable connectivity of neutral atoms to parallelize logical operations. Our approach achieves up to \textbf{$\mathbf{\sim 3 \times}$ speedup} over extractor architectures at no extra space cost and achieves the best spacetime performance among other viable architectures before accounting for external \textit{resource-states}. To rigorously evaluate performance, we construct explicit quantum advantage benchmarks and \textit{simulate} compilation to a fault-tolerant instruction set, including low-level gate scheduling and shuttling patterns, and resource-state nondeterminism. We find that our speedups still apply and report exact space-time cost along with success probabilities, identifying architectures capable of achieving quantum advantage \textbf{with as little as $\mathbf{11,495}$ atoms and a runtime of $\mathbf{\sim 15}$ hours}.
one_liner: A teleportation-based neutral-atom FT architecture uses reconfigurable connectivity to speed logical computation without extra space cost.
why_it_matters: It identifies a realistic route to early fault-tolerant quantum advantage on neutral atoms with explicit compiled benchmarks and hardware-aware scheduling.
detailed_summary: The paper studies early fault-tolerant neutral-atom architectures and argues that existing spatially efficient schemes underuse available space because they serialize too much logical work. It introduces a teleportation-based approach that exploits atom reconfigurability to parallelize operations while keeping space overhead fixed. The evaluation includes compiled quantum-advantage benchmarks, low-level scheduling, atom shuttling patterns, and resource-state nondeterminism. The proposed architecture achieves up to about 3x speedup over extractor-based designs and can reach quantum advantage with as few as 11,495 atoms in roughly 15 hours.
main_result: They demonstrate a neutral-atom FT architecture with up to ~3x better spacetime performance and benchmarked quantum-advantage operation at 11,495 atoms and ~15 hours runtime.
application_angle: The scheme is practically relevant for first-generation fault-tolerant neutral-atom systems because it improves execution time without demanding more physical qubits.

title: Co-Designing Error Mitigation and Error Detection for Logical Qubits
abstract: Near-term quantum workloads demand error management, yet the two lightest-weight techniques, Quantum Error Detection (QED) and Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC), have complementary cost profiles whose joint architectural design space remains unexplored. QED encodes logical qubits and discards error-flagged runs, filtering noise with low qubit overhead but leaving residual errors; PEC can correct these in software, but at exponential cost in noise strength. If QED efficiently reduces per-gate noise, PEC's cost savings can outweigh QED's discard overhead; realizing this, however, requires solving two system-level design challenges. First, the \textit{QED interval} -- how often detection cycles are inserted -- is a tunable architectural parameter governing the cost-accuracy tradeoff. We derive an efficiency condition and show that the canonical one-cycle-per-gate frequency does not achieve break-even in any code we evaluate, while optimized intervals on high-rate Iceberg codes do. Second, we discover that naive PEC+QED integration \textit{degrades} accuracy below the QED-only baseline. The root cause is a transient error profile in the first detection cycle that corrupts PEC's noise model. We develop \textit{steady-state extraction}, a co-designed characterization protocol that isolates steady-state error behavior, reducing estimation bias by up to $10.2\times$. On a $[[6,4,2]]$ Iceberg code running QAOA ($p{=}4$--$8$) with a fixed shot budget, PEC+QED achieves $2$--$11\times$ lower absolute error and up to $31\times$ lower MSE versus PEC on physical qubits, with per-interval savings compounding over interval depth.
one_liner: A co-designed QED+PEC workflow cuts logical-qubit error more efficiently by optimizing detection intervals and calibrating steady-state noise.
why_it_matters: It shows lightweight logical encoding and software mitigation can be combined practically, improving accuracy at fixed shot budget on near-term hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper studies joint use of quantum error detection and probabilistic error cancellation for logical qubits, treating the QED interval as a system-level optimization parameter rather than inserting detection after every gate. It derives an efficiency condition showing canonical per-gate detection is not cost-effective for the evaluated codes, while optimized intervals on high-rate Iceberg codes can cross break-even. The authors also identify a failure mode where naive PEC+QED worsens accuracy because the first detection cycle has a transient error profile inconsistent with the PEC noise model. Their steady-state extraction protocol removes this bias and improves characterization accuracy by up to 10.2x.
main_result: On QAOA with p=4-8 and fixed shots, optimized PEC+QED achieves 2-11x lower absolute error and up to 31x lower MSE than PEC on physical qubits.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to near-term logical accelerators because it reduces algorithmic error without the full overhead of fault tolerance.

title: Error-correcting transition pulses for co-located spin ensembles without frequency selectivity
abstract: We present a new class of control pulses designed to transfer co-located ensembles without relying on frequency selectivity, thereby allowing much faster state-transitions. A geometric approach allows us to construct sequences which are robust to changes in the background magnetic field along multiple axes, and errors in the pulse area. \red{These pulses are extremely fast, with robustness to pulse area shown at half the quantum speed limit.} We demonstrate these sequences on nuclear-dipole states, showing milliradian precision over several hours, 30-fold beyond the previous state of the art. This provides a path for extending the coherent integration time of ultra-long-lived nuclear-spin states to the fundamental limit set by their $>$10000 second lifetimes, as the limiting self-interactions of the nuclei are suppressed in the symmetric superposition. The state-preparation quality demonstrated here directly opens up 30-fold improvements in next generation tests of the standard model, especially tests of the symmetries of QCD and searches for dark matter; it is also crucial for the development of nuclear-spin based quantum memories and may be useful in other scenarios demanding extremely fast but robust transitions.
one_liner: Geometrically designed ultrafast pulses transfer co-located spin ensembles robustly without frequency selectivity.
why_it_matters: Fast, robust ensemble control can extend coherent integration toward very long nuclear-spin lifetimes for precision sensing and memory applications.
detailed_summary: The work introduces transition-pulse sequences that drive co-located ensembles without relying on spectral selectivity, enabling substantially faster state transfer. Using a geometric construction, the pulses are made robust to magnetic-field variations along multiple axes and to pulse-area errors, with pulse-area robustness demonstrated at half the quantum speed limit. Experiments on nuclear-dipole states show milliradian preparation precision maintained over several hours. This suppresses self-interaction limits in symmetric superpositions and supports much longer coherent integration times.
main_result: The authors demonstrate ultrafast, error-correcting state-transfer pulses with milliradian precision over hours, about 30x beyond prior state of the art.
application_angle: This directly benefits long-coherence quantum sensors, symmetry tests, dark-matter searches, and nuclear-spin quantum memories that need fast but highly robust control.

title: Program gain and loss for broadband soliton microcombs
abstract: Soliton microcombs provide compact, broadband, coherent light sources for precision metrology, spectroscopy, communications, and microwave photonics. Extending their spectral span while retaining useful output power remains challenging and often requires impractically high pump power. Existing approaches mainly tailor the dispersion and pumping conditions, but they do not exploit the coupling spectrum as a programmable aspect of soliton operation. Here we introduce a meta-coupler whose lithographically programmed coupling spectrum concentrates strong pump access near the pumped resonance while leaving most comb lines close to the intrinsic loss rate. Si$_3$N$_4$ microresonators incorporating a meta-coupler exhibit broader circulating soliton spectra, nearly twofold larger 3 dB soliton bandwidths, up to about 12 dB higher central comb-line power, and up to about fivefold greater emitted comb power, without an additional pump-power penalty. Our work unlocks gain and loss as simultaneous programmable knobs for realizing high-performance soliton microcombs.
one_liner: A lithographically programmed meta-coupler boosts soliton microcomb bandwidth and output power without extra pump power.
why_it_matters: Programmable coupling adds a practical new design knob for compact high-power broadband comb sources used in metrology and communications.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a meta-coupler that shapes the coupling spectrum so pump access is strong near the driven resonance while most generated comb lines remain close to the intrinsic loss rate. In Si3N4 resonators, this selective gain-loss engineering broadens the circulating soliton spectrum and improves extraction of useful comb power. Measured devices show nearly 2x larger 3 dB bandwidths, up to about 12 dB higher central-line power, and up to about 5x greater emitted comb power. The key advance is treating coupling, not just dispersion and pump conditions, as a programmable soliton design parameter.
main_result: Meta-coupler-integrated resonators produce broader soliton spectra and significantly higher comb output power with no added pump-power penalty.
application_angle: The result supports deployable chip-scale comb engines for spectroscopy, telecom, microwave photonics, and precision timing where power-bandwidth tradeoffs are critical.

title: The influence of evanescent waves on the nature of optical cooperative effects in atomic ensembles in a waveguide
abstract: Based on a consistent quantum microscopic approach, we investigate the peculiarities of collective polyatomic effects in atomic ensembles placed in a waveguide, caused by the presence of evanescent modes of electromagnetic field. We analyze the influence of these modes on the process of cooperative spontaneous decay, as well as on the nature of radiation transfer in the ensembles under consideration. We show that under certain conditions, their influence can be dominant compared to the role of radiation modes, and the mechanism for this influence is the modification of dipole-dipole interatomic interaction.
one_liner: A microscopic theory shows evanescent modes can dominate cooperative decay and transport in waveguide-coupled atomic ensembles.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when near-field guided interactions, rather than radiative modes, set collective optical behavior in integrated atom-photon interfaces.
detailed_summary: Using a consistent quantum microscopic treatment, the paper analyzes collective optical effects for atoms placed near or in a waveguide, explicitly including evanescent field modes. It studies how these modes alter cooperative spontaneous emission and radiation transport through modified interatomic dipole-dipole coupling. The calculations show regimes where evanescent contributions can exceed those of propagating radiation modes. This identifies a distinct interaction mechanism that can reshape superradiant or transport behavior in guided geometries.
main_result: The study shows that evanescent-field-induced dipole-dipole interactions can become the dominant driver of cooperative decay and light transport in atomic waveguides.
application_angle: These insights matter for designing nanophotonic quantum interfaces, where collective emission and transport must be engineered in tightly confined guided modes.

title: Fractional-Time Jaynes-Cummings Model: Unitary Description of its Quantum Dynamics, Inverse Problem and Photon Statistics
abstract: We analyze the quantum dynamics of the fractional-time Jaynes-Cummings model using a recent unitary framework for the fractional-time Schrödinger equation. We examine how the fractional derivative order $α$ influences non-classical features under different initial conditions. For an initial Fock state, fractional evolution introduces transient dynamics and heightened sensitivity to coupling strength. Through an inverse problem approach, we interpret these effects as arising from an effective time-dependent coupling with a strong initial pulse. For an initial coherent state, the fractional order tunes the system between dynamical regimes, with a transition at $α= 0.50 $ where standard collapse-and-revival is replaced by stable, periodic evolution. This regime enhances non-classical field properties, including stronger sub-Poissonian statistics, periodic quadrature squeezing, and the formation of Schrödinger cat states.
one_liner: A unitary fractional-time Jaynes-Cummings model predicts tunable nonclassical dynamics, including periodic squeezing and cat-state formation.
why_it_matters: It offers a controllable theoretical route to engineer effective light-matter dynamics and photon statistics beyond standard Jaynes-Cummings behavior.
detailed_summary: The paper applies a unitary framework for the fractional-time Schrodinger equation to the Jaynes-Cummings model and tracks how the fractional order alpha reshapes quantum dynamics. For initial Fock states, the fractional evolution behaves like an effective time-dependent coupling with a pronounced initial pulse, explaining transient features through an inverse-problem analysis. For initial coherent states, varying alpha induces a regime change near alpha=0.50 where standard collapse and revival gives way to stable periodic evolution. In that regime, the field shows enhanced sub-Poissonian statistics, periodic quadrature squeezing, and Schrödinger-cat-state generation.
main_result: The model predicts that tuning the fractional derivative order can switch the system into a stable periodic nonclassical regime with stronger squeezing and cat-state signatures.
application_angle: Although theoretical, the framework is relevant to engineered photonic and cavity-QED platforms seeking tailored effective couplings and programmable nonclassical-state generation.

title: Topological Polarization Beam Splitter with Polarization-Selective Edge States
abstract: The realization of on-chip polarization beam splitters robust to fabrication imperfections remains a key challenge for polarization-sensitive photonic integration. We demonstrate a topologically protected polarization beam splitter based on a Floquet-engineered microring lattice implemented on a CMOS-compatible silicon nitride platform. By tailoring the dispersion of inter-ring coupling, the lattice supports complementary trivial and topological band gaps for orthogonal eigenpolarizations. At telecom wavelengths, TE modes propagate via a topological edge state while TM modes are suppressed by a trivial gap; this behavior reverses at shorter wavelengths. We measure extinction ratios of 16-20 dB for the protected port and 10-20 dB for the non-protected port, with insertion loss of 2 dB at long wavelengths. Reduced TM extinction at shorter wavelengths is attributed to suboptimal input coupling. We further identify spectral regions where both polarizations exhibit nontrivial band gaps, enabling polarization-independent edge transport and establishing a Floquet dual-polarization topological regime. Because operation is governed by band topology rather than geometric fine-tuning, the device shows intrinsic robustness to defects. These results establish polarization-tailored topological lattices as a scalable platform for robust beam splitting, routing, and interconnects in classical and quantum photonic systems.
one_liner: A Floquet topological silicon-nitride microring lattice acts as a robust on-chip polarization beam splitter.
why_it_matters: Topology-based polarization routing could make integrated photonic interconnects less sensitive to fabrication errors in classical and quantum systems.
detailed_summary: The device is a CMOS-compatible SiN microring lattice with Floquet-engineered coupling dispersion that creates complementary trivial and topological band gaps for orthogonal polarizations. At telecom wavelengths, TE light is guided by a topological edge state while TM is blocked by a trivial gap; at shorter wavelengths the roles reverse. Experiments measure 16-20 dB extinction at the protected port, 10-20 dB at the non-protected port, and about 2 dB insertion loss at long wavelengths. The authors also identify a dual-polarization topological regime where both polarizations can propagate via nontrivial edge transport.
main_result: The team demonstrates a topologically protected polarization beam splitter with strong extinction and low insertion loss on a silicon-nitride integrated platform.
application_angle: This is directly useful for robust on-chip routing, beam splitting, and interconnects in polarization-sensitive classical and quantum photonic circuits.

title: 6.2-GW tabletop attosecond light source
abstract: The generation of attosecond pulses (1 as=10-18 s) has enabled real-time observation and manipulation of coherent electron dynamics, yet their low peak power has hindered the development of advanced attosecond pump-probe spectroscopy and attosecond nonlinear metrology. Here we overcome this limitation by generating 1.64 uJ, 263 as isolated attosecond pulses with a peak power of 6.2 GW, the highest pulse energy and peak power reported for a tabletop isolated attosecond source. This is achieved by combining a 2.1 TW, few-cycle (8.3 fs) two-color synthesizer with a loose focusing geometry that enables macroscopic phase-matching. The synthesizer features a stabilized carrier-envelope phase and an actively synchronized relative time delay between the two-color channels, ensuring high stability and reproducibility. This robust tabletop attosecond source enables nonlinear effect experiments that were previously inaccessible with lower-power IAPs, establishing a foundation for advanced attosecond spectroscopy and nonlinear metrology.
one_liner: A tabletop two-color driver produces isolated attosecond pulses at 1.64 uJ and 6.2 GW peak power.
why_it_matters: It pushes attosecond sources into a regime suitable for nonlinear XUV experiments and stronger ultrafast spectroscopy signals.
detailed_summary: The work reports isolated attosecond pulse generation with 263 as duration, 1.64 uJ pulse energy, and 6.2 GW peak power, claimed as the highest for a tabletop isolated attosecond source. The source uses a 2.1 TW, 8.3 fs two-color synthesizer with stabilized carrier-envelope phase and actively synchronized inter-channel delay. A loose-focusing geometry is used to achieve favorable macroscopic phase matching while maintaining stability and reproducibility. This combination opens access to pump-probe and nonlinear attosecond measurements that were previously power-limited.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a stable tabletop isolated attosecond source delivering 263 as pulses with 1.64 uJ energy and 6.2 GW peak power.
application_angle: The source is directly relevant for advanced attosecond spectroscopy, nonlinear XUV metrology, and strong-field ultrafast probing of electron dynamics.

title: Universality cost of non-Gaussian enhancement in continuous-variable quantum teleportation: A fidelity--deviation trade-off
abstract: Continuous-variable (CV) quantum teleportation is usually benchmarked by average fidelity, but when the teleportation is repeatedly used within optical networks or measurement-based architectures, uniformity across the input ensemble becomes equally important. We analyze this issue using two complementary figures of merit: the average fidelity and the fidelity deviation, which quantifies the input dependence of the single-shot teleportation fidelity. We prove that any deterministic unity-gain teleportation channel that is displacement covariant has vanishing fidelity deviation for coherent-state benchmarking, irrespective of whether the shared entangled resource is Gaussian or non-Gaussian. Nonzero deviation therefore diagnoses covariance breaking rather than non-Gaussianity. We then show that when a protocol raises the average fidelity through input-selective conditioning, the deviation generically increases in tandem, giving a quantitative universality cost. As a concrete example, we study teleportation enhanced by the so-called measurement-based noiseless linear amplification, where a heralded filter acts on the Bell-measurement record. The resulting trade-off among average fidelity, fidelity deviation, and success probability shows that stronger filtering can improve the conditional fidelity only by concentrating the successful events in favored regions of phase space, thereby suppressing the success probability and reducing input uniformity. Our results provide an operational framework for distinguishing genuine channel improvement from selectivity-driven post-selected advantage and suggest that the probabilistic CV teleportation should be assessed with average quality, universality, and heralding rate treated on an equal footing.
one_liner: This paper quantifies how post-selected non-Gaussian CV teleportation gains fidelity at the cost of universality and success rate.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical benchmark for optical teleportation protocols by treating fidelity, input-uniformity, and heralding probability together.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze continuous-variable teleportation using both average fidelity and fidelity deviation, the latter measuring input-state dependence of single-shot performance. They prove that deterministic unity-gain displacement-covariant channels have zero fidelity deviation for coherent-state benchmarks, regardless of whether the resource is Gaussian or non-Gaussian. For input-selective conditioning, they show a quantitative trade-off: higher conditional fidelity generally comes with larger deviation and lower success probability. Measurement-based noiseless linear amplification is used as a concrete example of this fidelity-universality-heralding trade space.
main_result: The main result is a proof that universality loss in probabilistic CV teleportation appears as increased fidelity deviation, not simply from non-Gaussian resources.
application_angle: This framework helps assess whether a quantum communication primitive truly improves channel quality or only looks better through selective post-selection.

title: Hamiltonian simulation for 3D elastic wave equations in homogeneous elastic media
abstract: We present an explicit quantum circuit construction for Hamiltonian simulation of a first-order velocity--stress formulation of the three-dimensional elastic wave equation in homogeneous isotropic media. Previous studies have shown how elastic wave equations can be cast into forms amenable to Hamiltonian simulation, but they typically rely on black box Hamiltonian access assumptions, making gate complexity estimation difficult. Starting from the first-order velocity--stress formulation, we discretize the system by finite differences, transform it into Schrödinger form, and exploit the separation between the component register and the spatial register to decompose the Hamiltonian into structured tensor product terms. This yields explicit implementations of first-order and second-order Trotter formulas for the resulting time evolution operator. We derive corresponding error bounds and constant sensitive qubit and CNOT complexity estimates in terms of the discretization parameter, simulation time, target accuracy, and material parameters. Numerical experiments validate the proposed framework through comparisons with the exact time evolution and reconstructed physical fields.
one_liner: An explicit quantum-circuit construction simulates 3D elastic wave dynamics with concrete qubit, CNOT, and Trotter error estimates.
why_it_matters: It moves wave-equation simulation closer to implementable quantum algorithms by replacing black-box assumptions with circuit-level resource counts.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates the 3D homogeneous isotropic elastic wave equation in first-order velocity-stress form, discretizes it with finite differences, and maps it into Schrödinger form for Hamiltonian simulation. By separating component and spatial registers, the Hamiltonian is decomposed into structured tensor-product terms that admit explicit first- and second-order Trotter circuits. The authors derive error bounds and constant-sensitive qubit and CNOT complexities as functions of discretization, simulation time, accuracy, and material parameters. Numerical studies compare the simulated evolution to exact dynamics and reconstruct physical fields to validate the approach.
platform_or_system: quantum circuit model for elastic-wave PDEs
main_result: The work provides explicit implementable circuits and resource estimates for Hamiltonian simulation of 3D elastic wave propagation in homogeneous media.
application_angle: It is relevant for future quantum accelerators targeting wave propagation, imaging, and materials modeling where concrete gate costs matter.

title: Column Generation for the Optimization of Switching in Repeaterless Quantum Networks
abstract: Efficient resource allocation and optical switching promise high key rates, network adaptability, and cost reduction in repeaterless quantum communication networks. However, identifying optimal switching configurations remains a significant challenge due to the combinatorial complexity. We introduce a novel graph formulation to model the physical and logical structure of repeaterless quantum networks, enabling the systematic optimization of switching strategies. The problem is posed as a linear program and solved using a column generation approach. This method enables scalable computation despite the exponential number of possible network configurations. Our results not only provide a formal foundation but also a practical algorithm for the optimization of switching. Empirical tests confirm the solver's scalability with network size, demonstrating the framework's effectiveness and laying the groundwork for future optimization of quantum network control.
one_liner: A column-generation solver optimizes optical switching configurations in repeaterless quantum networks without brute-force enumeration.
why_it_matters: It offers a scalable control and resource-allocation method for practical quantum key distribution and entanglement-distribution networks.
detailed_summary: The authors model repeaterless quantum networks with a graph representation capturing both physical links and logical switching structure. Switching optimization is then cast as a linear program whose full variable set is exponentially large. To handle this, they use column generation to iteratively add only promising configurations, enabling tractable optimization at scale. Empirical tests show the approach scales with network size while producing optimized switching strategies.
main_result: The paper demonstrates a scalable column-generation method for computing near-optimal switching and resource-allocation policies in repeaterless quantum networks.
application_angle: This can improve key rates, flexibility, and hardware utilization in deployed metropolitan or backbone quantum communication infrastructures.

title: Quantum Optical Signatures of Band Topology in Solid-State High Harmonics
abstract: We develop a general theory of high-harmonic generation (HHG) in solid-state systems, based on a weak-correlation expansion of photonic and matter degrees of freedom. Unlike standard HHG theories, which treat light-matter dynamics through the Schrodinger equation, our approach employs density-matrix evolution, naturally capturing the mixed-state character of both the field and the matter - a critical aspect for describing complex solid-state band structures. We show explicitly that the properties of the emitted fields are governed by the quantum statistics and quantum geometry of the underlying solid. Taking the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model in a one-sided optical cavity as a paradigmatic example and considering the dual regime, we demonstrate that in the topological phase a system exhibits a stronger HHG response and stronger quantum-light signatures than in the trivial phase. Furthermore, we show that cavity-matter interaction gives rise to squeezed high-harmonic quantum light, whose properties are directly imprinted by the current-current fluctuations in the material system. Crucially, the observed squeezing does not rely on a separate quartic Kerr mechanism. In the mesoscopic regime, the genuine quantum Kerr term is higher order in light-matter coupling strength and negligible, while the relevant non-classical effect is governed by current-current fluctuations encoded in the complex susceptibility of the material. This work establishes a direct link between band topology and photon statistics, opening new avenues for topology-sensitive quantum light generation and photon statistics based spectroscopy of solid-state systems.
one_liner: A density-matrix theory links band topology to high-harmonic quantum-light statistics, including squeezing, in cavity-coupled solids.
why_it_matters: It connects topological condensed-matter physics with measurable nonclassical emitted light, suggesting new spectroscopy and source concepts.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a general high-harmonic generation theory for solids using density-matrix evolution of both light and matter, allowing mixed-state and quantum-statistical effects to enter naturally. In a one-sided cavity coupled to an SSH chain, the authors find that the topological phase yields stronger HHG emission and stronger quantum-light signatures than the trivial phase. They further show that squeezed high-harmonic light emerges from current-current fluctuations encoded in the material susceptibility rather than requiring a separate quartic Kerr process. The framework directly ties photon statistics to band geometry and topology.
platform_or_system: SSH solid in a one-sided optical cavity
main_result: The main result is that topological band structure enhances HHG and imprints nonclassical statistics, including squeezing, onto the emitted harmonics.
application_angle: It points to topology-sensitive light sources and spectroscopy schemes that read out material quantum geometry through emitted harmonic photon statistics.

title: Control Over Fano Parameter in Grating and One-Dimensional Photonic Crystal Cavity
abstract: Fano resonances are sharp asymmetrical spectral peaks which are now ubiquitous in nanophotonics. The high sensitivity of these resonances to system parameter has been exploited to improve light matter interaction and in applications such as sensing, filters and on-chip processing. The ability to dynamically change the Fano slope and spectral phase would enable optimization of the device parameters post fabrication for various applications. Here we demonstrate such a control over the Fano resonance in a one-dimensional photonics crystal cavity integrated on a silicon waveguide -grating platform. In our device, Fano resonance arises due to interference between cavity mode and an oscillatory background due to grating coupler. The dynamics tuning of Fano asymmetric parameter is achieved using thermos-optic effect in silicon. We experimentally tune the Fano parameter from ~-3.2 to +1.7 achieving a highest extinction ratio of 21.6 dB and spectral slope of 108dB/nm. All the above is achieved in an ultra-compact design with simple fabrication and with multiple cavities or feedback elements. The steep slope offers distinct advantage over conventional cavity for sensing and modulation applications and the tunability enables dynamic control over gain, dynamic range, bandwidth and noise coupling.
one_liner: A silicon grating-coupled 1D photonic crystal cavity enables dynamic tuning of the Fano asymmetry parameter across sign reversal.
why_it_matters: Post-fabrication control of Fano lineshapes is valuable for compact sensors, modulators, and filters that need optimized slope and extinction.
detailed_summary: The device combines a one-dimensional photonic crystal cavity with a silicon waveguide-grating platform, where interference between the cavity mode and an oscillatory grating background generates a Fano resonance. Using silicon thermo-optic tuning, the authors dynamically vary the Fano asymmetry parameter from about -3.2 to +1.7. They report up to 21.6 dB extinction ratio and a steep 108 dB/nm spectral slope in an ultra-compact structure. The approach provides tunable spectral phase and lineshape control without needing multiple cavities or feedback elements.
platform_or_system: silicon 1D photonic crystal cavity on waveguide-grating platform
main_result: The authors experimentally tune the Fano parameter over a wide range, including sign change, while achieving high extinction and very steep spectral slope.
application_angle: The tunable steep resonance can improve sensitivity, dynamic range, and bandwidth tradeoffs in integrated sensing, modulation, and filtering hardware.

title: Quantum metrology via mitigation of single-photon loss using an engineered nonlinear oscillator
abstract: The fragility of quantum metrological advantages under loss remains a major barrier to practical quantum sensing. For a two-photon-driven (TPD) Kerr resonator (TPD-Kerr model) subject to unavoidable single-photon loss (SPL), both the quantum Fisher information gain and squeezing level exhibit hard-to-track long-lived damped oscillations, restricting useful sensing and squeezing to extremely short time windows. We show that adding engineered two-photon loss (ETPL) -- forming a TPD-Kerr-ETPL hybrid model -- significantly mitigates these oscillations and converts the decay into a smooth, monotonic drop. This extends the high-sensitivity windows by over an order of magnitude. Moreover, we reveal a temporal hierarchy of quantum resources: the initial boost in metrological sensitivity arises from Gaussian squeezing, while sustained high-precision sensing stems from dissipatively stabilized non-Gaussian even-parity cat states. Crucially, only in models that include ETPL -- such as the TPD-Kerr-ETPL and TPD-ETPL systems -- does the dynamics actively mitigate SPL's detrimental effects, transforming damped oscillation into a smooth, easily trackable trajectory and enabling a prolonged, usable metrological window. Our approach transcends encoding-based or feedback-controlled schemes, offering a fully autonomous route to high-precision measurement without real-time feedback control. This establishes a general design principle: engineered loss, combined with appropriate driving, can actively preserve metrologically useful non-Gaussian quantum resources even in the presence of SPL -- paving the way toward robust, scalable quantum sensors in superconducting circuits, optomechanics, and trapped-ion platforms.
one_liner: Engineered two-photon loss stabilizes metrologically useful states in a nonlinear oscillator despite single-photon loss.
why_it_matters: It shows an autonomous, hardware-compatible way to extend useful quantum-sensing time windows without feedback control.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a two-photon-driven Kerr resonator with unavoidable single-photon loss and shows that adding engineered two-photon loss changes long-lived damped oscillations in quantum Fisher information and squeezing into a smoother monotonic decay. This substantially lengthens the interval over which enhanced sensitivity is practically accessible, by more than an order of magnitude. The authors identify a resource crossover in time: early sensitivity gain is dominated by Gaussian squeezing, while later robust performance comes from dissipatively stabilized even-parity non-Gaussian cat states. The result establishes engineered dissipation as an autonomous protection mechanism for lossy quantum metrology.
main_result: Adding engineered two-photon loss mitigates single-photon-loss-induced oscillations and prolongs high-sensitivity metrological operation in the nonlinear oscillator.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to robust sensors in superconducting, optomechanical, and trapped-ion platforms where loss normally destroys quantum advantage.

title: Device-independent quantum cryptography with input leakage
abstract: Device-independence is the gold standard of quantum cryptography. To meet this standard, a central assumption is that no information leakage occurs during protocol execution. We relax this assumption by analyzing CHSH-based randomness certification and key distribution with partial leakage of the inputs, modeled in terms of a noisy channel. Our results quantify the certifiable local randomness and the secret key rate as a function of the magnitude of the input leakage.
one_liner: This work quantifies how much device-independent randomness and key rate survive when protocol inputs partially leak.
why_it_matters: It relaxes an idealized security assumption and gives concrete performance bounds for more realistic DI quantum cryptography deployments.
detailed_summary: The authors study CHSH-based device-independent cryptographic protocols under partial leakage of the users' inputs, modeled through a noisy leakage channel. They derive how the certifiable local randomness and secret key rate degrade as the leakage magnitude increases. Rather than assuming perfectly shielded settings choices, the framework explicitly incorporates side-channel exposure during execution. This makes the analysis more aligned with practical implementations of DI randomness expansion and DI-QKD.
main_result: The paper provides quantitative bounds on certifiable randomness and secret-key rate as explicit functions of input leakage.
application_angle: These leakage-tolerant security benchmarks are useful for assessing real photonic DI-QKD and randomness systems with imperfect isolation of setting choices.

title: Bound, antibound and resonance two-photon states in chiral waveguide QED
abstract: We present a theoretical study of the two-particle spectrum $ω(K)$ for the chiral waveguide QED setup of an array of two-level atoms directionally interacting with photons propagating along the waveguide. We demonstrate that for each pair center-of-mass momentum $K$ there exist distinct solutions with $\Imω\le 0$ in the two-particle spectrum, corresponding to bound, antibound and resonance states, in addition to the continuum of scattering states. Contrary to previous studies, which showed the bound and resonance-state spectra only over a limited range of $K$, the calculated spectrum is consistent across all $K$ values. An interesting finding is that the real part of the spectrum $\Re ω(K)$ in the chiral model is gapless.
one_liner: A chiral waveguide-QED theory maps bound, antibound, and resonance two-photon states across the full center-of-mass momentum range.
why_it_matters: It sharpens understanding of few-photon spectral structure in chiral light-matter interfaces used for nonlinear quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: This theoretical work computes the two-particle spectrum for an array of two-level atoms chirally coupled to guided photons and identifies bound, antibound, and resonance branches in addition to scattering continua. A key result is a consistent description of these spectral solutions for all center-of-mass momenta K, extending beyond earlier partial treatments. The authors also find that the real part of the chiral-model spectrum is gapless. The analysis provides a fuller map of correlated two-photon excitations in directional waveguide QED.
main_result: The study establishes the full-K two-photon spectrum in chiral waveguide QED, including bound, antibound, and resonance states with a gapless real spectrum.
application_angle: This spectral understanding can inform design of waveguide-mediated photon interactions, few-photon transport devices, and strongly nonlinear photonic circuits.

title: A quantum frequency conversion hub interfacing with DWDM networks
abstract: Interconnecting heterogeneous quantum systems is an important step toward realizing the quantum internet. We propose a quantum network hub that interfaces local quantum devices with dense wavelength-division multiplexing (DWDM) networks in the telecom band via quantum frequency conversion (QFC) with frequency-channel selectivity. We show that standard periodically poled lithium niobate waveguides used for QFC exhibit a dispersion sweet spot, for example around the 780 nm band, enabling wide tunability of the pump wavelength while maintaining phase matching. Experimentally, we demonstrate the network hub by implementing a channel-selective and polarization-insensitive QFC from 780 nm to telecom wavelengths around 1540 nm. We achieve a pump tuning range of 2 THz and successfully distribute polarization-encoded single photons into 16 frequency channels on the ITU-T DWDM grid with 25 GHz channel spacing, while preserving the quantum information. These results position the QFC-based hub as a versatile backbone for connecting a wide range of quantum devices, spanning both photonic and matter-based systems, across frequency-multiplexed telecom networks.
one_liner: A polarization-preserving frequency-conversion hub routes 780 nm single photons into 16 telecom DWDM channels.
why_it_matters: It is a concrete networking primitive for linking diverse quantum emitters to standard fiber infrastructure with dense frequency multiplexing.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes and experimentally demonstrates a quantum network hub based on channel-selective, polarization-insensitive quantum frequency conversion from 780 nm to the telecom band. Using standard periodically poled lithium niobate waveguides near a dispersion sweet spot, the system supports about 2 THz of pump tuning while maintaining phase matching. The experiment distributes polarization-encoded single photons across 16 ITU-T DWDM channels with 25 GHz spacing and preserves the encoded quantum information. This combines frequency agility, telecom compatibility, and multiplexed routing in one interface.
main_result: The authors demonstrate 780 nm to telecom conversion with polarization preservation and selective distribution into 16 DWDM frequency channels.
application_angle: The hub can connect trapped-atom, ion, or other visible/near-IR quantum nodes to existing telecom fiber networks with scalable wavelength multiplexing.

title: Topological Word for Non-Abelian Topological Insulators
abstract: We propose a unified framework, dubbed topological word, for the complete non-Abelian bulk-boundary correspondence in multigap non-Abelian topological insulators. Composed by an ordered sequence of letters, each a non-Abelian charge depicting the gap-resolved topology, the topological word captures both the global non-Abelian topology corresponding to the homotopy classification, and the band-adjacency information. The latter, though crucial for the edge-state pattern across multiple gaps, is often overlooked in previous studies. We confirm our framework using both static models and periodically driven Floquet systems, and discuss its connection and distinction with existing descriptions, such as the phase-band singularities and braiding representations. Intriguingly, topological word continues to provide insight regarding topology and edge states, even as the global non-Abelian topology becomes ill-defined under broken parity-time symmetry.
one_liner: The paper introduces a "topological word" that encodes multigap non-Abelian topology and predicts edge-state structure.
why_it_matters: It offers a more complete descriptor for complex topological bandstructures relevant to photonic and Floquet-engineered platforms.
detailed_summary: The authors define a topological word as an ordered sequence of non-Abelian charges that captures both global multigap topology and band-adjacency information. This resolves an important deficiency of descriptions that miss how neighboring bands shape edge-state patterns across multiple gaps. They validate the framework on static and Floquet models and compare it with phase-band singularity and braiding-based descriptions. The construction remains informative even when broken parity-time symmetry makes the global non-Abelian invariant ill-defined.
main_result: The work provides a unified descriptor that predicts non-Abelian bulk-boundary correspondence and edge-state arrangements across multiple gaps.
application_angle: For topological photonics and driven quantum materials, the framework can guide engineering of protected edge transport and multiband device functionality.

title: Distributed Quantum-Enhanced Optimization: A Topographical Preconditioning Approach for High-Dimensional Search
abstract: Optimization problems become fundamentally challenging as the number of variables increases. Because the volume of the search space grows exponentially, classical algorithms frequently fail to locate the global minimum of non-convex functions. While quantum optimization offers a potential alternative, mapping continuous problems onto near-term quantum hardware introduces severe scaling limits and barren plateaus. To bridge this gap, we propose the Distributed Quantum-Enhanced Optimization (D-QEO) framework. Instead of forcing the quantum processor to find the exact minimum, we use it simply as a topographical preconditioner. The QPU maps the landscape to locate the most promising basin of attraction, generating high-quality seed points for a classical GPU-accelerated solver to refine. To make this approach viable for utility-scale problems, we exploit the mathematical structure of separable functions. This allows us to cut a 50-qubit (i.e., $2^{50}$) global search space into independent and manageable sub-spaces using 5-qubit subcircuits. By executing these fragments concurrently with CUDA-Q, we completely bypass the overhead of cross-register entanglement and classical tensor knitting for separable functions. Benchmarks on the 10-dimensional Rastrigin and Ackley functions show that D-QEO prevents the exponential failure rates observed in purely classical algorithms. Furthermore, this quantum warm-start significantly reduces the number of classical BFGS iterations required to converge, providing a highly practical blueprint for utilizing near-term quantum resources in complex global search.
one_liner: A distributed hybrid optimizer uses small quantum subcircuits as basin finders before classical refinement in high-dimensional search.
why_it_matters: It proposes a near-term hardware strategy that extracts practical value from limited QPUs without requiring large entangled registers.
detailed_summary: The D-QEO framework uses a quantum processor as a topographical preconditioner rather than a full optimizer, identifying promising basins of attraction that seed a classical GPU-based local solver. For separable objectives, the global space is decomposed into independent low-qubit subproblems, avoiding the scaling cost of large entangled registers and classical tensor knitting. Benchmarks on 10D Rastrigin and Ackley functions show reduced exponential failure relative to purely classical search and fewer BFGS refinement steps. The method is positioned as a utility-oriented hybrid workflow for near-term quantum hardware.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that parallel 5-qubit quantum subcircuits can warm-start classical optimization and improve performance on high-dimensional nonconvex benchmarks.
application_angle: This is relevant for deploying NISQ hardware in practical optimization pipelines where small quantum accelerators can complement classical HPC resources.

title: Quantum Advantage for Coordinated Frequency Selection Against Distributed Jammers
abstract: Consider two parties who want to agree on a common frequency band for communication in the presence of independent jammers. Such jammers block a different subset of bands at each site, where each party can observe only its own set of unjammed bands. Yet, they must agree on a common band without communicating. We first establish the optimal classical strategy, maximizing the probability they output a common frequency band in a single shot. We proceed to show that sharing an entangled pair of local dimension d allows the parties to coordinate strictly better, provided both the number of safe bands d and the spectrum size n are sufficiently large. We study explicit quantum strategies offering a pathway to near-term demonstrations, including an explicit strategy for d = 2 that outperforms the classical optimum for all spectrum sizes, achieving a 5.4% advantage asymptotically (in n) with just one shared Bell pair. Our approach is based on a general framework for constructing quantum strategies from classical spreading sequences via symmetric orthonormalization that may be of independent interest, and opens the door to concrete applications of quantum networks for cognitive radio and spread-spectrum communication.
one_liner: Entanglement improves one-shot frequency coordination against distributed jammers, even with a single shared Bell pair.
why_it_matters: It turns a communication-without-communication task into a near-term quantum-network advantage relevant to anti-jam spectrum access.
detailed_summary: The paper studies two separated users who must independently choose the same unjammed frequency band despite each seeing only local jammer constraints and being unable to communicate. It derives the optimal classical single-shot success probability and then constructs entanglement-assisted strategies that beat it when the safe-band count and spectrum size are large enough. An explicit qubit strategy using one Bell pair already outperforms the classical optimum for any spectrum size, with an asymptotic 5.4% gain. The construction uses symmetric orthonormalization of classical spreading sequences, giving a concrete route to implementable quantum coordination protocols.
main_result: A shared entangled state, including a single Bell pair for d=2, yields strictly higher agreement probability than the optimal classical strategy in distributed anti-jam frequency selection.
application_angle: The result maps directly onto cognitive radio and spread-spectrum coordination tasks where nodes need robust common-channel selection under adversarial interference.

title: Controllable non-Hermitian topology in a dynamically protected cat qubit
abstract: Dissipatively stabilized cat qubits are promising for fault-tolerant quantum information processing, yet their non-Hermitian (NH) spectral topology remains largely unexplored. We uncover rich Liouvillian exceptional structures in a cat-qubit mode stabilized by two-photon drive (TPD) and engineered two-photon loss, in the presence of single-photon drive (SPD) and single-photon loss. In the parameter space spanned by SPD strength and detuning, we identify both second- and third-order Liouvillian exceptional points (LEP2s and LEP3s). Remarkably, we show that the phase $θ$ of TPD provides coherent control over these exceptional points: the LEP3 diverges and vanishes at $θ=π/2$, while remaining stable and tunable elsewhere. We introduce a topological invariant based on the winding number of a resultant vector, which robustly identifies LEP3s with unit topological charge. Full master-equation simulations confirm that the system dynamics remains confined to the logical subspace with near-unity fidelity. Our results bridge dissipative stabilization, phase-coherent control, and NH topology, demonstrating controllable higher-order LEPs in open quantum systems.
one_liner: A dissipatively stabilized cat qubit exhibits tunable second- and third-order Liouvillian exceptional points controlled by drive phase.
why_it_matters: It links cat-qubit hardware to controllable non-Hermitian topology while preserving logical-subspace confinement needed for protected operation.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze a cat-qubit mode stabilized by two-photon drive and engineered two-photon loss, perturbed by single-photon drive and loss. In the parameter space of single-photon-drive strength and detuning, they identify both second- and third-order Liouvillian exceptional points and show that the two-photon-drive phase coherently controls their existence and location. A winding-number invariant built from a resultant vector diagnoses the third-order points with unit topological charge. Full master-equation simulations indicate the dynamics stays confined to the logical cat subspace with near-unity fidelity despite the non-Hermitian structure.
main_result: The work demonstrates phase-controllable higher-order Liouvillian exceptional points in a protected cat-qubit platform without losing logical-subspace fidelity.
application_angle: This could enable topology-aware control and sensing protocols embedded directly in bosonic qubit hardware for fault-tolerant quantum processors.

title: Reflections on Quantum Reflectometry: Quantum and Tunneling capacitances as well as Sisyphus and Hermes resistances
abstract: When a quantum electronic device is coupled to an electrical resonator, admittance changes of the quantum subsystem may be detected. The effective reactance may include capacitive and inductive terms that incorporate geometric, quantum, and tunneling components; while the effective resistance may be composed of Sisyphus and Hermes terms linked to relaxation and decoherence, respectively. Such reflectometry is usually studied when all characteristic times of the quantum system are much shorter than the resonator's period, in which case only stationary quantum states are probed. We present a rigorous description of a driven-dissipative qudit-resonator system. Our approach demonstrates how to strictly introduce quantum and tunneling capacitances as well as Hermes and Sisyphus resistances, and how these values are modified when the dynamics of the subsystems becomes mutually dependent. We present the cases of a Cooper-pair box, a single-Cooper-pair transistor, a double quantum dot, and a single-electron box. Our approach can be applied to describe any quantum system coupled to any classical resonator.
one_liner: A rigorous driven-dissipative theory of quantum reflectometry separates quantum, tunneling, Sisyphus, and Hermes admittance contributions.
why_it_matters: It gives device builders a more complete framework for interpreting resonator readout signals beyond the fast-relaxation approximation.
detailed_summary: This work develops a general treatment of a driven-dissipative qudit coupled to a classical resonator, without assuming the quantum subsystem is always much faster than the resonator. Within that framework, the authors define quantum and tunneling capacitances and distinguish Sisyphus and Hermes resistive terms associated with relaxation and decoherence. They show how these effective admittance components are modified when resonator and quantum-system dynamics become interdependent. Worked examples include a Cooper-pair box, single-Cooper-pair transistor, double quantum dot, and single-electron box.
main_result: The paper derives a unified admittance formalism for quantum reflectometry that remains valid when subsystem dynamics are mutually coupled rather than adiabatically separated.
application_angle: It can improve parameter extraction and readout design for superconducting and semiconductor quantum devices monitored through RF resonators.

title: Knotted spacetime electromagnetic vortex unlinking and unknotting with vector and scalar reconnections and field twist compensation
abstract: Optical vortex knots have been realized in monochromatic laser beams, but monochromatic fields are stationary and their topology is frozen. Here we show that knotted spatiotemporal vortices, whose phase singularities form closed loops in spacetime, undergo topology changing reconnections with free space propagation. When null lines of different vector components unlink, the electric spin, magnetic spin, linear momentum, and electromagnetic helicity densities, each built from a specific pair of field components, twist to exactly compensate the change in linking number. This compensation is enforced by the argument principle where the total for each component pair, combining mutual phase twist, geometric linking, and open-line threading, vanishes identically and remains exactly zero through all reconnection events.
one_liner: Spatiotemporal electromagnetic vortex knots can change topology in flight, with field-twist quantities exactly compensating reconnections.
why_it_matters: It advances structured-light control in fully time-dependent fields, which matters for robust information encoding in singular optical waveforms.
detailed_summary: The paper moves beyond stationary monochromatic vortex knots by studying phase singularities that form closed loops in spacetime and evolve during free-space propagation. It shows that topology-changing reconnections can unlink or unknot vector-component null lines while electric spin, magnetic spin, momentum, and helicity densities acquire compensating twist. Using the argument principle, the authors prove that for each field-component pair the sum of mutual phase twist, geometric linking, and open-line threading stays exactly zero through reconnections. This provides a conservation-like topological accounting framework for dynamic electromagnetic singularities.
main_result: The work proves and illustrates exact twist-compensation rules governing topological reconnections of knotted spacetime optical vortices during propagation.
application_angle: Dynamic topological control of structured light could inform future free-space encoding schemes that exploit vortex topology rather than static beam structure alone.

title: Light-induced Self-Organization in Cooperative Free Space Atomic Arrays
abstract: We investigate how laser-driven, cooperative dipole-dipole interactions in weakly trapped atomic arrays give rise to self-organized configurations. Starting from an analytically tractable two-emitter system, we identify the possible steady-state spatial arrangements accessible to the atoms. We then extend this analysis to larger ensembles in both linear and ring geometries. In linear chains, we demonstrate the emergence of topologically nontrivial dimerized configurations across a range of initial interatomic spacings. In ring geometries, we find that the system undergoes self-organized contraction and expansion, enabling access to length scales below those set by the trapping lattice. Our results demonstrate that collective light-matter interactions in free space can spontaneously generate modified ordered geometries, even when the emitters are initially separated by distances larger than their transition wavelength.
one_liner: Laser-driven free-space atomic arrays self-organize into dimerized, contracted, or expanded configurations via cooperative dipole forces.
why_it_matters: It shows collective light-matter interactions can reconfigure emitter geometry on demand, a useful capability for tunable photonic quantum matter.
detailed_summary: Starting from an analytically solvable two-emitter problem, the paper identifies steady-state configurations generated by laser-driven cooperative dipole-dipole interactions in weak traps. It then extends the analysis to many-body linear chains and rings, finding topologically nontrivial dimerized order in chains and contraction or expansion dynamics in rings. Notably, the collective dynamics can produce interatomic spacing below the trapping-lattice scale and can reorganize arrays even when initial separations exceed the optical wavelength. The work highlights geometry as an emergent dynamical degree of freedom in free-space emitter ensembles.
main_result: Cooperative dipole interactions are shown to induce stable self-organized geometries, including dimerized chains and size-changing rings, in weakly trapped atomic arrays.
application_angle: Self-reconfiguring emitter arrays could be used to tune collective optical response, photon transport, and network interfaces without fabricating fixed photonic structures.

title: Qubit-efficient and gate-efficient encodings of graph partitioning problems for quantum optimization
abstract: We introduce a qubit- and gate-efficient higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) encoding for graph partitioning problems requiring label-count minimization. This widely applicable class of problems includes minimum graph coloring, minimum $k$-cut, and community detection. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to address the optimization versions of these problems in a quantum setting, rather than only their decision counterparts. Our construction encodes each $k$-valued vertex variable using $\lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ bits and employs a novel lexicographic penalty system that implicitly minimizes partition count without requiring dedicated indicator variables. We derive provably sufficient conditions on all penalty coefficients, including those arising from Rosenberg quadratization, guaranteeing feasibility and optimality of the lowest-energy solution. Analogous conditions are derived for a one-hot encoding to enable controlled comparison. We also show that our encoding reduces two-qubit gate count per QAOA layer from $Θ(|V||k|^2 + |E||k|)$ for the one-hot encoding to $Θ(|E| \cdot |k| \lceil\log_2 |k|\rceil)$. Benchmarking on a quantum annealer demonstrates that our logarithmic encoding significantly improves solution quality and time-to-solution for minimum graph coloring relative to one-hot encoding, with greater advantage as problem size increases.
one_liner: A logarithmic-variable HUBO encoding cuts qubit and gate costs for graph partitioning and improves annealer performance over one-hot encodings.
why_it_matters: Resource-efficient encodings are a practical bottleneck for near-term optimization hardware, and this work directly reduces that overhead.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization encoding for label-count-minimizing graph partitioning problems such as minimum coloring, minimum k-cut, and community detection. Each k-valued vertex variable is represented with ceil(log2 k) bits, and a lexicographic penalty construction implicitly minimizes partition count without extra indicator variables. They derive sufficient penalty conditions, including after Rosenberg quadratization, and compare against one-hot encodings. The resulting QAOA layer cost scales as Theta(|E|·|k|·ceil(log2|k|)) instead of Theta(|V||k|^2 + |E||k|), and annealer benchmarks show better solution quality and time-to-solution.
platform_or_system: Quantum optimization encodings for annealers and QAOA
main_result: The proposed logarithmic HUBO encoding reduces qubit and two-qubit-gate overhead and empirically outperforms one-hot encoding on minimum graph coloring benchmarks.
application_angle: Better encodings expand the size and quality of combinatorial optimization instances that near-term annealers and gate-model hardware can handle.

title: Nanoscale Fluorescence Thermometry: Probes, Recent Advances and Emerging Directions
abstract: The transition of materials and devices to nanometer, atomic, and quantum scales makes thermal characterization increasingly challenging, driving the need for advanced nanoscale thermometry. Fluorescence nanothermometry has emerged as a powerful approach, enabling remote, spatially resolved temperature measurements with sub-micrometer-to-nanometer precision across applications in nanoelectronics, microfluidics, and biological systems. In these systems, temperature is inferred from variations in fluorescence observables, including spectral position, intensity, linewidth, and excited-state dynamics. This review provides a comprehensive and critical overview of fluorescence nanothermometry, covering fundamental mechanisms, material platforms, recent advances, and emerging applications. It further presents a critical evaluation of key challenges and discusses emerging strategies and future research directions toward achieving robust, real-time thermometry. It is anticipated that this review will stimulate further advances in material platforms and system design, accelerating the development of accurate, scalable, and application-ready nanoscale thermometers.
one_liner: This review maps the probe physics, performance limits, and application paths of fluorescence thermometry from sub-micrometer to nanoscale regimes.
why_it_matters: It consolidates practical design rules for remote nanoscale temperature readout in nanoelectronics, microfluidics, and bioimaging where conventional thermometry fails.
detailed_summary: The paper reviews fluorescence-based nanoscale thermometry, where temperature is inferred from shifts in emission wavelength, intensity, linewidth, and excited-state lifetimes. It compares material platforms and sensing modalities, emphasizing spatially resolved, non-contact measurements at sub-micrometer to nanometer scales. The authors also analyze error sources, calibration challenges, and robustness requirements for real-time deployment. A key contribution is the critical assessment of what is needed to translate fluorescent probes into scalable, application-ready thermal metrology.
