main_result: It provides a critical, system-level synthesis of fluorescence nanothermometry mechanisms, probe platforms, limitations, and emerging implementation strategies.
application_angle: The review is directly useful for designing deployable nanoscale thermal sensors for chip diagnostics, lab-on-chip systems, and biological temperature mapping.

title: Magnetic-field control of interactions in alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms and applications to {\it XXZ} models
abstract: We study the magnetic-field dependence of the interactions between two alkaline-earth(-like) Rydberg atoms, ${}^{88}$Sr and ${}^{174}$Yb. Considering the pair of Rydberg states $|ns,{}^3S_1,m_J\rangle$ and $|(n+1)s,{}^3S_1,m_J\rangle$, we show that the effective Hamiltonian takes the form of an {\it XXZ}-type quantum spin model, as in the alkali-atom case [M. Kunimi and T. Tomita, Phys. Rev. A {\bf 112}, L051301 (2025)]. We find that the behavior of the anisotropy parameter for ${}^{174}$Yb at zero magnetic field is significantly different from that for other atomic species. This behavior originates from the strong spin-orbit coupling in ${}^{174}$Yb. We systematically calculate the interaction parameters of the {\it XXZ} model in the presence of a magnetic field and show that they can be tuned by the field. As applications to quantum many-body problems, we investigate one-dimensional systems in the large-anisotropy regime and show that the folded {\it XXZ} model can be realized in ${}^{174}$Yb systems without fine-tuning of the field. We also investigate two-dimensional square-lattice systems and show that a supersolid phase can emerge in the ground state at the mean-field level.
one_liner: Magnetic fields are shown to tune Rydberg interactions in Sr and Yb into controllable XXZ spin models with unusual Yb anisotropy.
why_it_matters: It identifies alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms, especially Yb, as a tunable hardware platform for engineered many-body Hamiltonians and exotic phases.
detailed_summary: The authors compute magnetic-field-dependent interactions between pairs of alkaline-earth(-like) Rydberg atoms in $|ns,^3S_1,m_J\rangle$ and $|(n+1)s,^3S_1,m_J\rangle$ states and derive an effective XXZ spin Hamiltonian. They find a distinct zero-field anisotropy behavior in $^{174}$Yb caused by strong spin-orbit coupling, unlike alkali platforms and the other species studied. Using the calculated couplings, they analyze 1D and 2D many-body regimes, including realization of a folded XXZ model and a mean-field supersolid phase on a square lattice. The work turns magnetic field control into a concrete knob for Hamiltonian engineering in neutral-atom hardware.
main_result: The main result is that Sr and Yb Rydberg interactions can be magnetically tuned into effective XXZ couplings, with $^{174}$Yb offering especially distinctive anisotropy and access to folded-XXZ and supersolid regimes.
application_angle: This gives a practical route to programmable analog quantum simulators using neutral atoms with richer interaction control than standard alkali implementations.

title: Time-optimal Qubit Reset via Environmental Spectral Structure
abstract: Fast qubit reset is essential for qubit reuse in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum computing era, yet it conflicts with the weak decoherence required for high-fidelity computation. We solve the time-optimal reset problem for a frequency-tunable qubit coupled to a structural environment under realistic spectral and control constraints. The optimal strategy consists of a switch--restore--switch sequence, where the qubit is moved from a low-decoherence computational configuration to a high-decoherence restoring configuration and then returned for reuse. For superconducting qubits in four representative environments, this strategy reduces the reset time from typically $\gtrsim\SI{100}{\nano\second}$ to $\SI{20}{\nano\second}$, about $40\%$ of a typical two-qubit gate time, while achieving a reset precision of $10^{-5}$. Our results identify environmental spectral structure as a practical resource for rapid, high-fidelity qubit reset and provide a design principle for qubit reuse on qubit-limited processors.
one_liner: A switch-restore-switch protocol exploits structured environments to reset tunable qubits in about 20 ns with 10^-5 residual excitation.
why_it_matters: Fast, precise reset is a core bottleneck for qubit reuse, and this work turns environmental spectral structure into a hardware-level reset resource.
detailed_summary: The paper solves the time-optimal reset problem for a frequency-tunable qubit coupled to a structured environment under realistic control and spectral constraints. The optimal protocol moves the qubit from a low-decoherence operating point to a high-dissipation restoring point and back, yielding a switch-restore-switch sequence. For superconducting qubits across four representative environments, the method cuts reset times from typically above 100 ns to 20 ns while reaching a reset error around 10^-5. The analysis establishes that nontrivial bath spectral structure can be engineered and exploited rather than treated only as a source of decoherence.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a time-optimal reset strategy that achieves approximately 20 ns reset with 10^-5 precision, around 40% of a typical two-qubit gate time.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to qubit-limited processors because it enables faster qubit recycling, lower idle overhead, and denser circuit execution.

title: StabilizerBench: A Benchmark for AI-Assisted Quantum Error Correction Circuit Synthesis
abstract: As quantum hardware scales toward fault tolerant operation, the demand for correct quantum error correction (QEC) circuits far outpaces manual design capacity. AI agents offer a promising path to automating this synthesis, yet no benchmark exists to measure their progress on the specialized task of generating QEC circuits. We introduce StabilizerBench, a benchmark suite of 192 stabilizer codes spanning 12 families, 4-196 qubits, and distances 2-21, organized into three tasks of increasing difficulty: state preparation circuit generation, circuit optimization under semantic constraints, and fault tolerant circuit synthesis. Although motivated by QEC, stabilizer circuits exercise core competencies required for general quantum programming, including gate decomposition, qubit routing, and semantic preserving transformations, while admitting efficient verification via the Gottesman Knill theorem, enabling the benchmark to scale to large codes without the exponential cost of full unitary comparison. We define a unified generator weighted scoring system with two tiers: a capability score measuring breadth of success and a quality score capturing circuit merit. We also introduce continuous fault tolerance and optimization metrics that grade error resilience and circuit improvements beyond binary pass or fail. Following the design of classical benchmarks such as SWE-bench, StabilizerBench specifies inputs, verification oracles, and scoring but leaves prompts and agent strategies open. We evaluate three frontier AI agents and find the benchmark discriminates across models and tasks with substantial headroom for improvement.
one_liner: StabilizerBench introduces a scalable benchmark suite for AI generation, optimization, and fault-tolerant synthesis of stabilizer-QEC circuits.
why_it_matters: QEC circuit design is becoming a throughput bottleneck, and this benchmark provides a concrete way to measure progress of AI coding agents on hardware-relevant quantum tasks.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a benchmark of 192 stabilizer codes spanning 12 families, 4 to 196 qubits, and code distances 2 to 21, organized into generation, optimization, and fault-tolerant synthesis tasks. Verification relies on efficient stabilizer-theoretic checks rather than full unitary comparison, making evaluation scalable. The benchmark defines weighted capability and quality scores plus continuous fault-tolerance and optimization metrics, allowing more nuanced grading than binary success. Initial evaluations on three frontier AI agents show clear discrimination and substantial headroom.
main_result: The main result is a verified, scalable benchmark framework for evaluating AI-assisted synthesis and optimization of stabilizer-based QEC circuits across a wide range of code families and sizes.
application_angle: It supports practical automation of quantum software and QEC compilation workflows needed as hardware scales toward fault-tolerant operation.

title: Observation of quantum multi-Mpemba effect in a trapped-ion system
abstract: The quantum Mpemba effect (ME) in Markovian systems is conventionally explained by a smaller overlap between the initial state and the slowest decay mode (SDM). Such state, initially farther away from equilibrium or steady state, relaxes faster than closer ones, resulting to a crossing of their trajectories. This picture, by neglecting the transient dynamics, holds in the long-time limit. Here we experimentally observe multiple trajectory crossings (multi-ME) in the relaxation dynamics of a trapped ion. Such novel dynamics takes place in a unusual scenario where the initial state instead has a larger overlap with the SDM. We develop a theoretical framework based on relaxation speed to understand the multi-ME. We show that the initial relaxation speed is governed by the fastest decay mode, which together with the SDM overlap gives a phase diagram that reveals both the occurrence and the types of quantum ME observed in our experiment. Our study goes beyond the simple picture based on the long-time limit, tracks continuously the quantum ME dynamics, and establishes a comprehensive framework to describe the transient quantum relaxation.
one_liner: A trapped-ion experiment observes multiple quantum Mpemba crossings and links them to transient relaxation speeds, not just slow-mode overlap.
why_it_matters: It refines how nonequilibrium quantum relaxation is understood and controlled in experimentally relevant open-system platforms.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally observe a multi-Mpemba effect in a trapped-ion system, where relaxation trajectories cross multiple times en route to steady state. Unlike the standard explanation based solely on reduced overlap with the slowest decay mode, the observed behavior occurs even when the initial state has larger slow-mode overlap. They develop a framework based on relaxation speed, showing that the fastest decay mode governs early-time dynamics while slow-mode overlap controls longer-time behavior. This yields a phase diagram that classifies when and what type of quantum Mpemba behavior appears.
main_result: The work demonstrates multiple relaxation trajectory crossings in a trapped-ion experiment and provides a transient-dynamics theory that explains them via competing fast and slow decay modes.
application_angle: Better control of transient relaxation could inform state preparation, reset, and dissipation engineering in ion-trap and broader quantum hardware.

title: Scalable surface ion trap design for magnetic quantum sensing and gradiometry
abstract: Magnetic quantum sensors based on trapped ions utilize properties of quantum mechanics which have optimized precision and beat current limits in sensor technology. Trapped ions are highly sensitive in a large span of signal ranging from DC or static B-field to the radiofrequency range in 100s of MHz and can attain the sensitivity in the range of pT to sub pT . They are tuneable to frequencies of interest and can be used as a lock-in frequency detector. This modelling and simulation based study presents an innovative design of Surface Paul Traps, enabling the use of trapped ions as ultra-sensitive sensors for magnetic field detection and precise measurement of magnetic field gradients at a sub-millimeter spatial resolution. The novel design features multiple trapping regions, allowing for the mapping of magnetic fields across various ion-trapping zones. The study demonstrates groundbreaking advancements in ion manipulation and confinement through innovative chip architecture.
one_liner: A modeled surface Paul trap architecture enables scalable trapped-ion magnetic sensing and sub-millimeter gradiometry with multiple sensing zones.
why_it_matters: It pushes trapped-ion sensors toward chip-scale, spatially resolved magnetic-field mapping rather than single-point laboratory demonstrations.
detailed_summary: This modeling and simulation study proposes a surface Paul trap chip with multiple trapping regions tailored for magnetic-field sensing and gradient measurements using trapped ions. The design targets sensitivity from DC to hundreds of MHz and supports field mapping across separate ion zones with sub-millimeter spatial resolution. The architecture emphasizes scalable ion confinement and manipulation on a surface-electrode platform, enabling distributed sensing rather than a single localized probe. The paper therefore connects ion-trap hardware design directly to quantum metrology use cases.
main_result: The main result is a scalable multi-zone surface ion trap design for high-sensitivity magnetic sensing and gradiometry with sub-millimeter spatial resolution.
application_angle: This could enable compact magnetic imagers and frequency-selective field sensors for materials diagnostics, device characterization, and precision instrumentation.

title: Vertical Shuttling Protocols for Trapped Ions in Multi-Rail, Multi-Zone Surface Ion Trap Architectures
abstract: We investigate optimized vertical ion-shuttling protocols for trapped-ion applications across a range of ion-trap experiments, including three-dimensional gradient-measurement sensors, on-chip ion fluorescence collection and imaging, improved laser accessibility, and quantum information processing. In this work, we focus on minimizing motional energy gain during ion transport. Our findings indicate that anomalous heating becomes the dominant limiting factor only for shuttling durations exceeding $500~μ\mathrm{s}$, whereas the final motional excitation is strongly dependent on the selected transport protocol. Using a recently measured heating rate of $(3.1 \pm 0.35)$ quanta/ms at an ion--surface separation of $134 \pm 1.5~μ\mathrm{m}$, we demonstrate that the motional excitation can be restricted to fewer than eight quanta when the ion is vertically displaced by $86~μ\mathrm{m}$ from its initial position. These results enable adiabatic shuttling within $0.5~\mathrm{ms}$, thereby meeting the operational requirements for high-fidelity quantum sensing and coherent control.
one_liner: Optimized vertical shuttling moves trapped ions across 86 um with low motional heating in multi-zone surface traps.
why_it_matters: It shows vertical transport can stay within sensing and gate-fidelity budgets, enabling 3D ion-trap architectures with practical reconfiguration times.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes vertical ion transport protocols for multi-rail, multi-zone surface traps with an emphasis on minimizing final motional excitation. Using an experimentally measured heating rate of 3.1 plus/minus 0.35 quanta/ms at 134 um ion-surface spacing, it finds anomalous heating only dominates for transport times longer than 500 us, while protocol choice sets the main excitation cost. For an 86 um vertical displacement, the authors show fewer than eight added quanta are achievable, supporting adiabatic shuttling within 0.5 ms. The target use cases include 3D gradient sensing, improved optical access, fluorescence collection, and scalable trapped-ion processing.
main_result: Vertical shuttling by 86 um can be performed in under 0.5 ms while keeping motional excitation below eight quanta under realistic measured heating rates.
application_angle: This enables practical vertical ion repositioning for better laser access, readout optics, and field-sensing geometries without sacrificing coherent control.

title: Bayesian Phase Stabilization at the Shot-Noise Limit for Scalable Quantum Networks
abstract: High-precision optical phase stabilization in quantum networks is fundamentally constrained by the strict photon-flux and duty-cycle limits required to avoid disturbing fragile quantum states. This challenge becomes especially critical when coordinating multiple independent light sources for multi-step quantum protocols. Here, we develop an integrated phase-stabilization framework that incorporates a Bayesian phase estimator to optimally extract information from sparse single-photon detection events. This approach outperforms conventional maximum-likelihood estimation and achieves the shot-noise limit under minimal photon flux. The framework enables real-time correction of combined phase noise from both nodal lasers and transmission fibers, facilitating a two-step excitation protocol for heralded entanglement generation between separate trapped-ion nodes via single-photon interference. Operating with a detected photon rate of approximately 1 MHz and a duty cycle less than or equal to 6.5%, the system maintains interferometric visibility greater than 97% over fiber links of 10 km and 100 km. This phase control yields deterministic ion-ion entanglement with parity contrast exceeding 85% at both distances, enabling device-independent quantum key distribution. Moreover, the resulting memory-memory entanglement at 10 km survives beyond the average time required to establish it -- a fundamental requirement for quantum repeaters. This work establishes a robust and scalable foundation for practical long-distance quantum networks.
one_liner: A Bayesian phase-locking scheme reaches the shot-noise limit and stabilizes 10 km to 100 km quantum links for ion-network entanglement.
why_it_matters: Robust low-flux phase control is a core bottleneck for long-distance entanglement distribution, repeaters, and DI-QKD in scalable quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The work introduces an integrated phase-stabilization framework based on Bayesian estimation from sparse single-photon detection events, explicitly targeting the low-photon-flux regime required in quantum networking. It corrects joint phase noise from remote lasers and transmission fibers in real time, enabling a two-step heralded entanglement protocol between separate trapped-ion nodes via single-photon interference. At about 1 MHz detected photon rate and duty cycle up to 6.5%, the system maintains more than 97% interferometric visibility over both 10 km and 100 km fiber links. This yields deterministic ion-ion entanglement with parity contrast above 85% at both distances, and memory-memory entanglement at 10 km that outlives the mean establishment time.
main_result: The authors achieve shot-noise-limited phase stabilization and sustain greater than 97% visibility over 10 km and 100 km links, enabling high-contrast deterministic remote ion entanglement.
application_angle: The method directly supports deployable repeater nodes and device-independent QKD by making long-haul phase stabilization feasible under realistic photon budgets.

title: A four-player potential game for barren-plateau-aware quantum ansatz design
abstract: We cast the design of parameterized quantum circuits as a four-player potential game whose state is a circuit directed acyclic graph (DAG) and whose players encode trainability, non-stabilizerness, task performance, and hardware cost. Per-player restricted action sets factorize the move space into append, remove, retype, and rewire operations; a block-coordinate $\varepsilon$-Nash residual $δ_\text{Nash}$ certifies that no single player can improve unilaterally. A single weight sweep on MaxCut $K_4$ traces a Pareto frontier from a Clifford endpoint $(M_2/n,\langle H\rangle)=(0,4.00)$ to a non-Clifford endpoint $(0.48,3.30)$. On three four-qubit hardware topologies (heavy-hex, $2\times 2$ grid, Rydberg all-to-all), Nash search achieves the highest mean potential; on the $2\times 2$ grid Nash reaches the theoretical ceiling $Φ_\text{max}=4.10$ on two of five seeds while the simulated-annealing baseline does so on one; paired Wilcoxon tests over five seeds cannot reject the null on any single topology ($p\ge 0.22$). On LiH/STO-3G, seeding Nash from a 58-gate Givens-doubles ansatz produces a 48-operation, depth-25 circuit retaining $97.7\%$ of the correlation energy while simultaneously reducing gate count, increasing non-stabilizerness, and controlling trainability. The framework is complementary to energy-only searches such as ADAPT-VQE and k-UpCCGSD, which reach chemical accuracy with fewer operations but do not optimize the other three axes.
one_liner: Quantum ansatz design is cast as a four-player game balancing trainability, non-Clifford resources, task quality, and hardware cost.
why_it_matters: It offers a hardware-aware route to circuits that resist barren plateaus without optimizing only one axis such as energy or gate count.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates parameterized-circuit synthesis as a four-player potential game on circuit DAGs, with players corresponding to trainability, non-stabilizerness, task performance, and hardware cost. Restricted moves decompose search into append, remove, retype, and rewire operations, while a block-coordinate epsilon-Nash residual certifies local equilibrium quality. On MaxCut K4 across heavy-hex, 2x2 grid, and all-to-all Rydberg topologies, Nash search attains the highest mean potential and reaches the theoretical optimum on the grid for two of five seeds. For LiH/STO-3G, it compresses a 58-gate Givens-doubles ansatz to a 48-operation, depth-25 circuit while preserving 97.7% of correlation energy.
main_result: The game-theoretic search produces hardware-constrained ansatze that improve multi-objective quality and, for LiH, retain 97.7% correlation energy with fewer operations and shallower depth.
application_angle: This can reduce iteration cost in near-term quantum processors by generating trainable, hardware-efficient ansatze tailored to specific connectivity constraints.

title: Microwave noise downconversion in interband cascade laser frequency combs
abstract: Chip-scale semiconductor laser frequency combs offer remarkable prospects for compact and power-efficient optical sensors. For the laser to be suitable for typical comb applications, its degree of coherence must first be assessed from a microwave self-mixing signal. Unfortunately, such measurements require scarcely available high-speed photodetectors with multi-GHz bandwidths and radio-frequency electronics. However, in this work, we demonstrate a simplified approach to comb coherence assessment for interband cascade lasers based on a relationship between easily-accessible MHz-frequency (baseband) noise and the multi-GHz-frequency intermode beat note. The downconversion of microwave noise to near-DC frequencies is found to originate intrinsically from the laser, which simultaneously acts as a frequency mixer due to electrical nonlinearities and a phase-to-amplitude noise converter due to the linewidth enhancement factor. Correlation between the electrical signals is explored in both frequency and time domains. Since this phenomenon is potentially universal in semiconductor lasers, it creates a new opportunity for frequency comb characterization, which may be particularly valuable in wavelength regions where fast photodetectors have limited availability.
one_liner: Interband cascade laser comb coherence can be inferred from baseband noise through intrinsic microwave-to-DC downconversion.
why_it_matters: It lowers the instrumentation barrier for comb diagnostics in spectral regions where fast photodetectors and RF hardware are scarce.
detailed_summary: This work shows that coherence information normally extracted from the multi-GHz intermode beat note of an interband cascade laser comb can be accessed through near-DC and MHz electrical noise. The mechanism is intrinsic: electrical nonlinearities make the laser act as a mixer, while the linewidth enhancement factor converts phase noise into amplitude noise. The authors establish correlation between baseband and microwave signals in both frequency and time domains. The result is a simplified coherence-assessment pathway that avoids reliance on scarce high-speed photodetectors and RF electronics.
main_result: The study demonstrates that intrinsic noise downconversion links accessible baseband noise to the comb intermode beat note, enabling simplified coherence characterization.
application_angle: This could make chip-scale comb qualification more practical for sensing systems, especially in wavelength bands with limited detector bandwidth.

title: Optical hopfions with arbitrary two winding numbers
abstract: Hopfions, as three-dimensional topologically nontrivial structures described by poloidal and toroidal winding numbers, hold promise as robust information carriers in spintronics, functional materials, and optical communications. Although they have been experimentally realized in various physical systems, such realizations have been restricted to low orders, with the winding numbers lacking tunability. Here, using optical fields as our platform, we outline how to make tunable hopfions in any order with any winding number. We use tailored superpositions of Laguerre-Gaussian modes in free-space as our construction, achieving effective control for arbitrary-order poloidal and toroidal winding numbers, which we demonstrate up to orders 5 and 3, respectively, for a new state-of-the-art. The resulting torus-knot structures are visualized experimentally via polarization filaments, confirming the designed topological textures. Our work reports an exotic optical topologies observed in free space, provides a systematic route hopfions of any order, with implications for topological photonics, optical communications, and analogies in magnetic and condensed-matter systems.
one_liner: Tailored Laguerre-Gaussian superpositions generate free-space optical hopfions with independently tunable poloidal and toroidal winding numbers.
why_it_matters: Controllable high-order topological light fields could encode information in robust 3D structures beyond standard mode or polarization multiplexing.
detailed_summary: The authors present a construction for optical hopfions with arbitrary combinations of poloidal and toroidal winding numbers using superpositions of Laguerre-Gaussian modes in free space. They experimentally visualize the resulting topological structures through polarization filaments and demonstrate orders up to 5 for one winding number and 3 for the other, extending the accessible state space beyond prior low-order realizations. The method provides systematic and independent control over the two topological indices. This yields a tunable platform for studying and exploiting complex 3D optical topology.
main_result: The paper demonstrates experimentally accessible optical hopfions with independently tunable winding numbers, reaching higher orders than previous realizations.
application_angle: Such topological field states may serve as robust multidimensional carriers for free-space optical information encoding and advanced topological photonics experiments.

title: HEOM-in-Calibration-Loop: Exposing Non-Markovian Bath Signatures That Markovian Calibration Elides in Superconducting-Qubit Tune-Up
abstract: Closed-loop superconducting-qubit calibration has matured into DAG-orchestrated protocol chains, yet published frameworks treat the bath via a Markovian master equation or a phenomenological likelihood, absorbing bath structure into fit residuals instead of reporting it as a diagnostic. We integrate a QuTiP 5.x hierarchical-equations-of-motion (HEOM) solver driven by a Tier-1 1/f Burkard bath into a multi-protocol calibration DAG (Rabi -> {Ramsey || T1}) and benchmark it against sesolve and mesolve on a frozen platform in a pulse-level simulator (no hardware validation). The Ramsey channel carries the headline: the Markovian fit is censored by its exponential-family numerical ceiling, while HEOM recovers a physical revival envelope whose primary T2* separates from the Markovian reference by at least 13x at 95% independent-bootstrap confidence within the HEOM-feasible budget; the point-estimate ratio reaches >=28x on the 50-point primary-t1 grid and ~72x on the 30-point biexp-family tau_aw pivot at L=5. Rabi contrast falls 2.17% below mesolve on a noise-limited 30-point grid; the paired-bootstrap CI crosses zero, so this channel corroborates rather than independently establishes the non-Markovian signature. T1 decay shape matches across backends (beta=1.000), yet HEOM's initial occupation drops from 1.000 to 0.879 -- a bath-dressed contamination stable under a 16-point densification. The DAG adds 9.62 us average per-protocol scheduling overhead, no meaningful latency penalty at protocol granularity. HEOM-in-loop thereby changes what calibration reports: bath structure appears as a quantifiable residual rather than a hidden confound.
one_liner: Embedding HEOM into superconducting-qubit calibration exposes non-Markovian bath signatures hidden by standard Markovian tune-up models.
why_it_matters: Calibration that reports bath structure instead of absorbing it into fit error can improve diagnosis, model fidelity, and control strategies for superconducting hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper integrates a QuTiP 5.x HEOM solver with a pulse-level calibration DAG spanning Rabi, Ramsey, and T1 protocols, using a Tier-1 1/f Burkard bath model. In simulation, the Ramsey channel is the clearest discriminator: HEOM recovers revival structure and a primary T2* at least 13 times larger than the Markovian reference at 95% bootstrap confidence within the tested computational budget. T1 decay shapes remain matched between models, but HEOM predicts a reduced initial excited-state occupation of 0.879, consistent with bath dressing rather than simple exponential decay. The workflow adds only 9.62 us average scheduling overhead per protocol, so richer bath inference comes with little protocol-level latency penalty.
main_result: HEOM-in-the-loop calibration reveals strong non-Markovian signatures in Ramsey and state-preparation behavior that standard Markovian fits suppress or misattribute.
application_angle: This approach can turn routine tune-up into a noise-spectroscopy diagnostic, informing better pulse calibration and hardware troubleshooting for superconducting processors.

title: Dynamical Regimes of Two Qubits Coupled through a Transmission Line
abstract: We investigate the reduced dynamics of two identical superconducting qubits capacitively coupled through a finite-length transmission line. Starting from circuit quantization, we derive a circuit Hamiltonian that naturally separates the line modes into even- and odd-parity sectors coupled to collective qubit operators. Depending on the hierarchy between the qubit frequency $ω_q$, the mode spacing $ω_{TL}$, and the coupling scale $ω_g$, the line acts either as a structured reservoir or as a discrete few-mode coupler. In the long-line continuum limit, each sector is described by a Drude--Lorentz spectral density and the dynamics is solved with the hierarchical equations of motion. Using the Breuer--Laine--Piilo measure, we identify the parameter region in which the reduced dynamics exhibits non-Markovian relaxation. In the short-line limit, the continuum description breaks down and the dynamics becomes respectively multimode or single-mode. This establishes a unified cQED picture of the dynamical regimes of finite-length transmission lines in superconducting-circuit architectures.
one_liner: A unified cQED analysis maps how two superconducting qubits coupled by a finite transmission line move between reservoir-like, multimode, and cavity-like regimes.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when a transmission line behaves as a dissipative environment versus a useful coherent bus, directly informing superconducting interconnect design.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a circuit-quantized Hamiltonian for two identical superconducting qubits capacitively coupled through a finite-length transmission line, with even- and odd-parity line modes coupling to collective qubit operators. In the long-line limit, each parity sector is mapped to a Drude-Lorentz spectral density and the reduced dynamics is solved using hierarchical equations of motion. The authors use the Breuer-Laine-Piilo measure to delineate where relaxation becomes non-Markovian, and show that shortening the line drives a crossover to discrete multimode and ultimately single-mode dynamics. This yields a single framework spanning structured-bath and few-mode coupler descriptions.
platform_or_system: two superconducting qubits and a finite transmission line
main_result: The study identifies parameter regimes where a finite transmission line mediates Markovian, non-Markovian, multimode, or single-mode qubit dynamics.
application_angle: The regime map can guide engineered superconducting links for coherent remote coupling, quantum buses, and noise-aware modular circuit layouts.

title: Gaussian pulse scattering by a chiral spherical shell
abstract: Theory was formulated for scattering by a coated chiral sphere of a plane wave of arbitrary polarization state with amplitude modulated by a Gaussian pulse. The spherical core and the concentric shell of the sphere were composed of two different homogeneous materials, both isotropic chiral. Calculations of energy efficiencies for extinction, total scattering, and absorption were carried out for the shell material with experimentally determined constitutive parameters, the core being vacuous. All three energy efficiencies depend on the relative thickness of the shell and the circular polarization state of the carrier plane wave.
one_liner: This work develops a time-domain scattering theory for Gaussian-pulse illumination of a coated chiral sphere and quantifies polarization- and thickness-dependent energy efficiencies.
why_it_matters: It gives actionable modeling tools for chiral photonic scatterers used in polarization control, sensing, and pulse-based optical components.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate scattering by a concentric coated chiral sphere driven by an arbitrarily polarized plane wave with Gaussian temporal modulation. Both shell and core materials are taken as isotropic chiral media, with calculations performed using experimentally determined constitutive parameters for the shell and a vacuous core. They compute extinction, total scattering, and absorption energy efficiencies and show that all three depend strongly on shell thickness and on the carrier wave's circular polarization state. The analysis extends standard chiral-sphere treatments from monochromatic excitation to pulsed excitation relevant for realistic optical systems.
main_result: The paper shows that pulse-energy extinction, scattering, and absorption in a chiral spherical shell are tunable via shell thickness and input circular polarization.
application_angle: These results are relevant for designing compact chiral photonic elements for polarization-selective filtering, pulse shaping, and optical sensing.

title: LightStim: A Framework for QEC Protocol Evaluation and Prototyping with Automated DEM Construction
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum computing increasingly demands rigorous, circuit-level evaluation of diverse quantum error correction (QEC) protocols and efficient prototyping of new ones. Such evaluation requires both the physical circuit and its Detector Error Model (DEM) to simulate end-to-end logical error rates. However, DEM construction today is performed by manual annotation, a tedious and error-prone process that effectively limits evaluation to simple memory experiments. We present LightStim, a framework that automates DEM construction concurrently with circuit compilation by maintaining a Pauli tableau augmented with measurement records, with no protocol-specific input required. We benchmark LightStim across protocols from memory experiments to end-to-end distillation circuits; cross-validation against public implementations confirms exact detector and observable counts and consistent logical error rates. LightStim additionally accelerates the exploration of new protocols, which we demonstrate through a novel heterogeneous cross-code lattice surgery design between surface and punctured quantum Reed-Muller codes. These capabilities together make LightStim a unified infrastructure for systematic QEC protocol evaluation and exploration.
one_liner: LightStim automates detector error model generation during QEC circuit compilation, enabling faster and less error-prone evaluation of complex fault-tolerant protocols.
why_it_matters: Automated DEM construction removes a major bottleneck in realistic QEC benchmarking and accelerates iteration on new hardware-relevant protocols.
detailed_summary: LightStim constructs detector error models concurrently with circuit compilation by tracking a Pauli tableau augmented with measurement records, eliminating protocol-specific manual annotation. The framework is benchmarked on protocols ranging from memory experiments to distillation circuits, with cross-validation against public implementations showing exact agreement in detector and observable counts and consistent logical error rates. It also supports rapid exploration of new schemes, illustrated with a heterogeneous lattice-surgery protocol linking surface codes and punctured quantum Reed-Muller codes. The contribution is primarily infrastructural but directly impacts hardware-facing QEC design workflows.
platform_or_system: fault-tolerant quantum circuits with automated DEM compilation
main_result: The framework reproduces validated DEMs and logical error rates across diverse QEC protocols while enabling automated prototyping of new fault-tolerant constructions.
application_angle: It can shorten the path from hardware noise models to end-to-end logical performance estimates for quantum processor architects and QEC compiler developers.

title: Suppressing the Erasure Error of Fusion Operation in Photonic Quantum Computing
abstract: Photonic quantum computing provides a promising route toward quantum computation by naturally supporting the measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) model. In MBQC, programs are executed through measurements on a pre-generated graph state, whose construction largely depends on probabilistic fusion operations. However, fusion operations in PQC are vulnerable to two major error sources: fusion failure and fusion erasure. As a result, MBQC compilation must account for both error mechanisms to generate reliable and efficient photonic executions. Prior state-of-the-art MBQC compilation, represented by OneAdapt, is designed for all-photonic architectures and mainly focuses on handling fusion failures. Nevertheless, it does not explicitly model fusion erasures induced by photon loss, which can be substantially more damaging than fusion failures. To mitigate fusion erasure errors, we introduce a new MBQC compilation scheme built upon the spin qubit quantum memory. We propose tree-encoded fusion, an encoding strategy that suppresses erasure errors during graph-state generation. We further incorporate this scheme into a compiler framework with algorithms that reduce the execution overhead of quantum programs. We evaluate the proposed framework using a realistic PQC simulator on six representative quantum algorithm benchmarks across multiple program scales. The results show that tree-encoded fusion achieves better robustness than alternative fusion-encoding strategies, and that our compiler provides exponential improvement over OneAdapt. In addition, we validate the feasibility of our approach through a proof-of-concept demonstration on real PQC hardware.
one_liner: A photonic MBQC compiler with tree-encoded fusion and spin-qubit memory suppresses fusion erasure errors and cuts execution overhead versus prior all-photonic methods.
why_it_matters: Photon loss is a central obstacle in photonic quantum computing, and this work targets it with a compilation strategy that appears both hardware-aware and experimentally grounded.
detailed_summary: The paper addresses photonic graph-state generation when fusion operations suffer both probabilistic failure and erasure from photon loss, emphasizing that erasures are especially damaging for MBQC execution. It introduces tree-encoded fusion built around spin-qubit quantum memory, and integrates this encoding into a compiler that reduces program overhead while explicitly mitigating fusion erasures. Simulations on six benchmark algorithms across multiple scales show improved robustness over alternative encodings and exponential improvement over OneAdapt, and the authors additionally report a proof-of-concept test on real photonic hardware. The work combines architecture, compilation, and experimental validation in a practical loss-tolerance stack.
platform_or_system: photonic MBQC with spin-qubit quantum memory
main_result: Tree-encoded fusion plus the proposed compiler substantially suppresses erasure-induced failures and outperforms prior all-photonic compilation baselines in realistic simulations.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to scalable photonic processors and photonic-networked architectures where loss-tolerant fusion operations are essential.

title: Pulse Shaping for Superconducting Qubits
abstract: High-fidelity control of superconducting qubits requires carefully shaped microwave pulses that account for multiple error channels. In this work, we present a pedagogical introduction to pulse-shaping techniques for transmon qubits, aiming to provide a unified, accessible framework that integrates physical intuition for pulse design, analytical understanding of gate-level descriptions, and practical considerations of hardware. This article further aims to serve as a guide for students and early researchers entering superconducting quantum computing. We begin by examining simple pulse envelopes and their spectral properties, highlighting how finite bandwidth leads to leakage outside the computational subspace. These observations motivate the introduction of the derivative removal by adiabatic gate (DRAG) technique, which uses a quadrature component proportional to the pulse's time derivative to suppress off-resonant excitations. We analyze the single-qubit case using the Magnus expansion, which provides a clear understanding of the order-by-order introduction of error channels. We discuss the practical hardware realities of control pulse generation, focusing on arbitrary waveform generators (AWG), local oscillators (LO), and IQ mixing. Common imperfections are discussed in terms of their impact on the effective pulse shape and qubit Hamiltonian. Finally, we extend the discussion to two-qubit operations, focusing on the cross-resonance gate and the emergence of effective interactions.
one_liner: A technically grounded tutorial unifies pulse-envelope design, DRAG control, hardware nonidealities, and cross-resonance gates for superconducting qubits.
why_it_matters: Reliable pulse engineering is a practical bottleneck for improving gate fidelity on superconducting hardware, and this article consolidates the core control toolbox.
detailed_summary: The article reviews how finite-bandwidth microwave control pulses induce leakage and other gate errors in transmon qubits, then develops pulse-shaping intuition from simple envelopes to DRAG corrections. It uses the Magnus expansion to connect pulse design choices to effective Hamiltonian error terms order by order, giving a compact analytical picture of single-qubit control. The discussion also covers AWGs, local oscillators, and IQ mixers, highlighting how hardware imperfections distort implemented pulses, before extending to two-qubit control through the cross-resonance gate. While pedagogical, it is focused on experimentally relevant mechanisms and mitigation strategies.
main_result: The paper synthesizes analytical and hardware-level pulse-shaping methods that suppress leakage and control errors in single- and two-qubit superconducting gates.
application_angle: It is useful for calibrating and improving control stacks in real superconducting processors where waveform distortions and leakage limit usable fidelity.

title: Hybridization of Kerr Solitons in Coupled Microresonators
abstract: Recent advances in manufacturing photonic integrated devices enable efficient coupling between high-Q microresonators in both linear and nonlinear regimes, creating a tunable, complex, hybridized optical system. Considering two coupled microresonators with normal and anomalous dispersion and equal free spectral range (FSR), we theoretically predict a novel nonlinear phenomenon: fully coherent hybridization of dissipative Kerr solitons (DKS) and propose a realistic integrated photonic design for its experimental observation. Using the Lugiato-Lefever equations in the supermode basis, we show that the emergent picture of inter-resonator DKS interactions can be understood as the formation of coherent structures in both supermodes generated by an unusual four-wave mixing process. The found hybridized DKS states can exhibit a broad, flat spectral profile near the pumped mode and remarkable oscillatory features in the spectral wings, promising broad applications in the generation and control of optical Kerr frequency combs.
one_liner: Coupled microresonators with opposite dispersion are predicted to support fully coherent hybridized Kerr solitons with distinctive flat-top and oscillatory comb spectra.
why_it_matters: Hybridized soliton states could expand on-chip frequency-comb engineering by adding new degrees of freedom for bandwidth and spectral-shape control.
detailed_summary: The work studies two linearly coupled high-Q microresonators with matched free spectral range but normal and anomalous dispersion, analyzed through coupled Lugiato-Lefever equations in the supermode basis. It predicts a new regime in which dissipative Kerr solitons hybridize coherently across both supermodes through an unusual four-wave-mixing process. The resulting states show broad, flat spectra near the pump together with oscillatory spectral wings, and the authors outline an integrated-photonics design intended to make the effect experimentally accessible. The result connects resonator coupling engineering to nonlinear comb-state synthesis.
main_result: The paper predicts and characterizes coherent hybridized dissipative Kerr solitons spanning coupled-resonator supermodes with distinctive frequency-comb signatures.
application_angle: Such states could enable more versatile chip-scale comb sources for metrology, communications, and programmable nonlinear photonic systems.

title: Speed-oriented quantum circuit backend
abstract: We present a new software package for efficient quantum circuit generation, designed to achieve optimal runtime performance. Despite being in an early stage of development, our implementation demonstrates significant advantages over existing tools. Using the quantum Fourier transform (QFT) as a benchmark, we show that our backend can generate circuits for systems with up to 2000 qubits faster than widely used frameworks such as Qiskit and Q#. This improvement is particularly relevant for applications where classical preprocessing time, including circuit generation, must be minimized to not diminish any potential quantum advantage - for example, in combinatorial optimization tasks. Additionally, our software provides high-level primitives for bit- and integer-level manipulations, offering a simplified interface for integration with high-level quantum programming languages.
one_liner: A high-speed quantum circuit backend generates very large circuits, including 2000-qubit QFTs, faster than common frameworks.
why_it_matters: Faster classical circuit generation reduces orchestration overhead that can erase practical quantum advantage in near-term workflows.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a software backend focused on minimizing circuit-construction runtime rather than adding new quantum algorithms. Using QFT as a benchmark, the authors report faster circuit generation than Qiskit and Q# for problem sizes up to 2000 qubits. The package also exposes higher-level bitwise and integer-level primitives intended for integration with higher-level quantum programming languages. The emphasis is on reducing classical preprocessing cost in practical quantum application pipelines.
main_result: The backend generates benchmark circuits such as large-QFT instances substantially faster than widely used quantum software frameworks.
application_angle: It can shorten compile-time bottlenecks in optimization and other workflows where classical circuit synthesis latency matters operationally.

title: Spectral Diffusion Mitigation with a Laser Pulse Sequence
abstract: The optical spectrum of a quantum system is jointly determined by the properties of the emitter and the driving field. All-optical spectral control can hence be a promising method to engineer the properties of single photon emitters for quantum technological applications. It was proposed that driving a two-level system with a periodic sequence of optical pi-pulses during the excited state lifetime shifts the emission and absorption maximum to an arbitrarily detuned pulse carrier frequency, enabling the mitigation of spectral diffusion in noisy emitters. In this article, we report on the first experimental observation of this effect. We implement the protocol on a solid-state emitter and reduce its inhomogeneously broadened optical linewidth close to the lifetime limit. By detuning the excitation laser, we are able to concentrate approximately half of the absorption to a freely selectable target frequency. Our approach is solely based on properties of coherently evolving quantum systems, rendering it applicable to a wide range of individual and ensembles of quantum emitters.
one_liner: A laser pi-pulse sequence suppresses spectral diffusion and steers emitter absorption toward a chosen optical frequency.
why_it_matters: This offers an all-optical route to make noisy solid-state single-photon emitters more indistinguishable and easier to interface in quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally demonstrate a proposed protocol in which periodic optical pi-pulses applied during the excited-state lifetime reshape the emission and absorption spectrum of a two-level emitter. On a solid-state emitter, they reduce the inhomogeneously broadened optical linewidth close to the lifetime limit. By detuning the excitation laser, they concentrate about half of the absorption into a freely selectable target frequency. The mechanism is purely coherent and does not rely on material-specific tuning knobs.
main_result: The experiment narrows the effective optical response close to the lifetime limit and redirects roughly half of the absorption to a selectable frequency.
application_angle: The method could improve photon indistinguishability and spectral matching for emitter-based quantum photonics without complex device-specific stabilization.

title: Bipartite entanglement under frequency comb pumping in parametric Josephson circuits
abstract: The creation of high-quality cluster states in superconducting microwave circuits is a relevant ingredient in continuous-variable quantum computing. Although large-scale cluster states have been established in optical systems, dissipation prevents their direct applicability to the microwave realm. Recent improvements in superconducting parametric circuits, in particular Josephson parametric amplifiers (JPA) and traveling wave parametric amplifiers (TWPA), have permitted substantial progress in producing entangled states using microwave photons. In this paper, we examine experimentally and theoretically the effects of numerous parametric pump tones on the degree of two-mode squeezing in a quantum circuit and apply it to the JPA. We find that additional pumps diminish the initial two-mode correlations achieved with a single pump by redistributing it among a larger network of modes and by introducing entanglement with additional idler frequencies. Taking into account the actual heterodyne measurement conditions, the experimental results are consistent with theoretical expectations.
one_liner: Frequency-comb pumping in a Josephson parametric amplifier redistributes entanglement across modes and weakens pairwise squeezing.
why_it_matters: It clarifies how multi-tone pumping trades targeted two-mode entanglement for broader multimode correlations in microwave photonic hardware.
detailed_summary: This work studies experimentally and theoretically how multiple parametric pump tones affect bipartite entanglement in superconducting microwave circuits, with application to a JPA. The authors find that adding pumps degrades the initial two-mode squeezing obtained with a single pump because correlations are redistributed over a larger mode network and shared with additional idler frequencies. Their analysis includes realistic heterodyne detection conditions, and the measurements agree with theory under those constraints. The results are directly relevant to engineering microwave continuous-variable resources in Josephson platforms.
main_result: Additional pump tones reduce pairwise two-mode correlations by spreading entanglement across multiple microwave modes and idlers.
application_angle: The study informs pump-design tradeoffs for superconducting microwave sources used in continuous-variable quantum computing and networking.

title: Testing Spontaneous Collapse Models with Coulomb Mediated Squeezing
abstract: We show that detecting steady-state Coulomb-mediated reduction in the thermal variance of the differential motional mode of two nanospheres can bound the Continuous Spontaneous Localization (CSL) parameter ($λ_{\text{CSL}}$). For realistic experimental parameters, the resulting bounds are comparable to those obtained from X-ray emission experiments and surpass those set by bulk-heating ones. Unlike these latter experiments, our bounds are robust against plausible coloured-noise extensions of collapse models. In the short-time regime, we find that a weak Coulomb-induced entanglement-based test between two charged nanospheres initialized in ground state can provide constraints on $λ_{\text{CSL}}$ comparable to limits set by early X-ray experiments.
one_liner: Coulomb-coupled charged nanospheres could test CSL collapse models through steady-state squeezing and short-time entanglement.
why_it_matters: It proposes an experimentally grounded sensing route to bound collapse-model parameters with robustness beyond some existing heating-based tests.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes two charged nanospheres whose Coulomb interaction reduces the thermal variance of their differential motional mode in steady state. Detecting this squeezing can place bounds on the CSL rate parameter comparable to X-ray-emission limits and stronger than bulk-heating constraints for realistic parameters. In a short-time protocol, weak Coulomb-mediated entanglement between ground-state nanospheres also yields competitive CSL bounds. The authors further argue that these bounds remain robust under plausible colored-noise extensions of collapse models.
main_result: Observable motional squeezing and transient entanglement in two charged nanospheres can constrain CSL parameters at or beyond several existing experimental bounds.
application_angle: It turns a mesoscopic optomechanical platform into a precision probe of fundamental-noise models using measurements accessible to quantum sensing experiments.

title: Near-Term Reduction in Nonlocal Gate Count from Distributed Logical Qubits
abstract: Modular quantum computing architectures require error correction schemes that remain effective in the presence of noisy inter-processor operations. As such, minimizing the number of such operations on logical circuits partitioned across quantum processors is a primary objective of distributed quantum computing. In this work, we develop basic techniques for qubit allocation using an exemplar color code family and explore generalizations to other color codes. In particular, we show that a 10% reduction in processor-nonlocal gates is achievable in a setting where syndrome extraction occurs after every logical gate, as in today's devices, and that this scales to significantly greater advantages in the multi-qubit case. We also explore methods of achieving universal gate sets efficiently in this distributed logical setting and evaluate the trade-offs of multiple approaches such as magic state distillation, code switching, and a new method based on logical swaps. Finally, we discuss some considerations for an allocation algorithm for these architectures to perform scalably and connect it to existing work on quantum circuit partitions.
one_liner: Distributed logical-qubit allocation in color-code architectures cuts nonlocal logical gates by about 10% in near-term settings.
why_it_matters: Reducing inter-processor logical operations directly lowers one of the main error and latency costs in modular fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors study qubit allocation for modular architectures using an exemplar family of color codes and extensions to related codes. In a regime where syndrome extraction follows every logical gate, they show an approximately 10% reduction in processor-nonlocal gates, with larger gains expected for multi-qubit cases. They also compare ways to implement universal logic efficiently in this distributed setting, including magic-state distillation, code switching, and logical-swap-based methods. The paper links these coding choices to scalable allocation algorithms and circuit partitioning strategies.
platform_or_system: distributed logical qubits in color-code modules
main_result: A distributed logical-qubit placement strategy reduces inter-processor logical gate count by roughly 10% in near-term modular error-corrected operation.
application_angle: The techniques can improve modular quantum processor throughput and fidelity by lowering the burden of noisy nonlocal logical operations.

title: Quantum-information diagnostics of cosmological perturbations with nontrivial sound speed in inflation
abstract: In this work, we systematically investigate the quantum-information diagnostics of cosmological perturbations with a nontrivial sound speed, utilizing a normalized open two-mode squeezed-state framework. Rather than introducing new observables, our analysis focuses on how a modified sound speed dynamically reshapes the Schrödinger evolution of the squeezing parameters ($r_k$ and $φ_k$). We demonstrate how these dynamical changes are inherited by the reduced density matrix of the observable sector. By employing a sound-speed-resonance parametrization, we derive and evaluate the purity, von Neumann entropy, Rényi entropies, and logarithmic negativity. To overcome the intrinsic multiscale stiffness of the post-inflationary equations, we introduce a bounded variable $x = \tanh r_k$ as a partial regularization, which enables reliable numerical simulations exclusively within the inflationary regime. Our numerical results reveal that a nontrivial sound speed significantly suppresses the purity of the reduced state, indicating enhanced effective mixedness. Simultaneously, it strongly amplifies and modulates both the entropic and entanglement diagnostics. More precisely, a nontrivial sound speed postpones the onset of classicality by modulating the decoherence process. Ultimately, we show that a nontrivial sound speed leaves distinct and identifiable quantum-information signatures within the entanglement structure of the early universe.
one_liner: Nontrivial inflationary sound speed leaves strong signatures in purity, entropy, and entanglement of two-mode squeezed cosmological states.
why_it_matters: It maps how a physically relevant model parameter changes quantum-information observables, sharpening connections between squeezing theory and cosmological quantum diagnostics.
detailed_summary: Using a normalized open two-mode squeezed-state framework, the paper studies how a nontrivial sound speed modifies the Schrödinger evolution of squeezing parameters and propagates into reduced-state observables. The authors compute purity, von Neumann entropy, Renyi entropies, and logarithmic negativity under a sound-speed-resonance parametrization. They introduce the bounded variable x=tanh(r_k) to regularize stiff numerics and obtain stable simulations within the inflationary regime. The results show suppressed purity, amplified and modulated entropic and entanglement diagnostics, and a delayed onset of classicality.
main_result: A nontrivial sound speed strongly alters squeezing-driven quantum-information diagnostics, enhancing mixedness and entanglement structure while delaying classicalization.
application_angle: While fundamental rather than device-oriented, the analysis extends squeezed-state diagnostics used in quantum optics to a broader modeling context with transferable theoretical tools.

title: Photon Sorting with a Quantum Emitter
abstract: High-quality photonic Bell state measurements (BSMs) enable scalable universal quantum computing and long distance quantum communication. However, when implemented with linear optics, BSMs are fundamentally probabilistic, introducing substantial hardware overheads and limiting noise tolerance in photonic quantum computing architectures. Nonlinear interactions at the single-photon level can overcome these limitations by enabling near-deterministic photon-photon gates. Here, we demonstrate a passive photon-sorting circuit based on the induced nonlinearity arising from photon scattering in a solid-state quantum emitter. The scattering is implemented in a directional waveguide-emitter coupling interface and embedded on-chip into a linear optical circuit, through which we demonstrate sorting of one- and two-photon components with a success probability of 62%. We find that the current system can enable BSMs with a 57% post-selected success probability without ancillary photons, exceeding the linear-optical limit of 50%, and can be readily improved to >65% with design optimisations.
one_liner: An on-chip emitter-waveguide nonlinearity passively sorts one- and two-photon components beyond linear-optics limits.
why_it_matters: Single-photon nonlinearities that improve Bell-state measurements can cut overhead in photonic computing and quantum repeater links.
detailed_summary: The work uses photon scattering from a solid-state quantum emitter coupled to a directional waveguide to realize a passive nonlinear photon-sorting element embedded in an on-chip linear optical circuit. The device distinguishes one- and two-photon components with 62% success probability, directly exploiting emitter-induced optical nonlinearity at the single-photon level. Based on the measured performance, the authors estimate a 57% post-selected Bell-state measurement success rate without ancillas, already above the 50% linear-optical ceiling, with a path to exceed 65% via design improvements.
platform_or_system: solid-state emitter in directional waveguide photonic circuit
main_result: The experiment demonstrates passive sorting of one- versus two-photon components on chip and projects Bell-state measurements above the linear-optics 50% limit.
application_angle: Improved Bell-state measurements are directly relevant to scalable photonic quantum computing, entanglement swapping, and long-distance quantum communication.

title: Rigorous Security Proofs for Practical Quantum Key Distribution
abstract: This thesis is concerned with rigorous security analyses of practical Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols, using a variety of modern proof techniques. The main results are as follows. First, we establish a security proof for variable-length QKD protocols against IID collective attacks, and extend this result to coherent attacks using the postselection technique. In doing so, we resolve a long-standing flaw in the application of the postselection technique to QKD, thereby placing it on a rigorous mathematical footing. Second, we develop a method to bound phase error rates in entropic uncertainty relation-based and phase error rate-based proofs, using only the observed statistics of the protocol, even when detectors are imperfect and only approximately characterized. This removes a key assumption of identical detector behaviour and enables these techniques to be applied in realistic settings. Third, we present a very general security analysis based on the marginal-constrained entropy accumulation theorem. The resulting framework can be readily adapted to practical imperfections and side channels, and is suitable for certification efforts. Finally, we show that the security of QKD protocols under realistic authentication assumptions can be reduced to the standard idealized setting, where authentication is assumed to behave honestly, with only minor protocol modifications. A distinctive feature of this thesis is its unified presentation of several major QKD security proof frameworks using consistent protocol descriptions and notation. Consequently, this thesis is intended not only as a collection of new technical results, but also as a useful reference for understanding rigorous security analysis in quantum key distribution.
one_liner: A unified thesis delivers rigorous security proofs for practical QKD under realistic detectors, authentication, and side-channel assumptions.
why_it_matters: It closes proof gaps and extends composable QKD security tools to more realistic deployed-system conditions.
detailed_summary: The thesis establishes security for variable-length QKD against IID collective attacks and extends it to coherent attacks using a corrected application of the postselection technique. It also develops phase-error bounding methods that rely only on observed statistics, avoiding the usual identical-detector assumption and accommodating approximately characterized detectors. A general framework based on marginal-constrained entropy accumulation is presented for practical imperfections and side channels, along with a reduction showing realistic authentication can be handled with minor protocol modifications.
main_result: The main result is a rigorous, unified set of composable proof techniques that make practical QKD security claims mathematically robust under realistic implementation assumptions.
application_angle: These methods support certification-grade security analysis for fielded QKD systems with detector imperfections, side channels, and non-ideal authentication.

title: Deterministic generation of grid states with programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits
abstract: Bosonic quantum error correction enables hardware-efficient protection of quantum information by encoding logical qubits in harmonic oscillators. Bosonic grid states, such as Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states, are particularly promising due to their potential to correct small displacements and boson loss. However, their generation remains challenging, typically relying on probabilistic protocols or auxiliary qubit systems. Here, we propose deterministic protocols for generating bosonic grid states using programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits composed solely of squeezing, displacement, and Kerr operations. We show that aiming to enforce GKP symmetries in the output of these circuits yields states with competitive performance with respect to current realizations, but whose quality saturates with increasing circuit depth due to imperfect symmetry restoration. Instead, we find that these bosonic circuits naturally give rise to a distinct class of states, that we label as phased-comb states, which are unitarily related to standard grid states but feature an intrinsic phase structure. We demonstrate that these states define a scalable bosonic quantum error-correcting code with near-optimal performance under boson loss comparable to that of approximate GKP states. We further analyze their logical operations and show how to implement a universal gate set for them. Our results establish programmable nonlinear bosonic circuits as a viable route towards the generation of scalable bosonic quantum error-correcting states beyond standard GKP encodings.
one_liner: Programmable bosonic circuits with squeezing, displacement, and Kerr gates can deterministically generate grid-like error-correcting states.
why_it_matters: Deterministic bosonic-code state preparation could remove a major bottleneck in hardware-efficient fault-tolerant quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes deterministic generation of bosonic grid states using nonlinear bosonic circuits composed only of squeezing, displacement, and Kerr operations, without auxiliary qubits or probabilistic heralding. It finds that directly targeting standard GKP symmetries leads to quality saturation with circuit depth, but the same circuits naturally produce phased-comb states with an intrinsic phase pattern. These phased-comb states are unitarily related to conventional grid states, support near-optimal boson-loss protection comparable to approximate GKP states, and admit a universal logical gate set.
main_result: The authors identify and characterize deterministically generated phased-comb bosonic code states that achieve scalable error correction performance comparable to approximate GKP encodings.
application_angle: The approach offers a hardware-native route to bosonic logical-state preparation for continuous-variable quantum processors and photonic or superconducting oscillator platforms.

title: High-performance cellular automaton decoders for quantum repetition and toric code
abstract: Execution of quantum algorithms on large-scale quantum computers will require extremely low logical error rates, which necessitates the development of scalable decoding architectures. Local decoders are promising candidates for this task, as they avoid the communication and data processing bottlenecks inherent in global decoding strategies. Cellular automaton (CA) decoders represent a distinct class of local decoders, offering a path toward the low-latency, real-time decoding required for practical applications. In this work, we present SCALA (Signaling CA with Local Attraction), a novel non-hierarchical cellular automaton decoder for quantum repetition and toric codes. By evaluating SCALA alongside the hierarchical CA decoder proposed by Harrington, we provide a direct comparison between non-hierarchical and renormalization-group-style local decoding strategies. We characterize SCALA across three key metrics: Performance, scalability, and robustness. Our results show that SCALA achieves a code-capacity threshold of approximately $p_c\approx 7.5\%$ and provides strong sub-threshold scaling of about $p_L\propto p^{d/4}$ on the toric code. In terms of scalability, our non-hierarchical design ensures that the local computational resources remain independent of system size, yielding a modular local architecture suitable for hardware implementation. Finally, SCALA demonstrates strong robustness to qubit measurement errors and noise within the decoder itself, a critical advantage for real-time decoding on noisy hardware. Our results establish SCALA as a high-performance, scalable, and robust local decoder for scalable quantum error correction.
one_liner: SCALA is a local cellular-automaton decoder with strong thresholds, modular scaling, and robustness to noisy syndrome extraction.
why_it_matters: Fast local decoding is essential for real-time fault tolerance when centralized decoders become a latency and bandwidth bottleneck.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces SCALA, a non-hierarchical signaling cellular automaton decoder for repetition and toric codes, and benchmarks it against Harrington's hierarchical CA approach. Under code-capacity noise on the toric code, SCALA reaches an approximate threshold of 7.5% and shows sub-threshold scaling around p_L proportional to p^(d/4). Its strictly local architecture keeps computational resources independent of system size and remains robust to both measurement faults and noise inside the decoder, which is important for embedded real-time control hardware.
platform_or_system: local decoder hardware for repetition and toric codes
main_result: SCALA achieves high-performance local decoding with about a 7.5% toric-code threshold while preserving modular constant-local-resource scaling.
application_angle: Its locality and robustness make it attractive for on-controller QEC pipelines in future quantum processors that cannot afford global decode latency.

title: Enhancing Coherence of Spin Centers in p-n Diodes via Optimization Algorithms
abstract: Solid-state spin defects hold great promise as building blocks for various quantum technologies. Embedding spin centers in $p$-$n$ diodes under reverse bias has proved to be a powerful strategy to narrow the optical linewidth and increase spin coherence, while also enabling control of the photoluminescence wavelength via Stark shift. Given the multitude of parameters influencing spin centers in diodes (e.g., doping densities and profiles, temperature, bias voltage, spin center position), a question that has not yet been answered is: which set of these design parameters maximizes spin center coherence? In this work, we address this question by developing a scaled gradient descent optimization algorithm that minimizes the optical linewidth of spin centers by combining the numerical solution of a diode's Poisson equation with calculated charge noise from the non-depleted regions. Our optimization is performed for both single- and multiple-parameter cases for divacancies in SiC $p$-$i$-$n$ diodes, including reverse-bias voltage, doping density and profile, and diode total length. Importantly, the optimization is subject to realistic physical constraints, such as small operating bias voltages, avoidance of the dielectric breakdown regime and physical thresholds for doping density. Additionally, due to the leakage current at reverse bias voltages, we develop a new formalism to investigate its influence on coherence. We show that the corresponding noise can be mitigated by implanting spin defects away from the diode's surfaces. Our work provides guidance on experimentally relevant diodes for hosting spin centers with the narrowest optical linewidths and longest coherence times.
one_liner: Optimization of SiC p-i-n diode design predicts narrower optical lines and longer coherence for embedded spin defects.
why_it_matters: Device-level design rules for defect-in-diode structures can materially improve solid-state qubit coherence in practical hardware.
detailed_summary: The work combines Poisson-equation device simulations with charge-noise modeling and scaled gradient descent to optimize diode parameters that minimize spin-center optical linewidth. It studies reverse bias, doping density and profile, total diode length, and defect placement for divacancies in SiC p-i-n structures under realistic constraints such as breakdown limits and modest operating voltages. The authors also develop a formalism for leakage-current-induced noise and show that placing defects away from surfaces mitigates its impact on coherence.
platform_or_system: SiC divacancies in reverse-biased p-i-n diodes
main_result: The study produces experimentally relevant diode-design optima that are predicted to maximize coherence and minimize optical linewidth for embedded SiC spin centers.
application_angle: These optimization rules can guide fabrication of better spin-photon interfaces, quantum memories, and solid-state node devices with improved spectral stability.

title: Loss-biased fault-tolerant quantum error correction
abstract: We investigate the limits of quantum error correction (QEC) in neutral-atom processors approaching high-fidelity gates and fast cycle times. We show that shorter QEC cycles amplify platform-specific errors, notably Rydberg excitation hopping, and hinder decay of residual Rydberg population, leading to non-Markovian correlated errors that degrade logical performance. To address this, we introduce loss biasing, where spurious Rydberg excitations are rapidly converted into atom loss via mid-circuit ionization, transforming errors into erasure-like noise and suppressing their propagation. Loss biasing restores the fault-tolerant logical error scaling for intra-cycle Pauli errors; furthermore, we argue that when supported with loss-aware decoding, it can achieve the optimal scaling of erasures while enabling shorter QEC cycles with reduced hardware overhead. We outline an implementation using fast autoionization in alkaline-earth(-like) atoms, establishing loss biasing as a practical route toward fault-tolerant quantum computing with sub-millisecond QEC cycles.
one_liner: Loss biasing in neutral-atom QEC converts harmful correlated Rydberg errors into erasure-like loss for better fault-tolerant scaling.
why_it_matters: Turning platform-specific non-Markovian errors into detectable loss could enable faster, lower-overhead neutral-atom fault tolerance.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes how shortening QEC cycles in neutral-atom processors can worsen logical performance by amplifying Rydberg excitation hopping and residual-population effects, creating correlated non-Markovian noise. To counter this, it proposes loss biasing: rapidly converting spurious Rydberg excitations into atom loss through mid-circuit ionization so the dominant faults resemble erasures rather than propagating coherent errors. The scheme restores fault-tolerant scaling for intra-cycle Pauli noise and, with loss-aware decoding, is argued to approach optimal erasure scaling while supporting sub-millisecond cycles.
platform_or_system: neutral-atom Rydberg processors with mid-circuit ionization
main_result: The main result is a practical fault-tolerance strategy that suppresses correlated Rydberg-induced errors by converting them into detectable atom loss.
application_angle: This could lower overhead and accelerate QEC cycles in neutral-atom hardware, improving the path to scalable fault-tolerant quantum computing.

title: A Universal Quantum Information Preserving Photonic Switch for Scalable Quantum Networks
abstract: Quantum networks are a keystone of the quantum internet. However, existing implementations remain largely confined to static point-to-point links due to the absence of a switching paradigm capable of dynamically routing fragile quantum entanglement without introducing decoherence. Here, we propose the Universal Quantum Switch, a foundational building block allowing on-demand, non-blocking, and encoding-agnostic routing of quantum information, as well as seamless modality conversion between disparate quantum platforms. We develop a prototype in thin-film lithium niobate and experimentally demonstrate robust switching with $\le 4\%$ decoherence via thermo-optic modulation and high-speed electro-optic switching of arbitrary entangled states at 1 MHz. Moreover, we show that our platform can support reconfiguration speeds up to 1 GHz. To our knowledge, this work represents the first demonstration of multi-node dynamic entanglement distribution at these speeds. Complementing these experimental results, we project the architecture's scalability, showing dimension-independent decoherence, and provide a scalable, interoperable building block for heterogeneous quantum network fabrics.
